# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**"Differentiate between plant location and plant layout with suitable examples from the food industry."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Plant Location** refers to the choice of the **geographic area** where the plant is to be set up. It is a strategic, macro-level decision. *Example:* Deciding whether to set up a pineapple processing unit in Vazhakulam (Ernakulam) or in Karnataka.
- **Plant Layout** refers to the **arrangement of machinery, equipment, and workstations** within the chosen site. It is a micro-level, operational decision. *Example:* Deciding whether the boiler room comes before or after the pasteurization section inside the Vazhakulam plant.
- **Key differences:** (1) Location is decided *before* layout; (2) Location is harder to reverse; (3) Location deals with external environment, layout deals with internal space utilization.
- **(3 marks — full marks awarded only if example is industry-specific.)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]`
**"List any six primary factors affecting plant location of a fruit processing industry in Kerala."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (any six of the following, each 0.5 mark):**
1. Proximity to fruit-growing clusters (e.g., pineapple in Vazhakulam, mango in Muthalamada).
2. Availability of skilled and unskilled labour.
3. Power and water supply reliability.
4. Transportation access (NH-544, rail head).
5. Government subsidies from KINFRA / KSIDC.
6. Effluent disposal / environmental clearance feasibility.
7. Land cost and soil suitability.
8. Market proximity (urban demand centres — Kochi, Calicut).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1a variant]` (14 marks)

**A bakery industry is evaluating two sites for a new unit.**
- **Site X:** Fixed cost = Rs. 50 lakh, Variable cost = Rs. 1200/tonne.
- **Site Y:** Fixed cost = Rs. 30 lakh, Variable cost = Rs. 1900/tonne.

#### (a) Determine the locational break-even output. Which site is preferred at an expected production of 5000 tonnes/year? *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Apply break-even formula:**

$$Q^{*} = \dfrac{F_X - F_Y}{v_Y - v_X} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000}{1900 - 1200}$$

$$Q^{*} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{700} = 2857.14 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with expected output:**

$$Q = 5000 > Q^{*} = 2857.14$$

Since expected production **exceeds** the break-even, the plant benefits from economies of scale — **choose Site X** (high fixed, low variable).

**Step 3 — Verify by direct cost calculation:**

$$TC_X = 50{,}00{,}000 + 1200 \times 5000 = 1{,}10{,}00{,}000$$

$$TC_Y = 30{,}00{,}000 + 1900 \times 5000 = 1{,}25{,}00{,}000$$

Savings = Rs. 15,00,000/year by choosing Site X. *(Full 7 marks with formula, computation, interpretation, and verification.)*

#### (b) What happens if production falls to 2000 tonnes/year due to demand drop? Re-evaluate and recommend with justification. *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Direct cost comparison at Q = 2000:**

$$TC_X = 50{,}00{,}000 + 1200 \times 2000 = 74{,}00{,}000$$

$$TC_Y = 30{,}00{,}000 + 1900 \times 2000 = 68{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Decision:**

$$Q = 2000 < Q^{*} = 2857.14$$

Therefore, the **fixed cost of Site X is no longer justified** — **choose Site Y**.

**Step 3 — Justification:**

When output drops below the break-even volume, the high fixed-cost option (Site X) becomes uneconomical. The plant should switch to (or relocate to) Site Y to leverage lower fixed overhead. If demand rebound is expected, a **flexible contract** on Site X (lower initial deposit) may be considered. *(Full 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 1, Q1b variant]` (14 marks)

**A dairy cooperative must select a site for a new milk processing plant from three alternatives — Sites P, Q, R — using the weighted factor method.**

#### (a) Explain the methodology of the weighted factor rating method with its steps. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Weighted Factor Rating Method** is a multi-criteria decision-making tool used when qualitative and quantitative factors must be considered together. **Steps:**

1. **Identify decision factors** relevant to the food industry (raw material, market, labour, power, transport, etc.).
2. **Assign weights** $w_j$ to each factor based on its relative importance, ensuring $\sum w_j = 1$.
3. **Rate each site** on each factor using a common scale (typically 1 = poor to 5 = excellent).
4. **Multiply** weight by rating to get the weighted score for each site-factor combination.
5. **Sum** all weighted scores per site to obtain the composite score $S_i$.
6. **Compare** composite scores and select the site with the highest $S_i$.
7. **Conduct sensitivity analysis** to verify robustness.

> *Advantages:* Simple, transparent, includes qualitative factors. *Limitations:* Subjectivity in weights and ratings; requires expert judgement. *(7 marks — full marks with all 7 steps plus limitations.)*

#### (b) Given the data below, compute composite scores and recommend the best site for the dairy plant. *(7 marks)*

| Factor | Weight | Site P | Site Q | Site R |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Milk catchment proximity | 0.30 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| Market access | 0.25 | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| Labour availability | 0.15 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Cold-chain infra | 0.20 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Power & water | 0.10 | 5 | 4 | 3 |

**Step 1 — Site P score:**

$$S_P = (0.30)(5) + (0.25)(4) + (0.15)(3) + (0.20)(4) + (0.10)(5)$$

$$S_P = 1.50 + 1.00 + 0.45 + 0.80 + 0.50 = 4.25$$

**Step 2 — Site Q score:**

$$S_Q = (0.30)(3) + (0.25)(4) + (0.15)(5) + (0.20)(3) + (0.10)(4)$$

$$S_Q = 0.90 + 1.00 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.40 = 3.65$$

**Step 3 — Site R score:**

$$S_R = (0.30)(4) + (0.25)(5) + (0.15)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.10)(3)$$

$$S_R = 1.20 + 1.25 + 0.60 + 1.00 + 0.30 = 4.35$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$S_R (4.35) > S_P (4.25) > S_Q (3.65)$$

**Recommendation: Site R is the best choice** for the dairy plant. *(7 marks — full marks with all site calculations, comparison, and recommendation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing "plant location" with "plant layout" in definitions — costs **1 mark**.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to verify that $\sum w_j = 1$ before computing scores — costs **0.5 mark**.
> - **Mistake 3:** In break-even problems, omitting the **decision rule** (which site at which Q) — costs **1 mark**.
> - **Mistake 4:** Writing only formula without substituting numerical values — costs **1–2 marks**.
> - **Mistake 5:** Not providing industry-specific examples (food industry specifically) — costs **0.5–1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Plant location is the most irreversible** strategic decision in food plant engineering.
- ✅ Always distinguish **plant location** (macro) from **plant site** (micro) from **plant layout** (internal).
- ✅ The two principal **quantitative methods** are: **(i) Locational Break-Even Analysis** and **(ii) Weighted Factor Rating Method**.
- ✅ Break-even formula: $Q^{*} = (F_A - F_B) / (v_B - v_A)$ — valid only when fixed cost of A > B and variable cost of B > A.
- ✅ Decision rule: Below $Q^{*}$ → low-fixed-cost site; Above $Q^{*}$ → low-variable-cost site.
- ✅ In weighted factor method, weights must sum to **1.00** (or 100), ratings typically on **1–5** scale.
- ✅ **Food industry-specific factors**: raw material perishability, cold-chain infrastructure, FSSAI compliance, effluent disposal, mega food park proximity.
- ✅ Always **verify break-even result** by direct cost substitution at the given $Q$.
- ✅ Always **state the decision rule** explicitly in numerical answers.
- ✅ Use the **"RAW MATERIALS MIGHT LEAP"** mnemonic for recalling 12+ location factors quickly.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 ESE, expect at least **one 14-mark question** from Module 1 covering either break-even or weighted factor method.
- ✅ KTU expects **Kerala-specific / India-specific examples** (Vazhakulam, Muthalamada, KINFRA, KSIDC, PMKSY) — not generic textbook examples.
- ✅ In sensitivity analysis, **±15% input cost variation** is the standard KTU-recommended stress test.
- ✅ Composite methods (CPM) combine weighted scoring with cost penalty — useful for 14-mark full-case questions.
- ✅ Centre-of-Gravity method is used for **multi-plant / distribution-warehouse** location, not single-plant food industry problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Location Factors - Plant Site Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT832 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plant Location Factors - Plant Site Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Location** is the strategic process of selecting a geographic region, area, or specific site for establishing a new manufacturing or food processing facility, with the objective of optimizing long-term operational efficiency, minimizing total logistics cost, and maximizing overall profitability.

**Plant Site Selection** refers to the narrower, micro-level evaluation step that follows regional screening. It involves choosing the exact plot of land within an already-approved region where the food plant will physically be constructed. Site selection considers topographical, geological, climatic, and immediate-neighborhood variables.

Together, these two decisions form the most **irreversible** and **capital-intensive** strategic choice in food process engineering — once a plant is built, relocation costs are astronomically high.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Choosing Where to Build a House"**
>
> Imagine you want to build your dream home. First, you decide the **City** (Kochi, Chennai, Bengaluru) — this is the *Plant Location* decision. It depends on where your job is, family is, and lifestyle preferences.
>
> Then, you pick the **specific street and plot** within that city — this is the *Plant Site Selection*. You check if the soil is firm (geotechnical), if the road is wide enough (transport), if the neighborhood has schools (labor/social infrastructure), and if the plot is above flood level (climate/topography).
>
> A wrong city choice wastes your commute for life; a wrong plot choice floods your house every monsoon. Both are "irreversible" mistakes — exactly like a food plant!

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — relevant for sizing once location is fixed
- **Breakeven Volume** — minimum output at which plant becomes profitable
- **Total Logistics Cost** = Inbound Freight + Outbound Freight + Inventory Carrying Cost + Material Handling Cost
- **Weighted Factor Rating Score** — dimensionless numerical ranking for site comparison
- **Payback Period** — typically **5–7 years** for food processing investments

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Plant Location Factors

The factors influencing plant location can be systematically classified into **Primary (Dominant) Factors** and **Secondary (Subordinate) Factors**.

#### 2.1.1 Primary / Dominant Factors

1. **Proximity to Raw Materials**
   - Critical for **perishable food commodities** (milk, fish, fresh fruits).
   - Reduces spoilage, cold-chain cost, and inventory holding.
   - Measured by: raw material weight-loss ratio, perishability index.
   - Example: Tomato paste plants must be located within **≤ 50 km** of tomato-growing belts to avoid fermentation losses.

2. **Proximity to Markets**
   - Governed by customer density, distribution reach, and product shelf life.
   - Finished, fragile, or perishable goods (bread, dairy, ice-cream) favor market-oriented location.

3. **Transportation & Logistics Infrastructure**
   - Availability of highways, railway sidings, ports, and airports.
   - Multi-modal connectivity reduces freight cost.

4. **Availability of Labor**
   - Skilled, semi-skilled, and unskilled workforce.
   - Wage rates, labor productivity index, and industrial relations climate.

5. **Availability of Utilities**
   - **Water** (food plants are water-intensive; typical requirement = **5–10 m³ per tonne of product**)
   - **Power** (continuous, stable supply; preferably three-phase industrial)
   - **Steam, refrigeration, compressed air**

6. **Government Policies & Fiscal Incentives**
   - Tax holidays, subsidies, SEZ benefits, land allotment concessions.

#### 2.1.2 Secondary / Subordinate Factors

7. **Climatic Conditions** — temperature, humidity, rainfall (affects HVAC load & storage)
8. **Topography & Soil Conditions** — bearing capacity, flood risk, drainage
9. **Ecological & Environmental Constraints** — effluent disposal, pollution norms (PCB consents)
10. **Community & Social Infrastructure** — housing, schools, hospitals for workforce retention
11. **Future Expansion Possibilities** — adjacent land availability
12. **Security & Political Stability**

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Numerical Problems**

| Formula | Mathematical Expression | Application | Units |
|---------|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Weighted Factor Rating** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (w_j \times r_{ij})$ | Site scoring | Dimensionless |
| **Cost-Profit-Volume Index** | $BEP = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | Location profitability | Units / Year |
| **Total Annual Location Cost** | $TAC = F + V_c + T_r + T_d$ | Comparing alternatives | ₹ / Year |
| **Centroid Method (X-Coordinate)** | $C_x = \dfrac{\sum (V_i \times X_i)}{\sum V_i}$ | Material flow center | km or m |
| **Centroid Method (Y-Coordinate)** | $C_y = \dfrac{\sum (V_i \times Y_i)}{\sum V_i}$ | Material flow center | km or m |
| **Break-Even Distance** | $D_B = \dfrac{\vert C_1 - C_2 \vert}{(R_d - R_o) \times Q}$ | Switch from supply to demand oriented | km |
| **Inventory Carrying Cost** | $ICC = \dfrac{Q}{2} \times P \times H$ | Storage at chosen location | ₹ / Year |

Where:
- $w_j$ = weight of factor $j$, $r_{ij}$ = rating of site $i$ on factor $j$
- $FC$ = Fixed Cost, $P$ = Selling Price, $VC$ = Variable Cost per unit
- $V_i$ = Volume of flow from source $i$
- $C_1, C_2$ = Fixed costs at Locations 1 & 2
- $R_d, R_o$ = Freight rates (destination vs. origin)
- $Q$ = Annual quantity shipped

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- Used in **Greenfield project feasibility studies** for dairy, bakery, brewing, and beverage plants.
- Drives decisions in **cluster-based food parks** (e.g., KSIDC Food Parks in Kerala).
- Influences **ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000** certification feasibility (since location affects food defense and traceability).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Weighted Factor Rating Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A food processing company has shortlisted **3 sites** (Site A, Site B, Site C) for a new fruit juice plant. Five factors have been identified with weights summing to **1.00**. Ratings are on a scale of **1 to 10**. Determine the most suitable site.

#### Step 1 — Construct the Factor-Rating Matrix

Let $S_i$ = Composite score of Site $i$, where $i \in \{A, B, C\}$.

Let $w_j$ = weight of factor $j$, where $j \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{5} w_j = 1$.

Let $r_{ij}$ = rating of site $i$ on factor $j$, where $r_{ij} \in [1, 10]$.

#### Step 2 — Write the Scoring Equation

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (w_j \times r_{ij})
$$

For our problem with $n = 5$:

$$
S_i = (w_1 \times r_{i1}) + (w_2 \times r_{i2}) + (w_3 \times r_{i3}) + (w_4 \times r_{i4}) + (w_5 \times r_{i5})
$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

| Factor ($j$) | Weight ($w_j$) | Site A Rating ($r_{Aj}$) | Site B Rating ($r_{Bj}$) | Site C Rating ($r_{Cj}$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Raw Material Proximity | 0.30 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| 2. Market Proximity | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| 3. Transport Infrastructure | 0.20 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| 4. Labor Availability | 0.15 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| 5. Power & Water | 0.10 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| **Sum of Weights** | **1.00** | — | — | — |

#### Step 4 — Compute Score for Site A

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.70 \\
&= 7.40
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute Score for Site B

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.30 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 1.80 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Compute Score for Site C

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.50 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.90 \\
&= 7.70
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7 — Rank the Sites

$$
S_C \, (7.70) > S_B \, (7.50) > S_A \, (7.40)
$$

**Decision:** **Site C** is the most suitable location for the fruit juice plant.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Break-Even Distance (Crossover Distance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A company must choose between Location P (raw-material-oriented) and Location Q (market-oriented).
>
> - Annual Quantity = **50,000 tonnes**
> - Freight rate from raw material source to P = **₹80/tonne-km**
> - Freight rate from Q to market = **₹120/tonne-km**
> - Additional fixed cost at Q (market-oriented) = **₹8,00,000**
>
> Find the break-even distance at which the two locations become equally economical.

#### Step 1 — Define the Break-Even Concept

Break-even distance is the point where the **total cost of raw-material-oriented plant** equals the **total cost of market-oriented plant**.

#### Step 2 — Formulate the Cost Equations

Total cost at raw-material-oriented location (P):

$$
TC_P = R_o \times Q \times D
$$

Total cost at market-oriented location (Q):

$$
TC_Q = F_Q + R_d \times Q \times D
$$

Where:
- $R_o$ = Freight rate at origin (raw material side) = ₹80/tonne-km
- $R_d$ = Freight rate at destination (market side) = ₹120/tonne-km
- $Q$ = Annual quantity = 50,000 tonnes
- $F_Q$ = Additional fixed cost at Q = ₹8,00,000
- $D$ = Distance (km)

#### Step 3 — Equate the Two Cost Equations

$$
R_o \times Q \times D = F_Q + R_d \times Q \times D
$$

#### Step 4 — Solve Algebraically

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_o \times Q \times D - R_d \times Q \times D &= F_Q \\
Q \times D \times (R_o - R_d) &= F_Q \\
D &= \dfrac{F_Q}{Q \times (R_o - R_d)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000 \times \vert 80 - 120 \vert} \\
&= \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000 \times 40} \\
&= \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 0.40 \, \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Interpret the Result

> [!WARNING]
> Since the actual market-to-source distance typically exceeds **0.40 km** by orders of magnitude, the market-oriented Location **Q** is more economical **beyond 0.40 km**. In real industrial settings, the break-even distance is computed in hundreds of kilometers — this numerical example is for pedagogical clarity.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Raw-Material vs. Market Orientation

| Criterion | Raw-Material Oriented | Market Oriented | Intermediary (Balanced) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Product nature | Weight-losing (e.g., sugar from cane) | Weight-gaining (e.g., bottling water) | Mixed |
| Perishability | High (fresh fruits, milk) | Low (cereals, packaged) | Medium |
| Transport cost share | Inbound dominates | Outbound dominates | Equal split |
| Typical industry | Cane sugar, fruit pulp, dairy | Beverages, bakery, dairy | Multi-product plants |
| Risk exposure | Seasonal supply shock | Demand fluctuation | Diversified |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plant Location Decision Flow Architecture

The following block-level flow diagram maps the complete decision hierarchy from macro-location analysis to micro-site selection, integrating all primary and secondary factors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plant Location Decision] --> B[Define Product Mix and Capacity]
    B --> C[Identify Macro Region Level Factors]
    C --> C1[Raw Material Proximity]
    C --> C2[Market Proximity]
    C --> C3[Labor Availability]
    C --> C4[Power Water Utilities]
    C --> C5[Government Policy Incentives]
    C1 --> D[Macro Region Shortlist]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Apply Weighted Factor Rating]
    E --> F[Rank Candidate Regions]
    F --> G[Select Top Region for Micro Analysis]
    G --> H[Identify Micro Site Level Factors]
    H --> H1[Topography and Soil Bearing]
    H --> H2[Climatic Conditions]
    H --> H3[Effluent Disposal Feasibility]
    H --> H4[Transport Connectivity]
    H --> H5[Future Expansion Scope]
    H1 --> I[Conduct Site Visits and Soil Tests]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    I --> J[Apply Cost Profit Volume and Centroid Methods]
    J --> K[Final Site Selection]
    K --> L[Land Acquisition and Approvals]
    L --> M[End: Plant Construction Phase]
```

### 4.2 Factor Classification Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plant Location Factors] --> PR[Primary Factors]
    P --> SE[Secondary Factors]
    PR --> PR1[Raw Material]
    PR --> PR2[Market]
    PR --> PR3[Transport]
    PR --> PR4[Labor]
    PR --> PR5[Utilities]
    PR --> PR6[Government Policy]
    SE --> SE1[Climate]
    SE --> SE2[Topography]
    SE --> SE3[Environment]
    SE --> SE4[Social Infra]
    SE --> SE5[Future Expansion]
    SE --> SE6[Security and Stability]
```

### 4.3 Orientation Logic Diagram (Raw Material vs Market)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does raw material lose weight during processing?} -->|Yes| Q2[Raw Material Oriented Plant]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Does finished product gain weight or is highly perishable?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Market Oriented Plant]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5[Balanced or Intermediate Location]
    Q2 --> R1[Locate near farms and supply zones]
    Q4 --> R2[Locate near urban consumption centers]
    Q5 --> R3[Locate near transport hubs]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six primary factors that govern the selection of a plant location for a food processing industry.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Proximity to raw materials (especially for perishable agro-produce).
2. Proximity to markets (for finished, perishable, or fragile goods).
3. Transportation and logistics infrastructure (road, rail, port, air).
4. Availability of skilled and unskilled labor at reasonable wage rates.
5. Availability and cost of utilities — water, power, steam, refrigeration.
6. Government policies, tax incentives, subsidies, and SEZ benefits.

*(Any six well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between plant location and plant site selection.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Plant Location | Plant Site Selection |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Macro-level (region/state) | Micro-level (specific plot) |
| Decision Maker | Top management / corporate board | Engineering project team + site surveyors |
| Time Horizon | Long-term strategic | Medium-term tactical |
| Factors | Raw materials, markets, labor, policy | Topography, soil, climate, neighbors |
| Reversibility | Almost irreversible | Reversible until land acquisition |
| Output | Shortlist of regions | Final approved plot of land |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Module 1**
> • Students often **memorize factor lists without relating them to a food product** → lose marks for lack of context.
> • In **numerical problems**, students forget to **normalize weights (sum = 1.00)** — leading to wrong scoring.
> • Avoid writing vague statements like *"transport is important"*. Always specify **HOW** it impacts cost (e.g., *"reduces inbound freight by ₹X/tonne"*).
> • For **break-even distance** problems, **units must be carried throughout** — examiner deducts 1–2 marks if units are inconsistent.
> • Draw at least one **diagram/flowchart** in 14-mark answers — it usually secures 1–2 easy marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **primary factors influencing plant location** for a food processing industry. Cite specific examples from the dairy, fruit processing, and bakery sectors.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company is evaluating **two sites (X and Y)** for setting up a fish processing plant. The factor weights and ratings (out of 10) are given below. Use the **Weighted Factor Rating Method** to determine the better site.

| Factor | Weight | Rating Site X | Rating Site Y |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proximity to fishing harbor | 0.35 | 9 | 7 |
| Cold storage availability | 0.25 | 8 | 9 |
| Labor skill in seafood processing | 0.15 | 6 | 8 |
| Export market connectivity | 0.15 | 7 | 9 |
| Power and water supply | 0.10 | 8 | 7 |

**Solution to (a):**
The six primary factors are:
1. **Raw material proximity** — Fish must be processed within hours of catch; hence fish plants must be located near harbors (e.g., Kochi, Mangalore).
2. **Market proximity** — Bread and bakery items have a shelf life of hours → locate near urban markets.
3. **Transport** — Cold-chain logistics are decisive for seafood exports (Cochin Airport proximity).
4. **Labor** — Specialized butchers, filleters, and quality inspectors are needed.
5. **Utilities** — Continuous refrigeration, ice, and potable water are critical.
6. **Government policy** — MPEDA, EIA clearance, and SEZ benefits (e.g., CSEZ Kakkanad).

**Solution to (b):**

Score for Site X:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X &= (0.35 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 3.15 + 2.00 + 0.90 + 1.05 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.90
\end{aligned}
$$

Score for Site Y:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Y &= (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 2.45 + 2.25 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.70 \\
&= 7.95
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S_Y \, (7.95) > S_X \, (7.90)$, **Site Y is marginally better**, primarily driven by superior cold storage and export market connectivity. However, the closeness of scores suggests a **sensitivity analysis** should be conducted before final decision.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Centroid Method** for plant location. Under what circumstances is it most applicable?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dairy company sources milk from **three collection centers** with the following monthly volumes and map coordinates. Calculate the centroid location for the proposed processing plant.

| Center | Monthly Volume (tonnes) | X (km) | Y (km) |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | 200 | 10 | 8 |
| M2 | 150 | 25 | 15 |
| M3 | 100 | 40 | 20 |

**Solution to (a):**
The **Centroid Method** (also called the *grid method* or *center-of-gravity method*) locates a facility at the point that **minimizes total weighted transportation distance**. It assumes:
- Straight-line distances
- Constant freight rates
- Homogeneous product

Formulas:

$$
C_x = \dfrac{\sum (V_i \times X_i)}{\sum V_i}
\quad ; \quad
C_y = \dfrac{\sum (V_i \times Y_i)}{\sum V_i}
$$

**Applicability:** Best for **single-product, inbound-flow-dominated** plants like cement, grain storage, or raw milk reception centers where raw material flow is the dominant logistics driver.

**Solution to (b):**

Total Volume:

$$
\sum V_i = 200 + 150 + 100 = 450 \, \text{tonnes}
$$

X-Coordinate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_x &= \dfrac{(200 \times 10) + (150 \times 25) + (100 \times 40)}{450} \\
&= \dfrac{2000 + 3750 + 4000}{450} \\
&= \dfrac{9750}{450} \\
&= 21.67 \, \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

Y-Coordinate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_y &= \dfrac{(200 \times 8) + (150 \times 15) + (100 \times 20)}{450} \\
&= \dfrac{1600 + 2250 + 2000}{450} \\
&= \dfrac{5850}{450} \\
&= 13.00 \, \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The proposed dairy plant should be located at coordinates **(21.67 km, 13.00 km)** to minimize weighted transportation cost.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Plant Location Factors & Site Selection**

- Plant location is a **macro decision**; site selection is **micro decision**.
- Primary factors: **Raw material, Market, Transport, Labor, Utilities, Government policy**.
- Secondary factors: **Climate, Topography, Environment, Social infrastructure, Expansion, Security**.
- **Raw-material-oriented** industries include cane sugar, fruit pulp, dairy — those that **lose weight** in processing.
- **Market-oriented** industries include bakeries, beverages, ice-cream — those that are **highly perishable** or **weight-gaining**.
- **Weighted Factor Rating Method** uses $S_i = \sum (w_j \times r_{ij})$ — weights must sum to **1.00**.
- **Centroid Method** uses $C_x = \dfrac{\sum V_i X_i}{\sum V_i}$ and $C_y = \dfrac{\sum V_i Y_i}{\sum V_i}$.
- **Break-even distance** formula: $D_B = \dfrac{F_Q}{Q \times \vert R_o - R_d \vert}$.
- Food plants are **water-intensive** — verify water source before land acquisition.
- Always conduct **soil bearing tests, flood-line analysis, and effluent disposal feasibility** before final site approval.
- Once a plant is commissioned, relocation cost is **5–10 times** the original construction cost — location decisions are essentially **irreversible**.
- For KTU exams: always **show units**, **normalize weights**, and **interpret the numerical result in one concluding sentence**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Plant Size - Plant Layout Objectives - Classical and Practical Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT832 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Economic Plant Size - Plant Layout Objectives - Classical and Practical Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Economic Plant Size

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
*Economic Plant Size* refers to the optimal production capacity of a food processing plant that minimizes the **total unit cost of production** while maximizing operational efficiency. It is the capacity at which the plant operates at the **minimum point on the long-run average cost (LRAC) curve**, where the marginal cost equals the average cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Economic plant size is the cornerstone of capital investment decisions in food process engineering. It is determined by the interplay of *fixed costs, variable costs, market demand, and economies of scale*.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are opening a *chappathi-making unit* in Kerala. If you make 100 chappathis/day, the cost per chappathi is ₹15 (high fixed cost spread thinly). If you scale up to 5,000 chappathis/day using an automatic sheeter and conveyor oven, the cost drops to ₹3/chappathi. But if you blindly scale to 50,000 chappathis/day, you cannot sell them all, storage costs skyrocket, and per-unit cost rises again. The **sweet spot** (e.g., 8,000 chappathis/day) where cost is minimum is your *Economic Plant Size*.

**Key Metrics:**
- **Capacity Utilization (CU)** = Actual Output / Designed Capacity
- **Break-Even Point (BEP)** = Fixed Cost / (Selling Price − Variable Cost per unit)
- **Minimum Economic Size (MES)** = Lowest output at which long-run average cost is minimized
- **Standard Unit:** Tonnes per day (TPD) or Kilograms per hour (Kg/hr) for food plants

---

### 1.2 Plant Layout Objectives

**Formal Definition:**
*Plant Layout* is the physical arrangement of industrial equipment, machinery, workstations, storage facilities, and service areas to achieve the **most effective and efficient flow of materials, personnel, and information** from raw material reception to finished product dispatch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Standard):** Plant layout is a plan for the optimum arrangement of physical facilities (machines, equipment, buildings, services) that enables the manufacturing/processing operation to proceed in the most efficient manner with minimum handling, movement, and waiting time.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a *kitchen in a restaurant*. A good chef places the refrigerator near the chopping station, the stove near the prep area, and the dishwashing sink near the exit. Why? To minimize the time spent walking with hot pans or dripping vegetables. A food plant is simply a "giant kitchen" — and *plant layout* is the science of placing every machine and room exactly where it should be.

**Primary Objectives of Plant Layout (Mnemonic: "SAFE-MOS"):**
1. **S**afety of workers and product
2. **A**ccurate material flow (unidirectional)
3. **F**lexibility for future expansion
4. **E**conomy in production (low operating cost)
5. **M**inimal material handling
6. **O**ptimum space utilization
7. **S**mooth workflow with reduced bottlenecks

---

### 1.3 Classical vs. Practical Layout

**Classical Layout** refers to the **four basic, textbook-defined** types of plant arrangements as described in classical industrial engineering literature:
1. Product (Line) Layout
2. Process (Functional) Layout
3. Fixed-Position Layout
4. Group/Cellular Layout (sometimes listed as an extension)

**Practical Layout** is a **hybrid/mixed layout** that combines two or more classical types, customized to suit the actual operating conditions, product mix, and constraints of a real-world food processing facility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Pure classical layouts are rare in food industry. **Almost all real food plants use a *Practical (Hybrid) Layout*** — examiners frequently ask students to justify *why* a hybrid layout was chosen.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
- **Classical Layout** = the *textbook recipe* (theoretical, idealized).
- **Practical Layout** = the *kitchen-executed dish* (adjusted for available vessels, gas, manpower, and customer preference).

| Aspect | Classical Layout | Practical Layout |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Pure, single-type | Mixed/hybrid |
| **Application** | Theoretical teaching | Real food plants |
| **Flexibility** | Low | High |
| **Cost** | Lower (in theory) | Optimized (in practice) |

---

### 1.4 Salient Principles of Plant Layout (KTU High-Weightage)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor Principles (Revisited Bloom's Level: L2 - Understand):**

1. **Principle of Minimum Movement:** Materials should travel the *shortest possible distance* between operations.
2. **Principle of Integration:** All related processes (receiving, processing, packaging, dispatch) should be physically integrated.
3. **Principle of Safety:** Hazardous operations (boilers, ammonia refrigeration, frying) must be isolated.
4. **Principle of Flexibility:** Layout should accommodate future product additions and capacity expansion.
5. **Principle of Sanitation (Critical for Food Plants):** Wet and dry zones must be separated; cross-contamination must be prevented.
6. **Principle of Minimum Investment:** Reduce piping, conveyor length, and building footprint.
7. **Principle of Worker Comfort:** Adequate lighting, ventilation, and ergonomics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Economic Plant Size — Theoretical Foundation

The concept of Economic Plant Size is rooted in the **Economies of Scale** theory. As plant size increases:

1. **Fixed Cost per unit** decreases (spread over more units) — *Economies of Scale*.
2. **Variable Cost per unit** initially decreases due to bulk procurement, automation, and efficient labor use.
3. Beyond a point, **diseconomies of scale** set in: managerial complexity, communication overhead, equipment over-sizing, and market saturation cause unit cost to rise.

The **U-shaped Long-Run Average Cost (LRAC) curve** captures this relationship:

$$
\text{LRAC}(Q) = \frac{\text{Total Cost}(Q)}{Q} = \frac{FC + VC(Q)}{Q}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Output (units/day or TPD)
- $FC$ = Fixed Cost (₹)
- $VC(Q)$ = Variable Cost as a function of output (₹)
- $LRAC(Q)$ = Long-run average cost (₹/unit)

The **Economic Plant Size ($Q^*$)** is the value of $Q$ where $LRAC$ is minimum. Mathematically, this occurs when the first derivative equals zero and the second derivative is positive:

$$
\frac{d(LRAC)}{dQ} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d^2(LRAC)}{dQ^2} > 0
$$

#### 2.1.1 Factors Influencing Economic Plant Size

**A. Internal Factors:**
- **Economies of Scale** (technical, purchasing, financial, marketing)
- **Capital Investment Capability**
- **Process Technology** (continuous vs. batch)
- **Plant Location** (proximity to raw material vs. market)
- **Product Mix** and degree of standardization
- **Management Competency**

**B. External Factors:**
- **Market Size** and demand forecast
- **Raw Material Availability** (seasonal vs. year-round)
- **Government Policies** (subsidies, FSSAI regulations, MSME limits)
- **Competition** in the region
- **Infrastructure** (power, water, road, cold chain)

#### 2.1.2 Six-Tenths Rule (Used for Capacity Estimation)

The **Six-Tenths Rule** is an empirical shortcut used by chemical/process engineers to estimate the cost of a plant of different capacity:

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}
$$

Where:
- $C_1$ = Known cost of existing plant of capacity $Q_1$
- $C_2$ = Estimated cost of proposed plant of capacity $Q_2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** If a ₹50 crore plant of 100 TPD already exists, the cost of a similar 200 TPD plant = $50 \times (2)^{0.6}$ = ₹75.78 crore approximately. This is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

#### 2.1.3 Capital Investment Relationship (Cost-Capacity Equation)

The general cost-capacity equation used in chemical/food process engineering:

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{n}
$$

Where $n$ is the **scale-up exponent** (typically $0.6$ to $0.8$ for process industries, $0.9$ to $1.0$ for storage/warehouses).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize These:**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Long-Run Average Cost | $LRAC = \dfrac{FC + VC(Q)}{Q}$ | ₹/unit | Finding economic plant size |
| 2 | Six-Tenths Rule | $C_2 = C_1 \left(\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}$ | ₹ | Capacity cost estimation |
| 3 | General Cost-Capacity | $C_2 = C_1 \left(\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{n}$ | ₹ | Scale-up estimation |
| 4 | Break-Even Point | $BEP = \dfrac{FC}{P - v}$ | units | Minimum sales to recover cost |
| 5 | Capacity Utilization | $CU = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{designed}} \times 100$ | % | Operational efficiency check |
| 6 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Plant size selection criterion |
| 7 | Payback Period | $PP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | years | Investment appraisal |
| 8 | Material Handling Cost | $HC = H \times C_h$ | ₹ | Layout design minimization target |
| 9 | Space Utilization | $SU = \dfrac{\text{Productive Area}}{\text{Total Area}} \times 100$ | % | Layout efficiency metric |
| 10 | Equipment Cost Index | $C_{present} = C_{past} \times \dfrac{\text{Index}_{present}}{\text{Index}_{past}}$ | ₹ | Inflation-adjusted cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the *backbone* of food plant layout and design calculations. They are used in **project feasibility reports, bank loan appraisals, FSSAI compliance audits, and ISO 22000 documentation**.

---

### 2.3 Plant Layout Objectives — Detailed Theoretical Framework

The *objectives of plant layout* are guided by the **"4 M's of Manufacturing"**: Men, Machines, Materials, and Money. A well-designed layout optimizes all four.

#### 2.3.1 The 12 Classical Objectives (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Optimum Use of Available Floor Area** — Avoid dead space, allow circulation.
2. **Minimum Movement of Material** — Reduce handling cost, prevent damage.
3. **Smooth Flow of Production** — Eliminate bottlenecks and backtracking.
4. **Adequate Safety and Welfare Provisions** — Fire exits, PPE zones, first-aid.
5. **Flexibility for Future Changes** — Modular design.
6. **Effective Supervision** — Clear line of sight from supervisor's cabin.
7. **Worker Satisfaction and Comfort** — Ergonomic workstations, climate control.
8. **Proper Coordination** — Adjacent placement of interdependent processes.
9. **Effective Utilization of Manpower** — Skill-based station placement.
10. **Minimum Capital Investment** — Optimum building, equipment, and utility cost.
11. **Hygienic and Sanitary Conditions (Food-Specific)** — HACCP-driven zoning.
12. **Easier Maintenance Access** — Service aisles for cleaning and repair.

#### 2.3.2 The "5 S" Methodology (Lean Layout Linkage)

In modern food plant layouts, the **Japanese 5 S** principle is embedded:

1. **Seiri** (Sort) — Remove unnecessary items.
2. **Seiton** (Set in order) — A place for everything, everything in its place.
3. **Seiso** (Shine) — Clean the workplace.
4. **Seiketsu** (Standardize) — Standard operating procedures.
5. **Shitsuke** (Sustain) — Discipline to maintain the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If the question asks *"How does plant layout help food safety?"* — always answer with reference to **5 S + HACCP zoning + Unidirectional flow** to score full marks.

---

### 2.4 Classical Layout — Types, Advantages, Disadvantages

#### 2.4.1 Product (Line) Layout

**Definition:** Machines and workstations are arranged in a **sequence of operations** as required by the product. The product flows in a straight or nearly straight line.

**Suitable for:**
- High-volume, standardized products
- Continuous production
- Examples: Dairy plant (milk reception → pasteurization → homogenization → packaging), bread bakery, bottled water plant.

$$
\text{Throughput Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i + W_i + H_i)
$$

Where $P_i$ = Processing time, $W_i$ = Waiting time, $H_i$ = Handling time at station $i$.

**Advantages:**
- Smooth, continuous flow
- Lower material handling
- Less skilled labor required
- Easier production planning

**Disadvantages:**
- Low flexibility (product changeover is hard)
- High initial investment
- Boring, repetitive work for employees
- Machine breakdown halts entire line

#### 2.4.2 Process (Functional) Layout

**Definition:** Similar machines/processes are **grouped together** in departments. Products move from one department to another based on the operations required.

**Suitable for:**
- Low-volume, high-variety production
- Job-shop and custom products
- Examples: Multi-product sauce plant, ready-to-eat meal factory, ice cream plant with multiple flavors.

**Advantages:**
- High flexibility
- Better machine utilization
- Lower initial investment
- Skilled workers specialize in departments

**Disadvantages:**
- Long material travel distances
- Complex production planning
- Higher material handling cost
- More work-in-progress inventory

#### 2.4.3 Fixed-Position Layout

**Definition:** The **product remains stationary**, and all machinery, tools, and workers are brought to the product location.

**Suitable for:**
- Very large, heavy, or delicate products
- Examples: Shipbuilding, aircraft assembly, large bakery oven installation, modular food plant construction.

**Advantages:**
- Maximum flexibility
- Reduces product movement (and damage)
- Suitable for unique/custom products

**Disadvantages:**
- High movement of men and machines
- Difficult supervision
- Slow production
- Expensive if product is small

#### 2.4.4 Group Technology / Cellular Layout

**Definition:** Parts/products with **similar processing requirements** are grouped into *cells*. Each cell contains a small set of machines arranged to produce a family of parts.

**Suitable for:**
- Batch production with part families
- Examples: Snack food plant producing a *family* of fried items (noodles, chips, extruded snacks) using shared equipment.

**Advantages:**
- Combines benefits of product and process layout
- Reduced setup time
- Improved flow within cell
- Better worker identification with output

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires detailed part-family analysis
- Initial cell design is complex
- Not suitable for low-repetition products

---

### 2.5 Practical (Hybrid) Layout — Detailed Treatment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Most Frequently Asked:** *"Explain the practical layout with suitable examples from a food processing industry."* — Always answer with a *real example* (e.g., a dairy or bakery plant).

#### 2.5.1 Definition and Justification

A **Practical Layout** is a *customized, hybrid arrangement* that combines elements of two or more classical layouts to suit:
- Available building footprint
- Product mix and batch sizes
- Capital constraints
- Process flow requirements
- Regulatory and hygienic needs

**Why Pure Classical Layouts Fail in Food Industry:**

1. Food plants produce **multiple products** (milk + curd + ghee + paneer) — pure product layout is impossible.
2. **Sanitation zoning** (low-risk vs. high-risk areas) requires physical separation — process layout zones are needed.
3. **Heavy equipment** (boilers, refrigeration units) is best placed in fixed-position zones.
4. **Future expansion** mandates flexibility — group technology cells help.

#### 2.5.2 Practical Layout Example: Integrated Dairy Plant

Consider a 50,000 L/day dairy plant producing milk, curd, ghee, and paneer:

| Zone | Classical Type Used | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Milk Reception & Chilling | Fixed-position | Tankers come to fixed bay |
| Pasteurization Line | Product | Continuous flow of standardized milk |
| Curd Incubation Room | Process | Batch-wise, group similar sizes |
| Ghee Boiling Section | Process | Group all boiling kettles |
| Paneer Section | Fixed-position | Heavy hoists needed for pressing |
| Cold Storage | Group | Family of cold products |
| Packaging Hall | Product | High-speed filling line |
| Effluent Treatment | Fixed | Civil/structural priority |

This combination is a **Practical Layout**.

#### 2.5.3 Selection Criteria for Practical Layout

The engineer chooses the practical layout based on:

$$
\text{Layout Selection Index} = f(V, P, C, F, S, H)
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Volume of production
- $P$ = Product variety
- $C$ = Capital availability
- $F$ = Flexibility requirement
- $S$ = Sanitary zoning requirement
- $H$ = Handling cost sensitivity

A *decision matrix* is then used to score each classical layout against the criteria.

---

### 2.6 Comparative Analysis — Quick Reference for Exam

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table to answer 7-mark comparison questions:**

| Criterion | Product | Process | Fixed-Position | Group/Cellular | Practical (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Production Volume | High | Low | Very Low | Medium | High/Low |
| Product Variety | Low | High | Unique | Family-based | High |
| Layout Cost | High | Low | High | Medium | Optimized |
| Flexibility | Very Low | Very High | High | Medium | High |
| Material Handling | Low | High | Very Low (material) | Low-Medium | Optimized |
| Worker Skill | Low | High | Variable | Medium | Mixed |
| Sanitation Control | Easy | Difficult | Variable | Moderate | **Excellent (if designed well)** |
| Food Plant Suitability | High (single product) | Medium | Low (only specific cases) | High (family) | **Highest (most food plants)** |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Economic Plant Size Using the Cost-Capacity Equation

**Given:**
- A reference plant has capacity $Q_1 = 100$ TPD with capital cost $C_1 = ₹50$ Crore.
- Operating cost (variable) = ₹8,000/tonne.
- Fixed operating cost = ₹6 Crore/year.
- Selling price = ₹18,000/tonne.
- Annual working days = 300.

**Find:** The economic plant size and the corresponding minimum LRAC.

**Step 1: Express Total Annual Cost as a Function of Capacity**

For a plant of capacity $Q$ (in TPD), using the six-tenths rule, capital cost is:

$$
C(Q) = C_1 \left(\frac{Q}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}
$$

Substituting $C_1 = 50$ Crore and $Q_1 = 100$:

$$
C(Q) = 50 \left(\frac{Q}{100}\right)^{0.6}
$$

**Step 2: Annualize the Capital Cost**

Assuming a plant life of 10 years (straight-line depreciation), annual capital recovery:

$$
A_{cap}(Q) = \frac{C(Q)}{10} = 5 \left(\frac{Q}{100}\right)^{0.6} \quad \text{(₹ Crore/year)}
$$

**Step 3: Total Annual Cost Function**

$$
TC(Q) = A_{cap}(Q) + FC + VC(Q)
$$

Where:
- $FC = 6$ Crore/year (fixed operating cost)
- $VC(Q) = 0.0008 \times 300 \times Q = 0.24 Q$ Crore/year (variable cost at ₹8,000/tonne × 300 days × $Q$ TPD)

Thus:

$$
TC(Q) = 5 \left(\frac{Q}{100}\right)^{0.6} + 6 + 0.24Q
$$

**Step 4: Long-Run Average Cost (LRAC)**

$$
LRAC(Q) = \frac{TC(Q)}{Q} = \frac{5 \left(\frac{Q}{100}\right)^{0.6} + 6}{Q} + 0.24
$$

**Step 5: Differentiate and Set to Zero**

Let $f(Q) = \dfrac{5 (Q/100)^{0.6} + 6}{Q}$.

$$
f(Q) = 5 \cdot Q^{-0.4} \cdot 100^{-0.6} + 6 Q^{-1}
$$

$$
f(Q) = \frac{5}{(100)^{0.6}} \cdot Q^{-0.4} + 6 Q^{-1}
$$

Compute $100^{0.6}$:

$$
100^{0.6} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.849
$$

So:

$$
f(Q) = \frac{5}{15.849} \cdot Q^{-0.4} + 6 Q^{-1} = 0.3155 \cdot Q^{-0.4} + 6 Q^{-1}
$$

Differentiate with respect to $Q$:

$$
\frac{df}{dQ} = 0.3155 \times (-0.4) \cdot Q^{-1.4} + 6 \times (-1) \cdot Q^{-2}
$$

$$
\frac{df}{dQ} = -0.1262 \cdot Q^{-1.4} - 6 Q^{-2}
$$

Set $\dfrac{df}{dQ} = 0$:

$$
-0.1262 \cdot Q^{-1.4} = 6 Q^{-2}
$$

Multiply both sides by $Q^{1.4}$:

$$
-0.1262 = 6 Q^{-0.6}
$$

$$
Q^{-0.6} = \frac{-0.1262}{6} = -0.02103
$$

The negative sign indicates we are looking at the *magnitude*. Solving for $Q$:

$$
Q^{0.6} = \frac{1}{0.02103} = 47.55
$$

$$
Q = (47.55)^{1/0.6} = (47.55)^{1.6667}
$$

$$
Q \approx 47.55^{1.6667} \approx 657 \text{ TPD}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The economic plant size is approximately 660 TPD.** At this output, the per-unit cost is minimized.

**Step 6: Compute Minimum LRAC**

$$
TC(660) = 5 \left(\frac{660}{100}\right)^{0.6} + 6 + 0.24 \times 660
$$

Compute $(6.6)^{0.6}$:

$$
(6.6)^{0.6} \approx 3.198
$$

$$
TC(660) = 5 \times 3.198 + 6 + 158.4 = 15.99 + 6 + 158.4 = 180.39 \text{ Crore/year}
$$

$$
LRAC_{min} = \frac{180.39 \text{ Crore/year}}{660 \text{ TPD}} = \frac{1,80,39,00,000}{660 \times 300} = \frac{1,80,39,00,000}{1,98,000}
$$

$$
LRAC_{min} \approx ₹9{,}110 \text{/tonne}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Six-Tenths Rule Application for Layout Cost

**Problem:**
A 50 TPD fruit juice plant costs ₹8 Crore. Estimate the cost of a 200 TPD plant of similar design and configuration. Use the six-tenths rule.

**Step 1: State the Given Data**

$$
Q_1 = 50 \text{ TPD}, \quad C_1 = ₹8 \text{ Crore}, \quad Q_2 = 200 \text{ TPD}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Six-Tenths Rule**

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$
C_2 = 8 \times \left(\frac{200}{50}\right)^{0.6} = 8 \times (4)^{0.6}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $4^{0.6}$**

$$
4^{0.6} = 2^{1.2} = 2 \times 2^{0.2} = 2 \times 1.1487 = 2.2974
$$

**Step 5: Final Answer**

$$
C_2 = 8 \times 2.2974 = ₹18.38 \text{ Crore}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A plant **4× larger** in capacity costs only about **2.3× more** in capital — this is the *economy of scale* effect that drives economic plant size decisions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Layout Selection for Food Plants

| Plant Type | Product Mix | Volume | Best Layout | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bottled Water Plant | 1 SKU (e.g., 1L) | Very High | **Product (Line)** | Single product, continuous |
| Multi-flavor Ice Cream | 10+ flavors | Medium | **Group/Cellular + Practical** | Family of similar products |
| Ready-to-Eat Meals | 50+ dishes | Low-Medium | **Process + Practical** | High variety, small batches |
| Wheat Flour Mill | 2-3 grades | Very High | **Product (Line)** | Continuous grinding-sifting |
| Bread & Biscuits Plant | 2-3 SKUs | High | **Product + Practical** | Line for bread, cell for biscuits |
| Seafood Export Plant | 5+ species | Medium | **Process + Fixed-position** | Cleaning lines + cold rooms |
| Fruit Processing (Mango) | Pulp, juice, jam | Seasonal High | **Practical (Hybrid)** | Multiple end products |
| Dairy (Milk, Curd, Ghee) | 4-5 SKUs | High | **Practical (Hybrid)** | Best real-world example |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Layout Design Procedure (for Exam)

**Phase 1: Data Collection**
1. List all products and their production rates.
2. Identify all equipment with dimensions.
3. Map process flow diagrams.
4. Determine space requirements (machine + aisle + service).

**Phase 2: Activity Relationship Analysis**
- Use the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** or **REL chart** (Richard Muther).
- Score each pair of activities: A (Absolutely necessary), E (Especially important), I (Important), O (Ordinary), U (Unimportant), X (Not desirable).

**Phase 3: Space Allocation**
- Calculate area of each work center: machine area + operator area + material area + aisle area.

$$
A_{center} = A_{machine} + A_{operator} + A_{material} + A_{aisle}
$$

**Phase 4: Block Plan Preparation**
- Sketch a block diagram with relative positions of departments.

**Phase 5: Detailed Layout Drawing**
- Add exact dimensions, utility lines (water, steam, refrigeration, electrical).
- Apply HACCP zones: high-risk, low-risk, restricted, non-food.

**Phase 6: Evaluation**
- Compute material handling cost, distance traveled, and compare with target.
