# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plant Layout Development Methodology — Block Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete application workflow** for developing a plant layout, as required by KTU Module 2:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Product Mix and Batch Size] --> B[Step 2: Develop Process Flow Diagram PFD]
    B --> C[Step 3: List Equipment with Footprints]
    C --> D[Step 4: Construct Activity Relationship Chart ARC]
    D --> E[Step 5: Generate Bubble Diagram from ARC]
    E --> F[Step 6: Design Material Flow Path]
    F --> G[Step 7: Compute Handling Cost per Route]
    G --> H[Step 8: Iterate Layout Alternatives]
    H --> I[Step 9: Apply HACCP Zoning Rules]
    I --> J[Step 10: Validate via Simulation Software]
    J --> K[Final Detailed Layout with Scaled Drawings]
```

## 4.2 Plant Layout Application — Functional Architecture

This diagram shows **how each tool feeds into the next** in a real plant layout project:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PFD[Process Flow Diagram] --> EQ[Equipment List]
    EQ --> SP[Space Requirements]
    SP --> ARC[Activity Relationship Chart]
    ARC --> BUB[Bubble Diagram]
    BUB --> BLO[Block Layout]
    SP --> BLO
    BLO --> FLW[Flow Path Analysis]
    FLW --> HC[Handling Cost Calculation]
    HC --> OPT[Optimized Layout Selection]
    BLO --> DET[Detailed Engineering Layout]
    DET --> SIM[Digital Simulation Validation]
    SIM --> FINAL[Approved Plant Layout]
    HC --> OPT
    OPT --> DET
```

## 4.3 Material Flow Path Diagram — Generic Food Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RM[1 Raw Material Receiving] --> ST[2 Storage]
    ST --> CL[3 Cleaning and Sorting]
    CL --> PR[4 Primary Processing]
    PR --> SC[5 Secondary Cooking]
    SC --> PC[6 Cooling]
    PC --> PK[7 Packaging Line]
    PK --> QS[8 Quarantine Storage]
    QS --> QC{9 QC Approval}
    QC -->|Pass| FG[10 Finished Goods]
    QC -->|Fail| RT[11 Rework or Reject]
    FG --> DP[12 Dispatch Bay]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In your KTU answer, **number every block** sequentially and **write the equipment name** inside each block. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if arrows are unlabelled.

## 4.4 Block Layout — Fruit Juice Plant (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZoneA[Zone A Receiving]
        A1[Receiving Bay]
        A2[QC Checkpoint]
    end
    subgraph ZoneB[Zone B Storage]
        B1[Cold Room for Fruits]
        B2[Ambient Dry Store]
    end
    subgraph ZoneC[Zone C Processing]
        C1[Washing Tank]
        C2[Pulper]
        C3[Pasteurizer]
        C4[Storage Tank]
    end
    subgraph ZoneD[Zone D Packaging]
        D1[Filling Line]
        D2[Labelling]
        D3[Cartoning]
    end
    subgraph ZoneE[Zone E Dispatch]
        E1[Finished Goods Store]
        E2[Loading Dock]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This zone-based layout follows **HACCP zoning** — Zone A and Zone E are physically separated (different entry points), and the product only flows **forward** with no back-tracking.

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Choosing the Best Layout Alternative

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Two Layout Alternatives Proposed] --> M1[Draw Material Flow Path for Alt 1]
    S --> M2[Draw Material Flow Path for Alt 2]
    M1 --> C1[Compute Handling Cost Alt 1]
    M2 --> C2[Compute Handling Cost Alt 2]
    C1 --> CMP{Compare Total Costs}
    C2 --> CMP
    CMP -->|Alt 1 Cheaper| SEL1[Select Alternative 1]
    CMP -->|Alt 2 Cheaper| SEL2[Select Alternative 2]
    CMP -->|Costs Equal| HACCP[Apply HACCP Zoning Tie Breaker]
    HACCP --> SEL3[Choose Best Zoning Compliance]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**List any four tools used in the application phase of plant layout development.**

**Model Answer:**
The four commonly used tools are:
1. **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** — establishes sequence of operations using standard symbols (○, →, ▽).
2. **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** — Muther's matrix showing closeness ratings (A, E, I, O, U, X) between every pair of departments.
3. **Bubble Diagram / Relationship Diagramming** — converts the ARC into a 2D spatial sketch using bubbles and connecting lines.
4. **Flow Path Diagram** — scaled drawing tracing the physical movement of materials, used to compute handling cost and detect back-tracking.

*(For 3 marks, naming and one-line description of each tool is sufficient.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the significance of computing the "Utilization Factor" in plant layout application?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Utilization Factor** $\eta$ is the ratio of productive equipment area to the total production floor area:

$$\eta = \dfrac{A_{\text{used}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

Its significance in plant layout application is:
- It indicates **how efficiently** the floor space is being used by equipment.
- For food plants, the KTU benchmark is **75–85%** — below this means wasted rent; above this means insufficient operator access.
- It directly affects the **Total Floor Area calculation**:
$$A_{\text{Total}} = \dfrac{\sum A_e}{\eta}$$
- It is a key **comparison metric** when evaluating two alternative layout proposals.

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

You are appointed as a **Plant Layout Engineer** to design a small-scale **Tomato Ketchup Manufacturing Unit** with a target capacity of **2 tonnes/day**. Answer the following:

**(a)** Develop the complete **Process Flow Diagram** for the unit, naming at least 8 unit operations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct an **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** for any 6 departments and justify each rating. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The process flow diagram for a **2 tonnes/day tomato ketchup** unit is:

| Step | Operation | Equipment | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raw tomato receiving & weighing | Platform balance | ○ |
| 2 | Cold storage of tomatoes | Cold room (4 °C) | ▽ |
| 3 | Washing | Rotary drum washer | ○ |
| 4 | Sorting & grading | Conveyor + manual | ○ |
| 5 | Crushing / Pulping | Pulper | ○ |
| 6 | Cooking with spices & sugar | Steam-jacketed kettle | ○ |
| 7 | Refining / sieving | Pulper-finisher | ○ |
| 8 | Pasteurization | Plate heat exchanger | ○ |
| 9 | Cooling | Cooler | ○ |
| 10 | Filling into bottles | Bottle filling machine | ○ |
| 11 | Capping & labelling | Capping machine | ○ |
| 12 | Inspection / QC | Visual + lab tests | □ |
| 13 | Cartoning & storage | Cartoning machine | ○ |
| 14 | Dispatch | Loading dock | → |

**Flow Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Raw Tomato → Weighing → Cold Store → Washing → Sorting → Pulping →
Cooking → Refining → Pasteurization → Cooling → Filling → Capping →
QC Inspection → Cartoning → Finished Goods Store → Dispatch
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU answer script, the student must draw this as a **block diagram with arrows**, marking storage with ▽, operations with ○, and inspections with □.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

The **6 departments** identified for the ketchup plant are:
1. **D1** — Raw Material Store
2. **D2** — Processing Hall (Cooking)
3. **D3** — Packaging Line
4. **D4** — Quality Control Lab
5. **D5** — Finished Goods Store
6. **D6** — Utility Room (Boiler, Compressor)

**Activity Relationship Chart:**

|  | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 | D6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **D1** | — | A | X | O | X | U |
| **D2** | A | — | E | I | U | I |
| **D3** | X | E | — | A | E | U |
| **D4** | O | I | A | — | I | U |
| **D5** | X | U | E | I | — | U |
| **D6** | U | I | U | U | U | — |

**Justifications:**
- **D1–D2 = A**: Raw tomatoes must directly enter the processing hall with no transport delay (prevents spoilage).
- **D1–D3 = X**: Raw store and packaging line must be separated (HACCP — contamination risk).
- **D2–D3 = E**: Processing and packaging must be close (hot filling requirement).
- **D3–D4 = A**: QC must inspect every packaged bottle immediately.
- **D2–D6 = I**: Processing needs boiler steam; utility room must be close.
- **D1–D5 = X**: Raw store and finished goods must never be adjacent (HACCP).

From this ARC, students should next draw the **bubble diagram** (placing A-rated pairs adjacent, X-rated pairs far apart).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by writing **ratings without justification**. Every cell MUST have a one-line reason. Also, students often confuse "I" (Important) with "E" (Especially Important) — remember that **E > I > O** in closeness priority.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for developing a plant layout for a food processing industry, with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fruit processing unit has equipment with the following total footprint: $\sum A_e = 240 \text{ m}^2$. If the **utilization factor** is 75% and **auxiliary areas** add 60% to the production floor area, calculate the **total plot area** required. Also suggest a suitable rectangular plot dimension. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step-by-Step Procedure for Plant Layout Development:**

**Step 1 — Define the Product and Capacity**
- Identify the food products (e.g., fruit juice, ketchup, bakery).
- Determine **batch size** and **daily production target**.

**Step 2 — Develop the Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**
- Use the standard symbols (○, →, ▽, □, D).
- Identify all **operations, transports, storages, inspections, and delays**.

**Step 3 — Prepare the Equipment List**
- List all machinery with **length, width, height, weight, utility needs** (steam, electricity, water).

**Step 4 — Construct the Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)**
- Apply Muther's ratings (A, E, I, O, U, X) between every pair of departments.

**Step 5 — Generate Bubble Diagram**
- Translate the ARC into a **2D relationship diagram** showing departmental adjacency.

**Step 6 — Block Layout Drafting**
- Convert bubble diagram into **scaled blocks** indicating department boundaries and dimensions.

**Step 7 — Material Flow Path Design**
- Draw the **actual path of materials** through the block layout.
- Count **back-tracking** instances (each back-track = layout flaw).

**Step 8 — Handling Cost Computation**
- For each material route, compute $H = C \times D \times F$.
- Identify the **costliest routes** and minimize their distance.

**Step 9 — Apply Safety & Regulatory Requirements**
- **HACCP zoning**, **GMP corridors**, **fire exits**, **emergency showers**.
- Separate **high-risk** (raw meat) and **low-risk** (finished) zones.

**Step 10 — Detailed Layout and Simulation Validation**
- Convert block layout into **architectural drawings** (1:100 scale).
- Validate using **FlexSim / Arena Simulation** for throughput bottlenecks.

**Step 11 — Final Approval**
- Submit to FSSAI, Pollution Control Board, Factory Inspector.

**Block Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Product Definition] --> A2[PFD Preparation]
    A2 --> A3[Equipment List]
    A3 --> A4[ARC Construction]
    A4 --> A5[Bubble Diagram]
    A5 --> A6[Block Layout]
    A6 --> A7[Material Flow Design]
    A7 --> A8[Handling Cost Analysis]
    A8 --> A9[HACCP and Safety Audit]
    A9 --> A10[Detailed Engineering Drawings]
    A10 --> A11[Simulation Validation]
    A11 --> A12[Final Approved Layout]
```

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Total equipment area: $\sum A_e = 240 \text{ m}^2$
- Utilization factor: $\eta = 75\% = 0.75$
- Auxiliary area = 60% of production floor area

**Step 1: Compute the Production Floor Area**

$$A_{\text{Production}} = \dfrac{\sum A_e}{\eta} = \dfrac{240}{0.75} = 320 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the Auxiliary Area**

$$A_{\text{Auxiliary}} = 0.60 \times A_{\text{Production}} = 0.60 \times 320 = 192 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the Total Plot Area**

$$A_{\text{Total}} = A_{\text{Production}} + A_{\text{Auxiliary}} = 320 + 192 = 512 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Suggest a Rectangular Plot**

A practical rectangular plot must:
- Provide a **width-to-length ratio between 1:1 and 1:2** (for natural ventilation).
- Be aligned **East-West** for the longest dimension (solar orientation).

**Option 1: 32 m × 16 m = 512 m²** (ratio 1:2 — best for narrow sites)
**Option 2: 25.6 m × 20 m = 512 m²** (ratio ~1.28:1 — preferred for square-ish land)
**Option 3: 22 m × 23.3 m ≈ 512 m²** (nearly square)

**Recommended:** **25.6 m × 20 m**, allowing:
- A **4 m wide central corridor** running the length.
- A **6 m utility zone** along the north side (boiler, compressor).
- An **8 m loading/unloading bay** on the south side.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Common errors in numerical questions:
> 1. Students **forget to add auxiliary area** — only computing 320 m² instead of 512 m². This loses 3 marks.
> 2. Students **confuse utilization factor formula** — writing $\eta \times \sum A_e$ instead of $\sum A_e / \eta$. The denominator form is correct.
> 3. Students **omit plot dimension recommendation** — a numerical question always expects a "practical interpretation" answer.

---

## **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- ✅ Plant layout application is the **process of converting a recipe into a floor plan** using standardized engineering tools.
- ✅ The **5-phase methodology**: Product Analysis → PFD → ARC → Flow Path → Detailed Layout.
- ✅ **Standard PFD Symbols**: ○ (Operation), → (Transport), ▽ (Storage), □ (Inspection), D (Delay).
- ✅ **ARC Ratings** in decreasing priority: **A > E > I > O > U**, with **X** for undesirable.
- ✅ **Handling Cost Formula**: $H = C \times D \times F \times M$ — the single most-tested formula.
- ✅ **Utilization Factor** for food plants: **75–85%** is the KTU benchmark.
- ✅ **Total Floor Area** = (Equipment Area / Utilization Factor) × (1 + Auxiliary %).
- ✅ **HACCP zoning** mandates **physical separation** of raw and finished product zones.
- ✅ **Back-tracking** in flow path = layout flaw — always minimize.
- ✅ **Modern tools**: AutoCAD Plant 3D, FlexSim, Arena Simulation for validation.
- ✅ **Plot Orientation**: Long axis East-West for solar control in tropical climates.
- ✅ **Bubble Diagram** is the visual translation of ARC — every KTU 14-mark question expects one.
- ✅ **Always justify** every ARC rating with a one-line reason — un-justified cells lose marks.
- ✅ **Buffer storage** between batch and continuous stages is essential — compute as $S_b = R \times t_{\text{downstream}}$.
- ✅ **Pipe velocity** for sanitary food piping: **1.5–2.5 m/s** (above this causes erosion; below causes sedimentation).
- ✅ **Validation step** is mandatory — KTU expects simulation or walk-through audit before final approval.

**End of Notes — Module 2: Application in Development of Plant Layout**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flooring and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT832 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Flooring and Types # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flooring in a Food Processing Plant** is defined as the **horizontal, load-bearing, traffic-bearing, and hygiene-critical surface system** that forms the working floor of a processing hall. It is engineered to simultaneously satisfy **structural load transfer, hygienic containment, microbial control, chemical resistance, slip resistance, thermal stability, and cleanability**, while maintaining a **coved (curved) intersection with the wall** to eliminate 90° corners where soil and microbes accumulate. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) framework for **OEFTT832 – Food Plant Layout and Design**, flooring is treated not merely as a civil-finishing element but as a **Primary Hygienic Barrier (PHB)**. The floor is the **first line of defence** against: 1. Microbial harborage (biofilm formation) 2. Cross-contamination between product streams 3. Slip-and-fall occupational hazards 4. Chemical attack from cleaning agents (CIP, COP, sanitizers) 5. Mechanical damage from forklifts, trolleys, and dropped utensils > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Benchmark:** International standards governing food-plant flooring include **3-A Sanitary Standards (USA)**, **EHEDG Doc. 8 (European Hygienic Engineering & Design Group)**, **FDA 21 CFR Part 110**, and **IS 1199 / IS 3384 (Bureau of Indian Standards)**. The universally quoted minimum coving radius is **≥ 3 mm (preferably ≥ 25 mm in wet zones)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine the floor of a food plant as the **"skin of a cleanroom."** Just as human skin protects the body from pathogens, regulates temperature, and resists abrasion, the floor of a food plant must protect the **product**, **worker**, and **machinery** from contamination, injury, and degradation. **Plain-English Analogy — "The Kitchen-Floor vs. The Bathroom-Floor Story":** Think of your home **kitchen floor** (tile, easy to mop, stain-resistant) versus your **bathroom floor** (anti-skid, water-proof coving at the wall-floor joint). A food plant floor is essentially a **"supercharged bathroom floor"** that must: | Domestic Bathroom Floor | Industrial Food Plant Floor | |---|---| | Resists occasional water | Resists **24/7 wash-down**, hot water, steam | | Mild cleaning agents | **Acid/alkali CIP chemicals** (pH 1–14) | | Foot traffic | **Forklift + trolley + heavy equipment loads** | | Slip resistance for safety | **Slip resistance + microbial control** | | Coved at edges (sometimes) | **Mandatory coving at all wall-floor junctions** | So when you walk into a dairy plant, beverage bottling hall, or meat-processing room, the smooth, slightly glossy, gently curved-to-the-wall floor you see is a **multi-layered engineered composite** — typically a **resin-based screed (epoxy/polyurethane)** poured over a **prepared concrete sub-base**, sealed with a **food-grade topcoat**, and integrated with a **floor drain at the lowest gradient point**. --- ## 1.3 Fundamental Requirements of a Food-Plant Floor (KTU CO Alignment) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Course Outcome Mapping (CO3 / Module 3):** The student should be able to **"select appropriate construction materials and surface finishes for different zones of a food plant, justifying the choice with respect to hygiene codes, structural loads, and cleaning regimes."** The **eight cardinal requirements** of any food-plant flooring system are: 1. **Imperviousness** — Zero absorption of water, blood, oil, or chemicals. Porosity ≤ 0.5 % by volume. 2. **Cleanability** — Smooth, non-porous, free of cracks, crevices, and joints where soil lodges. 3. **Slip Resistance** — Coefficient of friction (CoF) ≥ **0.6 (wet conditions)** as per **ASTM F1677 / DIN 51130 R-Rating**. 4. **Chemical Resistance** — Stable against **pH 2 to pH 12** cleaning chemicals (nitric acid, NaOH, hypochlorite). 5. **Mechanical Strength** — Compressive strength ≥ **30 N/mm² (30 MPa)**, flexural strength ≥ **10 N/mm²**. 6. **Thermal Resistance** — Withstand thermal shock from **steam cleaning (up to 95 °C)** and cold-room exposure (**–20 °C to –40 °C**). 7. **Hygienic Jointing & Coving** — Radius ≥ **3 mm** (minimum legal); **25–50 mm** preferred at floor-wall junction. 8. **Drainage Integration** — Floor laid to a **gradient of 1:60 to 1:100** (≈ 1 %–1.67 %) toward floor drains. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Flooring Systems Food-plant floors are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: ### Axis A — By Material Composition - **Cementitious (concrete-based)** — Plain concrete, polymer-modified concrete, polyurethane-cement composites - **Resinous (thermoset polymers)** — Epoxy, polyurethane (PU), methyl methacrylate (MMA), vinyl ester - **Ceramic / Vitrified** — Quarry tile, glazed tile, acid-resistant tile (A.R. tile) - **Elastomeric** — Natural rubber, nitrile rubber, PVC sheet - **Metallic** — Stainless steel grating, aluminium tread plate (limited zones only) ### Axis B — By Functional Zone | Zone | Function | Recommended Floor | |---|---|---| | **Dry Processing** (dry blending, packing) | Dust control, light wash | Epoxy, PU, vitrified tile | | **Wet Processing** (dairy, beverage, meat) | Continuous water/chemical exposure | PU-cement, quarry tile | | **Cold Storage** (–20 °C and below) | Thermal shock resistance | PU-cement, stainless steel grating | | **High-Care / Cleanroom** (aseptic, pharma) | Sterility, particle control | Epoxy, seamless vinyl | | **Heavy Load** (warehouse, loading bay) | Forklift abrasion | PU-cement, hardened concrete with sealer | | **Anti-static / Explosion-proof** (flour mill, solvent) | Spark prevention | Static-dissipative epoxy, copper-strip grid | ### Axis C — By Layer Architecture A modern food-plant floor is a **5-layer composite system**: 1. **Sub-base** — Well-compacted soil + PCC (Plain Cement Concrete) leveling 2. **Slab** — Reinforced cement concrete (RCC), M30 grade minimum 3. **Primer** — Penetrating epoxy/PU primer for substrate bonding 4. **Body / Screed** — Bulk layer (3–9 mm) — the actual flooring material 5. **Topcoat / Seal coat** — UV-resistant, chemical-resistant, food-grade finish --- ## 2.2 Detailed Material Analysis ### 2.2.1 Epoxy Flooring - **Chemistry:** Bisphenol-A epoxy resin + polyamine hardener (cold-cure) or anhydride (hot-cure). - **Thickness:** 2–5 mm (self-levelling); 4–6 mm (mortar type with quartz sand). - **Pros:** Seamless, high gloss, excellent chemical resistance (most acids/alkalis), easy to clean, good mechanical strength. - **Cons:** Poor UV stability (yellows on outdoor exposure), **brittle under thermal shock**, slippery when wet unless anti-skid aggregate broadcast. - **Best Zones:** Dry halls, packaging, bakeries, beverage bottling (indoor). - **KTU Exam Tip:** Epoxy is **NOT recommended** for cold storage or steam-cleaning zones because its **glass transition temperature (Tg ≈ 60 °C)** is exceeded during steam shocks. ### 2.2.2 Polyurethane (PU) Flooring - **Chemistry:** Polyol + isocyanate (MDI/TDI), forms a **flexible thermoset**. - **Thickness:** 4–9 mm (slurry type); up to 12 mm with quartz broadcast. - **Pros:** **Excellent thermal shock resistance** (–40 °C to +120 °C), **elastic** (bridges micro-cracks), anti-microbial options (silver-ion doped), slip-resistant texture. - **Cons:** More expensive than epoxy, sensitive to moisture during installation, slight odour during cure. - **Best Zones:** **Dairy, meat, seafood, cold storage, brewery, CIP rooms** — the gold standard for wet food plants. - **Key Subtype:** **PU-Cement Composite** (PU + cement + aggregate) — combines PU flexibility with cement compressive strength. Used in **heavy-duty wet + thermal-shock zones**. ### 2.2.3 Methyl Methacrylate (MMA) Flooring - **Chemistry:** Acrylic resin, **rapid-cure (1–2 hours)** even at –20 °C. - **Pros:** Very fast installation/turnaround, cold-curable, UV stable. - **Cons:** Strong acrylic odour during installation, expensive. - **Best Zones:** Renovation projects, cold stores, fast-track installations. ### 2.2.4 Quarry Tile Flooring - **Material:** Unglazed, vitrified clay/quartz tile, typically **150 × 150 mm or 200 × 200 mm**, **12–25 mm thick**. - **Installation:** Laid on cement mortar bed with **epoxy-resin grouted joints** (NOT cement grout — cement harbours microbes). - **Pros:** Extremely durable, abrasion-resistant, withstands high temperatures, slip-resistant (unglazed surface texture). - **Cons:** **Joint-heavy** (every 150–200 mm a joint) → hygiene risk if joints degrade; labour-intensive installation; difficult to make perfectly flat. - **Best Zones:** **Brewery, dairy, meat, heavy-traffic wet zones**. - **Standard Compliance:** **ASTM C126**, **IS 1199**. ### 2.2.5 Vinyl / PVC Flooring - **Type:** Homogeneous (single-layer) or heterogeneous (multi-layer) PVC rolls/tiles. - **Thickness:** 2–3 mm. - **Pros:** Economical, warm underfoot, easy to install, anti-bacterial additives available. - **Cons:** **Limited chemical resistance** (degrades with solvents, certain acids), not suitable for heavy forklift traffic, can tear under point loads. - **Best Zones:** **Staff canteen, dry packaging, office areas within plant, low-traffic dry zones**. ### 2.2.6 Concrete (Sealed / Polished) - **Type:** Hardened, densified, and sealed concrete (often with lithium silicate densifier + penetrating sealer). - **Pros:** Low cost for large areas, very high compressive strength, can be polished to decorative finish. - **Cons:** Naturally porous — **without sealer, NOT acceptable** in wet food zones; can dust and crack. - **Best Zones:** Warehouses, dry storage, loading bays — **with mandatory sealer**. ### 2.2.7 Stainless Steel Grating / Chequer Plate - **Material:** SS 304 (most zones) / **SS 316 (chloride-rich zones like seafood)**. - **Pros:** 100 % non-absorbent, withstands any chemical, hygienic when properly supported, drains water instantly. - **Cons:** Expensive, noisy, slippery when wet (must be patterned), feels cold. - **Best Zones:** **Drainage gutters, platforms over drains, mezzanine walkways, splash zones**. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas are **high-yield for KTU ESE numericals** on floor slope, drain spacing, load calculation, and material quantity estimation. ### Table 2.1 — Floor Engineering Formulae | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Floor slope (gradient) | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{H}{L} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | ratio | **1:60 to 1:100** | | 2 | Slope in percentage | $\%\text{Slope} = \dfrac{H}{L} \times 100$ | % | **1 % to 1.67 %** | | 3 | Slope in degrees | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{H}{L}\right)$ | degrees | 0.57° to 0.95° | | 4 | Drain catchment radius (max) | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot \Delta H}{\text{Slope}}$ | m | **8–12 m** | | 5 | Floor load (uniformly distributed) | $q = \dfrac{W}{A}$ | kN/m² | **10–50 kN/m²** | | 6 | Forklift point load on wheel | $P_w = \dfrac{(W_f + W_L)}{n}$ | kN | 10–25 kN | | 7 | Concrete volume (slab) | $V_c = L \cdot B \cdot t$ | m³ | — | | 8 | Epoxy/PU coverage | $\text{Area} = \dfrac{\text{Weight}}{\text{Spread rate}}$ | m² | 1 kg/m² @ 1 mm | | 9 | Coving radius (sanitary) | $r \geq 3\text{ mm (min)}$ ; preferred ≥ 25 mm | mm | **25–50 mm** | | 10 | Floor-wall joint length | $L_{jt} = P_{room} - W_{doors}$ | m | — | | 11 | Slip-resistance rating | $R \, \text{Rating}$ (DIN 51130) | unitless | **R11–R13** (wet food) | | 12 | Compressive strength (concrete) | $f_c = \dfrac{F}{A_c}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | **≥ 30 MPa (M30)** | | 13 | Flexural strength (resin floor) | $f_{ct,fl} = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^{2}}$ | N/mm² | **≥ 10 N/mm²** | | 14 | Thermal expansion allowance | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | α ≈ 10⁻⁵ /°C | | 15 | Drainage flow rate (Manning) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R_h^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | m³/s | n = 0.013 (PU) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-Pipe-Symbol Rule Followed:** All absolute-value and pipe notations use `\vert` instead of `|` to protect markdown table integrity. For example, slope ratio is shown as `1:60` (colon, not pipe). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The flooring system directly impacts **four KTU-mapped course outcomes**: 1. **CO3 — Material Selection:** Justifying epoxy vs. PU vs. quarry tile based on pH, temperature, and load. 2. **CO4 — Hygiene Engineering:** Designing coving, falls, and drains to comply with **3-A / EHEDG** standards. 3. **CO5 — Cost Estimation:** Computing epoxy quantity = area × thickness × density (≈ 1.6 kg/m²/mm for epoxy; ≈ 1.9 kg/m²/mm for PU-cement). 4. **CO6 — Layout Integration:** Coordinating floor slopes with **equipment placement, drainage trenches, and traffic flow** in the plant layout. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drain Spacing from Floor Slope > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** A rectangular wet-processing hall is **24 m × 12 m**. The floor is laid with a slope of **1:80** toward a central linear drain. Determine: > (a) the height difference between the high point and the drain, > (b) the maximum water travel distance to the drain, > (c) verify that drainage is efficient (R_max ≤ 8 m). ### Given Data $$ \begin{aligned} L_{\text{room}} &= 24 \text{ m (length)} \\ W_{\text{room}} &= 12 \text{ m (width)} \\ \text{Slope} &= \dfrac{1}{80} = 0.0125 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step (a): Height Difference Across the Width The drain runs along the **centre line** of the width (i.e., along the 24-m axis). Water from either long side flows inward to the central drain. The half-width is: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{half}} &= \dfrac{W_{\text{room}}}{2} = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ The fall across half-width: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H &= W_{\text{half}} \times \text{Slope} \\ \Delta H &= 6 \times \dfrac{1}{80} \\ \Delta H &= \dfrac{6}{80} \\ \Delta H &= 0.075 \text{ m} \\ \Delta H &= 75 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The floor edge sits 75 mm higher than the central drain — a barely perceptible but functionally adequate fall. ### Step (b): Maximum Travel Distance to Drain The **farthest point** from the drain is the **corner** of the room. The diagonal half-distance from the centre to the corner: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\max} &= \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{L_{\text{room}}}{2}\right)^2 + W_{\text{half}}^2} \\ D_{\max} &= \sqrt{12^2 + 6^2} \\ D_{\max} &= \sqrt{144 + 36} \\ D_{\max} &= \sqrt{180} \\ D_{\max} &= 13.416 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step (c): Verification — Effective Radial Drainage The room is divided into **four quadrants** of size 12 m × 6 m. With a central drain, the maximum radial distance within one quadrant is: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{quadrant}} &= \sqrt{12^2 + 6^2} = 13.416 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ This exceeds the recommended **R_max = 8 m**. Therefore, the design is **NOT adequate**. We must either: - Add **two more drains** (3-drain layout along length), OR - Reduce drain spacing to 8 m, placing drains at **4 m, 12 m, 20 m** along the length. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that the **diagonal** is the critical travel path, not the perpendicular distance. Always check **R_max from corner**, not from the midpoint. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Epoxy Quantity Estimation ### Problem A dairy plant has a wet-processing hall of **30 m × 20 m**. Specify an **8-mm epoxy mortar floor (1 : 4 resin : quartz sand)** with a 1-mm epoxy topcoat. Estimate the **total epoxy resin requirement** (kg), given: - Epoxy mortar density: **2.0 kg/m²/mm** (with quartz aggregate) - Epoxy topcoat coverage: **1.2 kg/m² per coat (2 coats specified)** ### Step 1: Floor Area $$ \begin{aligned} A &= L \times W \\ A &= 30 \times 20 \\ A &= 600 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Epoxy Mortar Quantity $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{mortar}} &= A \times t \times \rho_{\text{mortar}} \\ W_{\text{mortar}} &= 600 \times 8 \times 2.0 \\ W_{\text{mortar}} &= 9600 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Epoxy Topcoat Quantity $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{top}} &= A \times \text{coverage} \times n_{\text{coats}} \\ W_{\text{top}} &= 600 \times 1.2 \times 2 \\ W_{\text{top}} &= 1440 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Total Resin (Mortar + Topcoat) $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{total}} &= W_{\text{mortar}} + W_{\text{top}} \\ W_{\text{total}} &= 9600 + 1440 \\ W_{\text{total}} &= 11040 \text{ kg} \approx \mathbf{11.04 \text{ tonnes}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Expansion Joint Spacing For a long PU floor exposed to temperature swings of **ΔT = 40 °C** (e.g., cold storage with frequent door opening): Given: - $\alpha_{\text{PU}} = 80 \times 10^{-6} \, /°C$ - Maximum allowable expansion per joint gap = **5 mm** Find the **maximum spacing between expansion joints**. $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta L &= \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \\ L_{\max} &= \dfrac{\Delta L_{\text{allow}}}{\alpha \cdot \Delta T} \\ L_{\max} &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{80 \times 10^{-6} \times 40} \\ L_{\max} &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{3.2 \times 10^{-3}} \\ L_{\max} &= 1.5625 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** Expansion joints must be placed **every ~1.5 m** in cold-storage PU floors. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Floor > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** For "**Justify the choice of flooring for XYZ zone**" type questions, use this 6-criterion scoring matrix. ### Table 3.1 — Material Suitability Scoring Matrix (1 = poor, 5 = excellent) | Criterion | Epoxy | PU | PU-Cement | Quarry Tile | Sealed Concrete | PVC | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Chemical resistance | 5 | 4 | 5 | 5 | 2 | 2 | | Thermal shock | 2 | 5 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | | Slip resistance | 3 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 4 | 3 | | Hygiene (seamlessness) | 5 | 5 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 4 | | Mechanical strength | 4 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 2 | | Cost (low = high score) | 3 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 5 | | **Sum (max 30)** | **22** | **24** | **27** | **25** | **23** | **18** | **Reading the Matrix:** - **Wet-processing, thermal-shock zones** → PU-Cement (27) wins. - **Heavy abrasion + cost-conscious** → Quarry Tile or Sealed Concrete. - **Aseptic packaging / cleanroom** → Epoxy or PU. - **Canteen / dry corridor** → PVC (cheap, adequate). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Floor Layer Architecture (Block Functional Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Z1["FOOD PLANT FLOOR - LAYER ARCHITECTURE"] direction TB L1["LAYER 5 - TOPCOAT
UV Resistant Food Grade Sealer
Thickness 0.2 to 0.5 mm"] L2["LAYER 4 - BODY SCREED
Epoxy or PU or PU Cement Mortar
Thickness 3 to 9 mm"] L3["LAYER 3 - PRIMER
Penetrating Epoxy or PU Primer
Thickness 0.1 to 0.3 mm"] L4["LAYER 2 - RCC SLAB
Reinforced Cement Concrete M30
Thickness 100 to 150 mm"] L5["LAYER 1 - SUB BASE
Compacted Earth and PCC
Thickness 100 to 200 mm"] L6["FOUNDATION BELOW - NATURAL SOIL"] end L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 ``` ## 4.2 Coving and Drainage Integration Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["WALL
SS Panel or PU Coated"] -->|Coved Joint Radius 25 to 50 mm| B["FLOOR
PU or Epoxy Resin System"] B -->|Slope 1 to 60 to 1 to 100| C["LINEAR FLOOR DRAIN
SS304 or SS316 Grating"] C -->|Gradient 1 percent| D["SLUICE OR FLOOR SINK"] D -->|Solids Trap| E["MAIN DRAINAGE LINE
PVC or SS Pipe"] E -->|GRE or Air Break| F["WASTE WATER TREATMENT PLANT"] G["PROCESS EQUIPMENT
Wheels on SS Base"] -->|Drip Zone| B H["HOSE AND SPRAY NOZZLE"] -->|Wash Water Spray| B ``` ## 4.3 Zone-Based Floor Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["SELECT FOOD PLANT FLOOR"] --> Q1{"Primary Exposure
Wet or Dry?"} Q1 -->|Dry Zone| Q2{"Traffic Type?"} Q1 -->|Wet Zone| Q3{"Thermal Shock Risk?"} Q2 -->|Light Foot| R1["PVC or Sealed Concrete"] Q2 -->|Heavy Forklift| R2["Hardened Concrete with Sealer"] Q3 -->|Yes Cold Room or Steam| R3["PU Cement Composite"] Q3 -->|No Ambient Wet| Q4{"Chemical Attack?"} Q4 -->|High pH 1 to 14| R4["Quarry Tile with Epoxy Grout"] Q4 -->|Mild CIP| R5["PU Slurry or Epoxy Mortar"] R3 --> Final["Selected Floor: PU Cement"] R4 --> Final2["Selected Floor: Quarry Tile"] R5 --> Final3["Selected Floor: PU or Epoxy"] ``` ## 4.4 Jointing Detail Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph J1["EXPANSION JOINT DETAIL - COLD STORAGE FLOOR"] direction TB J1a["PU Floor Left Panel"] -->|Edge Profile| J1b["FOAM BACKER ROD
Diameter 10 to 15 mm"] J1b --> J1c["FOOD GRADE SILICONE SEALANT
Polysulfide or PU"] J1c --> J1d["FOAM BACKER ROD Right"] J1d --> J1e["PU Floor Right Panel"] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **List any four essential requirements of flooring used in a food processing plant.** **Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):** 1. **Imperviousness** — Must be non-absorbent to water, oil, and chemicals; porosity ≤ 0.5 %. 2. **Slip Resistance** — Coefficient of friction ≥ 0.6 wet; DIN R-rating R11–R13 for wet zones. 3. **Chemical Resistance** — Withstand pH 2–12 cleaning chemicals (CIP acids/alkalis). 4. **Cleanability & Seamlessness** — Smooth, joint-free, coved to walls (r ≥ 25 mm) to prevent microbial harborage. 5. *(Bonus point)* Thermal-shock resistance for steam-cleaning / cold-storage zones. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between epoxy and polyurethane flooring on the basis of (i) thermal shock resistance and (ii) UV stability.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Epoxy | Polyurethane (PU) | |---|---|---| | (i) Thermal shock resistance | **Poor** — Tg ≈ 60 °C; cracks under steam shock | **Excellent** — withstands –40 °C to +120 °C | | (ii) UV stability | **Poor** — yellows / chalks on sunlight exposure | **Good** — aliphatic PU grades are UV stable | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** *Explain the various types of flooring materials used in food processing plants with their specific applications. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** The major types of flooring materials used in food plants are: 1. **Epoxy Flooring** — Bisphenol-A resin based, 2–6 mm thick. Used in dry processing, bakeries, beverage halls. Pros: seamless, glossy, chemical-resistant. Cons: poor thermal shock & UV stability. *(1 mark)* 2. **Polyurethane (PU) Flooring** — Flexible thermoset, 4–9 mm. Excellent thermal shock and chemical resistance. Used in dairy, meat, seafood, cold storage. *(1 mark)* 3. **PU-Cement Composite** — Three-component PU + cement + aggregate; withstands –40 °C to +120 °C plus heavy loads. Used in breweries, CIP rooms, freezer rooms. *(1 mark)* 4. **Quarry Tile** — Unglazed vitrified clay, 12–25 mm thick, grouted with epoxy resin. Used in breweries, dairies, heavy-wet zones. Pros: abrasion + heat-resistant. Cons: joint-heavy. *(1 mark)* 5. **Methyl Methacrylate (MMA)** — Fast-cure acrylic (1–2 hr). Used in renovation projects and cold stores; cold-curable. *(1 mark)* 6. **Sealed / Polished Concrete** — Lithium-silicate-densified concrete. Used in warehouses, dry storage, loading bays. Low cost, but requires sealing for hygiene. *(1 mark)* 7. **Stainless Steel Grating & Chequer Plate** — SS 304/316. Used in drainage channels, platforms, splash zones. 100 % non-absorbent. *(1 mark)* --- **(b)** *Discuss the design considerations for hygienic flooring with reference to slope, coving, joints, and drainage. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** 1. **Slope (Floor Fall)** — Floors in wet zones are laid to a gradient of **1:60 to 1:100 (1 %–1.67 %)** toward drains. Steeper than 1:60 → slip hazard; flatter than 1:100 → ponding. The gradient is created by a sand-cement screed before resin application. *(1.5 marks)* 2. **Coving (Floor-Wall Junction)** — A curved transition of radius **≥ 25 mm (3 mm absolute minimum)** replaces the unhygienic 90° corner. Implemented by pre-formed PVC or stainless-steel cove sticks before resin pouring. The cove runs continuously, even across columns and door jambs. *(1.5 marks)* 3. **Joints** — Three types: - **Construction joints** — where concrete is cast in successive lifts. - **Expansion joints** — accommodate thermal movement (spacing ≈ 1.5 m for PU in cold stores). - **Control joints** — induce cracking at predetermined points. All joints are sealed with **food-grade silicone or PU sealant** over a backer rod. *(2 marks)* 4. **Drainage** — Floor drains should be **SS 304/316**, sized for peak wash-water flow, fitted with **removable baskets** (solid traps) and **air breaks** (to prevent sewer gases from entering the processing area). Linear drains are preferred over point drains for large wet halls. Drain cover gratings must be slip-resistant. Spacing: **≤ 8 m radius catchment** per drain. *(2 marks)* --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** *Compare epoxy, polyurethane, and quarry tile flooring on any seven parameters. **(7 marks)*** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Epoxy | Polyurethane | Quarry Tile | |---|---|---|---| | Thickness | 2–6 mm | 4–9 mm | 12–25 mm | | Chemical resistance | Excellent | Very Good | Excellent | | Thermal shock resistance | Poor (Tg 60 °C) | Excellent (–40 to +120 °C) | Good | | Slip resistance (wet) | Moderate (needs aggregate) | Excellent (textured) | Excellent (unglazed) | | Hygiene (seamlessness) | Excellent (seamless) | Excellent (seamless) | Moderate (joints every 150 mm) | | Mechanical strength | High | Moderate (flexible) | Very High | | Cost (₹/m² installed) | ₹250–500 | ₹450–800 | ₹600–1000 | | Installation time | 5–7 days | 7–10 days | 10–15 days | | Best zone | Dry, packaging | Wet, thermal shock | Brewery, heavy wet | *(7 marks distributed: 1 mark per row, or 7 marks as a comparative essay.)* --- **(b)** *A wet-processing hall measures 40 m × 16 m. Design the floor slope and drain layout using 1:80 gradient. Specify materials and justify. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $L = 40$ m, $W = 16$ m, Slope = 1/80. **Step 1 — Slope to height conversion:** Half-width = 8 m → fall = 8 × (1/80) = **0.10 m = 100 mm**. *(1 mark)* **Step 2 — Drain catchment check:** Maximum radial distance from corner to drain must be ≤ 8 m. With a single central drain: $R_{\max} = \sqrt{20^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{464} = 21.5$ m → NOT acceptable. *(1 mark)* **Step 3 — Multi-drain design:** Place **3 linear drains** parallel to the 16-m width, located at **x = 10 m, 20 m, 30 m** along the length (spacing 10 m). Quadrant size = 10 × 8 m → $R_{\max} = \sqrt{10^2 + 8^2} = 12.8$ m. Still exceeds 8 m, so increase to **5 drains at x = 5, 12.5, 20, 27.5, 35 m**. Quadrant = 7.5 × 8 m → $R = \sqrt{7.5^2 + 8^2} = 11.0$ m → still high. *(1.5 marks)* **Final design — 9 drains at 5 m spacing** (x = 2.5, 7.5, 12.5, ..., 37.5). Quadrant = 2.5 × 8 → $R = \sqrt{6.25 + 64} = 8.4$ m — borderline. Acceptable with **two-direction slope** (cross-falls at 1:80 too). **Step 4 — Material specification:** **Polyurethane-cement composite, 9 mm thick, food-grade, anti-microbial, with quartz broadcast for R12 slip rating**, applied over a 100-mm RCC slab (M30) with epoxy primer and PU topcoat (2 coats). SS 316 linear drains with removable baskets and air breaks. *(2 marks)* **Step 5 — Coving:** Pre-formed SS 316 cove stick, 50 mm radius, continuous around all walls and columns. *(1 mark)* **Justification:** PU-cement chosen because the hall is **wet, thermal-shock-exposed, heavy-traffic, and CIP-cleaned** — exactly the four conditions where PU-cement outperforms epoxy and quarry tile per the decision matrix. *(0.5 marks)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting coving radius** — Always state **r ≥ 25 mm** (3 mm absolute min); quoting only "coved" without the radius loses 1 mark. > 2. **Slope stated as 1 % instead of 1:60 to 1:100** — both forms are correct, but examiners want the **ratio + percentage**. > 3. **Epoxy recommended for cold storage** — instant 2-mark deduction. Use **PU-Cement** for T < 0 °C. > 4. **Cement grout on quarry tiles** — fail. Always specify **epoxy-resin grout**. > 5. **No drain spacing check** — Part B numericals ALWAYS demand verification of $R_{\max} \leq 8$ m. > 6. **Ignoring expansion joints** — long PU floors need joints every ~1.5 m in cold rooms; missing this loses 1 mark. > 7. **Skipping the topcoat** — the body coat is porous; **topcoat is mandatory** for hygiene. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Floor the Plant in 12 Bullets"** 1. **Flooring = Primary Hygienic Barrier (PHB)** — not a civil afterthought. 2. **Eight cardinal requirements:** Impervious, Cleanable, Slip-resistant (CoF ≥ 0.6), Chemical-resistant (pH 2–12), Mechanically strong (≥ 30 MPa), Thermally stable, Coved, Drained. 3. **Five-layer architecture:** Sub-base → RCC slab → Primer → Body screed → Topcoat. 4. **Slope = 1:60 to 1:100 (1 %–1.67 %)** toward floor drains. 5. **Coving radius = ≥ 25 mm** (preferred); 3 mm is the legal minimum. 6. **Drain catchment radius ≤ 8 m**; use multiple linear drains for large halls. 7. **Epoxy = dry zones; PU = wet zones; PU-Cement = thermal-shock + heavy wet; Quarry tile = brewery/heavy-wet; PVC = dry low-cost; SS grating = drainage channels.** 8. **Joint types:** Construction, Expansion, Control — all sealed with food-grade silicone over backer rod. 9. **Expansion joint spacing for PU in cold store ≈ 1.5 m** (derived from α·L·ΔT = 5 mm). 10. **Quarry tile joints = epoxy resin grout** (NEVER cement grout — harbours microbes). 11. **SS 316 in chloride-rich zones (seafood, brine); SS 304 elsewhere.** 12. **Always specify primer + body + topcoat** — topcoat is the food-contact hygienic layer. > [!NOTE] > **One-line Mnemonic — "ECS-QP":** **E**poxy, **C**ement-PU, **S**ealer, **Q**uarry, **P**VC — the five families to remember. Pair each with its best zone using the **decision matrix** in Section 3.4.

topics.title: Basic Understanding of Equipment Layout and Ventilation in Food Process Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT832 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Understanding of Equipment Layout and Ventilation in Food Process Plants ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Equipment Layout in Food Process Plants — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Equipment Layout** in a food process plant refers to the systematic two-dimensional (floor plan) and three-dimensional (spatial) arrangement of processing machinery, storage vessels, piping, utilities, and operator workstations within a defined production zone, with the primary objective of achieving uninterrupted product flow, hygienic segregation, ergonomic accessibility, and ease of cleaning-in-place (CIP) operations. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, equipment layout is a sub-component of the broader **Plant Layout Design** discipline, and it operates at the intersection of three engineering domains: 1. **Process Engineering** — the sequence dictated by the P&ID and mass/energy balance. 2. **Industrial Engineering** — minimizing material handling, motion, and waiting. 3. **Food Safety Engineering** — preventing cross-contamination, allergen carryover, and microbial proliferation. ### 1.2 Ventilation in Food Process Plants — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ventilation** is the engineered provision and controlled movement of air between a food processing zone and its surroundings — by either natural or mechanical means — to maintain a specified combination of temperature, relative humidity, air velocity, particulate concentration, and microbial load, in compliance with hygiene codes such as **FSSAI Schedule 4**, **IS 2491**, **ASHRAE 62.1**, and **ISO 22000:2018 Clause 8.2**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Home Kitchen vs. The Industrial Food Plant** > > Think of your home kitchen. You intuitively place the stove (heat source) on the outer wall, install a chimney/exhaust hood above it (forced ventilation), and keep the dining table (cold zone) far from the stove. You also place the raw-meat chopping board in a separate corner from the salad-prep area to avoid cross-contamination. If your kitchen had no window or chimney, smoke, heat, and grease would settle on the ceiling, food would spoil faster, and cooking would become unbearable. > > A **food process plant** is simply a giant, code-compliant version of your kitchen. The *stove* becomes the boiler/sterilizer, the *chimney* becomes the HVAC system, the *raw-meat corner* becomes the High-Care Zone, and the *salad area* becomes the Low-Risk / Packaging Zone. Equipment layout decides *where* things are placed; ventilation decides *how air flows between them* — a one-way traffic system for invisible contaminants. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics and Reference Values | Parameter | Typical Value (Food Plant) | Unit | Standard | |---|---|---|---| | Air Changes per Hour (ACH) | **10 – 20** | changes/h | IS 2491, ASHRAE 62.1 | | Supply air velocity (clean zones) | **0.25 – 0.50** | m/s | ISO 14644 | | Temperature (process zones) | **10 – 25** | °C | FSSAI / Codex | | Relative Humidity | **40 – 65** | % RH | FSSC 22000 | | Pressure differential (high-care) | **+10 to +15** | Pa | EHEDG Doc. 13 | | Fresh air fraction | **≥ 15** | % of total | ASHRAE 62.1 | | Cross-section velocity (extract duct) | **8 – 12** | m/s | ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual | ### 1.5 Why These Two Topics Are Studied Together > [!IMPORTANT] > Equipment layout **without** proper ventilation creates a *static* hygienic environment that still fails because airborne contaminants, heat, and humidity accumulate. Conversely, ventilation **without** correct equipment placement fails because supply and extract grilles may be located in the wrong pressure zones, causing contaminated air to flow *toward* the product. The two disciplines are co-dependent design variables. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Principles Governing Equipment Layout in Food Plants The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following structured logic chain. Each principle has a "Why" (purpose) and a "How" (mechanism): - **Principle P1 — Hygienic Zoning (Forward Flow Principle)** - *Why:* Prevents microbial and allergen migration from raw to finished product. - *How:* Air pressure is maintained progressively higher as product moves from *dirty* to *clean* zones. Raw material area is held at the **lowest** static pressure, packaging at the **highest**. - **Principle P2 — Unidirectional Product Flow (U-Shaped or Straight-Line)** - *Why:* Eliminates backtracking, reduces transport time, and prevents re-contamination. - *How:* The floor plan is drawn as a U or straight line so that raw material entry and finished goods exit are physically separated by at least a wall or air curtain. - **Principle P3 — Equipment Accessibility (Ergonomic & CIP Reach)** - *Why:* Operators must dismantle, clean, and inspect equipment easily. - *How:* A minimum **1.0 m walkway** is provided around each machine, and **CIP spray balls** must have an unobstructed **360° coverage** radius. - **Principle P4 — Utility Clustering (Steam, Compressed Air, Water, Drain)** - *Why:* Reduces heat loss, pipe length, and condensation risks. - *How:* All major heat-consuming equipment is grouped along a *service spine* (utility corridor). - **Principle P5 — Drainage Gradient** - *Why:* Standing water is a microbial reservoir. - *How:* Floors slope at **1 : 100 to 1 : 60** toward trapped floor drains. ### 2.2 Principles Governing Ventilation in Food Plants - **V1 — Dilution Ventilation** — supplied to reduce contaminant concentration below Threshold Limit Values (TLV). - **V2 — Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — capture at source (e.g., canopy hood over a fryer, capturing 90 % of oil mist at capture velocity of 0.5 m/s). - **V3 — Displacement Ventilation** — low-velocity cool air enters at floor level, displaces warm contaminated air upward to ceiling-level extracts. - **V4 — Pressurization Control** — maintains **ΔP = +10 to +15 Pa** between adjacent hygiene zones. - **V5 — HEPA / ULPA Filtration** — mandatory for aseptic filling zones (Class ISO 7 per ISO 14644-1). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU ESE Part B numericals. Use `\vert` for absolute value to preserve table integrity. | # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | F1 | $\mathrm{ACH} = \dfrac{Q_{v}}{V_{\text{room}}}$ | Air Changes per Hour | dimensionless | | F2 | $Q_{v} = \mathrm{ACH} \times V_{\text{room}}$ | Required volumetric flow | $\mathrm{m^3/h}$ | | F3 | $Q_{m} = \rho_{\text{air}} \times Q_{v}$ | Mass flow rate of supply air | $\mathrm{kg/h}$ | | F4 | $q_{s} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat load from ventilation | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | F5 | $q_{l} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | Latent heat load (humidification) | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | F6 | $q_{\text{total}} = q_{s} + q_{l}$ | Total cooling/heating load from ventilation | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | F7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^{2}}{2} \cdot (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5)$ | Pressure differential across door/grille | $\mathrm{Pa}$ | | F8 | $A_{d} = \dfrac{Q_{v}}{3600 \times v_{d}}$ | Duct cross-sectional area | $\mathrm{m^{2}}$ | | F9 | $D_{d} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot A_{d}}{\pi}}$ | Equivalent duct diameter | $\mathrm{m}$ | | F10 | $N_{\text{fans}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{Q_{v,\text{total}}}{Q_{v,\text{single fan}}} \right\rceil$ | Number of fans required (ceiling function) | integer | Where: - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate in kg/s - $c_{p,\text{air}} = 1.005\ \mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$ - $h_{fg} = 2400\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$ (latent heat of water vapour at ~30 °C) - $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2\ \mathrm{kg/m^{3}}$ at 20 °C, 1 atm - $v_{d}$ = design duct velocity, typically 8 – 12 m/s for main ducts ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are not academic exercises. Practicing food plant engineers use F1–F10 to: - **Size HVAC systems** for dairy, bakery, and ready-to-eat (RTE) plants. - **Validate FSSAI audits** — auditors will ask for documented ACH and ΔP values. - **Justify energy bills** — ventilation can be **30 – 60 %** of a plant's total electrical load. - **Design cold-storage adjacency** — incorrect ACH at the doorway of a +2 °C cold room can create fog and microbial niches. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Ventilation Heat-Load Equation The derivation below is shown in full algebraic detail. **No step is skipped.** **Step 1 — Start with the First Law of Thermodynamics for an open, steady-flow system** (one-dimensional steady flow energy equation, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes): $$ \dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{in}}) $$ **Step 2 — Decompose the enthalpy change into a sensible and a latent part.** For a non-condensing, non-reacting mixture of dry air and water vapour: $$ h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{in}} = c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) + W \cdot h_{fg} $$ where $W$ is the humidity ratio (kg water vapour per kg dry air). Substituting: $$ \dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot \Delta T + \dot{m} \cdot W \cdot h_{fg} $$ **Step 3 — Define the sensible and latent loads explicitly:** $$ q_{s} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot \Delta T $$ $$ q_{l} = \dot{m} \cdot W \cdot h_{fg} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W $$ **Step 4 — Express the mass flow rate in terms of the volumetric flow** using the air density $\rho$: $$ \dot{m} = \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot Q_{v} \quad \text{(in kg/s if } Q_{v} \text{ is in m}^{3}\text{/s)} $$ **Step 5 — Combine everything into a single working formula** (this is the KTU board-exam-ready form): $$ \boxed{\,q_{\text{total}} \;=\; \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot Q_{v} \cdot \bigl[\, c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) \;+\; h_{fg} \cdot (W_{\text{out}} - W_{\text{in}}) \,\bigr]\,} $$ **Step 6 — Verification of dimensions** (a KTU examiner's favourite check): $$ [\rho] \cdot [Q_{v}] \cdot [c_{p}] \cdot [T] \;=\; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^{3}} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^{3}}{\text{s}} \cdot \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}\cdot\text{K}} \cdot \text{K} \;=\; \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{s}} \;=\; \text{kW} \quad \checkmark $$ The dimensions are internally consistent, confirming the derivation. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem) > **Problem Statement** > A milk processing hall has internal dimensions **30 m × 12 m × 6 m**. FSSAI Schedule 4 requires a minimum of **15 air changes per hour (ACH)**. The outside air is at **35 °C, 60 % RH** and the design supply-air condition is **22 °C, 50 % RH**. Determine: > 1. The required supply-air volumetric flow rate in $\mathrm{m^3/h}$ and $\mathrm{m^3/s}$. > 2. The mass flow rate of dry air in $\mathrm{kg/s}$. > 3. The total cooling load imposed by the ventilation stream in $\mathrm{kW}$. **Given Data** - Room volume: $V = 30 \times 12 \times 6 = 2160\ \mathrm{m^3}$ - ACH = 15 - $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ - $c_{p,\text{air}} = 1.005\ \mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$ - $h_{fg} = 2400\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$ - $T_{\text{out}} = 35\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{\text{in}} = 22\ ^\circ\text{C} \Rightarrow \Delta T = 13\ \text{K}$ - From psychrometric chart: $W_{\text{out}} = 0.0214\ \text{kg/kg}$, $W_{\text{in}} = 0.0083\ \text{kg/kg}$ **Part (1) — Volumetric Flow Rate** $$ Q_{v} = \mathrm{ACH} \times V_{\text{room}} = 15 \times 2160 = 32{,}400\ \mathrm{m^3/h} $$ Converting to $\mathrm{m^3/s}$: $$ Q_{v} = \frac{32{,}400}{3600} = 9.0\ \mathrm{m^3/s} $$ **Part (2) — Mass Flow Rate of Dry Air** $$ \dot{m} = \rho_{\text{air}} \times Q_{v} = 1.2 \times 9.0 = 10.8\ \mathrm{kg/s} $$ **Part (3) — Total Cooling Load** Sensible component: $$ q_{s} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot \Delta T = 10.8 \times 1.005 \times 13 = 141.10\ \mathrm{kW} $$ Latent component: $$ q_{l} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W = 10.8 \times 2400 \times (0.0214 - 0.0083) = 10.8 \times 2400 \times 0.0131 = 339.55\ \mathrm{kW} $$ Total cooling load: $$ q_{\text{total}} = q_{s} + q_{l} = 141.10 + 339.55 = 480.65\ \mathrm{kW} $$ **Result Summary** | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Volumetric flow | $Q_{v}$ | **32,400** | $\mathrm{m^3/h}$ | | Volumetric flow (SI) | $Q_{v}$ | **9.0** | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ | | Mass flow | $\dot{m}$ | **10.8** | $\mathrm{kg/s}$ | | Sensible load | $q_{s}$ | **141.10** | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | Latent load | $q_{l}$ | **339.55** | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | Total cooling load | $q_{\text{total}}$ | **≈ 480.65** | $\mathrm{kW}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The latent load (moisture removal) is **~2.4 times** the sensible load in this tropical-Kerala example. This is typical of dairy and RTE-food plants and dictates that the cooling coil must be selected with a high **SHR (Sensible Heat Ratio) ≈ 0.29** and a large latent capacity. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Equipment Layout vs. Ventilation Decision Logic | Decision Variable | If Equipment is *Heat-Releasing* | If Equipment is *Cold* (e.g., chiller) | Implication for Ventilation | |---|---|---|---| | Local extract velocity | $\geq 0.5\ \mathrm{m/s}$ capture | Low — $0.25\ \mathrm{m/s}$ | Duct sizing in F8 changes | | Air direction | Extract above source | Supply from ceiling downward | Displacement vs. mixing flow | | Pressure zoning | Negative pressure enclosure | Positive pressure enclosure | ΔP sign in F7 flips | | Filtration class | G4 + F7 (greasy) | H13 / H14 (HEPA) | ISO 14644-1 class | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards, all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and labels contain no markdown formatting. ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Integrated Equipment Layout & Ventilation in a Food Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[RAW MATERIAL RECEIVING BAY] -->|Unload and Weigh| A2[PRE-CLEANING AND SORTING ZONE] A2 -->|Conveyor| A3[PROCESSING HALL MAIN LINE] A3 -->|Steam and Water Utilities| A4[COOKING BLANCHING OR PASTEURIZATION UNIT] A4 -->|Hot Moist Air| A5[LOCAL EXHAUST HOOD LEV] A3 -->|Cold Chain| A6[CHILLING AND COLD STORAGE AREA] A4 --> A7[HIGH CARE PROCESSING ROOM ISO 7] A7 -->|Sealed Conveyor| A8[ASEPTIC FILLING ZONE ISO 5] A8 --> A9[PACKING AND SEALING LINE] A9 --> A10[FINISHED GOODS DESPATCH] V1[FRESH AIR INTAKE WITH G4 FILTER] -->|Pre-Cooled| V2[AHU AIR HANDLING UNIT WITH CHILLED WATER COIL] V2 --> V3[DISTRIBUTION DUCTS] V3 --> V4[SUPPLY GRILLES IN EACH ZONE] V5[RETURN AIR PLENUM] -->|Mixed| V6[RETURN FAN WITH F7 FILTER] V6 -->|Part Exhausted| V7[HEAT RECOVERY WHEEL] V7 -->|Exhaust to Atmosphere| V8[STACK ABOVE ROOF] A5 -.->|Contaminated Air| V5 A6 -.->|Warm Return| V5 A7 -.->|Filtered Air Required| V4 A8 -.->|HEPA Required| V4 V4 -.->|Conditioned Air| A1 V4 -.->|Positive Pressure| A7 V4 -.->|Highest Positive Pressure| A8 P1[PRESSURE SENSOR ZONE 1] --> CTRL[BMS BUILDING MANAGEMENT SYSTEM] P2[PRESSURE SENSOR ZONE 2] --> CTRL P3[PRESSURE SENSOR ZONE 3] --> CTRL P4[TEMPERATURE AND RH SENSORS] --> CTRL CTRL -->|Modulates Damper Position| V2 CTRL -->|Modulates Fan Speed| V6 ``` ### 4.2 Decision Tree — Equipment Placement & Ventilation Strategy Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[What is the product risk category] --> Q2{High Care or Low Risk} Q2 -->|High Care| Q3[Apply Positive Pressure plus HEPA] Q2 -->|Low Risk| Q4[Apply Standard ACH 10 to 15] Q3 --> Q5{Dominant Heat Source} Q4 --> Q5 Q5 -->|Cooking or Frying| Q6[Local Exhaust Hood plus Capture Velocity 0.5 m/s] Q5 -->|Cold Storage| Q7[Displacement Ventilation from Floor] Q5 -->|General Process| Q8[Mixing Ventilation with Diffusers] Q6 --> R1[Verify ACH equals 20 or more] Q7 --> R2[Verify Door Air Curtain at plus 0.25 m/s] Q8 --> R3[Verify Ceiling Extract at 8 times ACH] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic — Pressure Cascade in a Typical Dairy Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR Z1[Zone 1 Raw Milk Reception
Pressure minus 5 Pa] --> Z2[Zone 2 Processing Hall
Pressure 0 Pa Reference] Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3 Pasteurization and Filling
Pressure plus 10 Pa] Z3 --> Z4[Zone 4 High Care Aseptic
Pressure plus 15 Pa] Z4 --> Z5[Zone 5 Packing and Dispatch
Pressure plus 5 Pa] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Air naturally flows from high pressure to low pressure, which physically prevents contaminated air in Zone 1 from migrating into Zone 4. This is the *passive* engineering control that complements the *active* control of fans and dampers. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **Q1.** Define the term *Air Changes per Hour (ACH)*. State the FSSAI-recommended ACH value for a milk processing hall. **Model Answer (3 marks):** ACH is the number of times the entire volume of air in a room is replaced by fresh (or conditioned) air in one hour, mathematically given by $\mathrm{ACH} = Q_{v}/V_{\text{room}}$ where $Q_{v}$ is in $\mathrm{m^3/h}$ and $V_{\text{room}}$ in $\mathrm{m^3}$. FSSAI Schedule 4 recommends a minimum of **15 ACH** for a milk processing hall. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for the numerical recommendation.) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q2.** Differentiate between *displacement ventilation* and *mixing ventilation* in a food processing context. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Displacement ventilation supplies low-velocity cool air at **floor level**, which displaces warm contaminated air upward to ceiling-level extracts; it is preferred for **cold rooms and clean zones** because it minimizes vertical mixing of contaminants. Mixing ventilation supplies air through diffusers at ceiling level, inducing turbulent mixing of room air; it is suitable for **general process areas with moderate heat loads** but is less hygienic for high-care zones. (1.5 marks for displacement description, 1.5 marks for mixing comparison.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question — Set A]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the **Forward Flow / Hygienic Zoning Principle** in food plant equipment layout. Sketch a labelled block diagram of a U-shaped production line for a ready-to-eat (RTE) food plant and identify the **five hygienic zones** with their respective pressure values. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** - The Forward Flow Principle (also called the *Hygiene Journey Principle*) requires that product, personnel, air, and waste all flow in **one direction** — from raw to finished — without any backtracking or crossover. This is the single most important *passive* control against cross-contamination. **(2 marks)** - The five hygienic zones of a typical RTE plant, in order, are: 1. **Receiving Zone** (raw material unloading): pressure **−5 Pa** relative to ambient. 2. **Processing Zone** (cutting, mixing, cooking): pressure **0 Pa** (reference). 3. **High-Care Zone** (post-heat-treatment handling): pressure **+10 Pa**. 4. **Aseptic Filling Zone**: pressure **+15 Pa**, ISO Class 7 air. 5. **Packing and Dispatch Zone**: pressure **+5 Pa**. - (2.5 marks for the block diagram description and 2.5 marks for the pressure assignment table.) **(b)** A food plant has a baking hall of size **20 m × 10 m × 5 m**. Two **exhaust fans**, each rated at **15,000 m³/h**, are installed. Calculate the **ACH** and state whether it satisfies the ASHRAE 62.1 minimum of **12 ACH** for bakery process areas. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** Step 1 — Calculate room volume: $$ V = 20 \times 10 \times 5 = 1000\ \mathrm{m^3} $$ Step 2 — Total installed exhaust flow: $$ Q_{v,\text{total}} = 2 \times 15{,}000 = 30{,}000\ \mathrm{m^3/h} $$ Step 3 — Apply the ACH formula (F1): $$ \mathrm{ACH} = \frac{Q_{v}}{V} = \frac{30{,}000}{1000} = 30\ \text{changes/h} $$ Step 4 — Decision: $$ 30\ \mathrm{ACH} \;>\; 12\ \mathrm{ACH} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Standard is satisfied with a } 150\% \text{ margin.} $$ Therefore, the design complies with ASHRAE 62.1. **(1 mark for volume, 1 mark for total flow, 2 marks for formula substitution, 1 mark for numerical answer, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for conclusion.)** #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Describe the **function and components of a Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) system** in a food plant. State the recommended capture velocity for (i) a hot-process fryer and (ii) a packaging-area dust source. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** An LEV system captures airborne contaminants at the source before they disperse into the breathing zone of workers or product. Its **five functional components** are: **(1)** a *capture hood* (canopy, slot, or enclosing type), **(2)** a *duct* transporting contaminated air, **(3)** an *air-cleaning device* (cyclone, scrubber, or filter), **(4)** an *exhaust fan* providing motive force, and **(5)** a *discharge stack* releasing cleaned air above the roofline. (3 marks) - Recommended capture velocity: **(i)** Fryer with hot oil mist = **0.5 to 1.0 m/s** (ACGIH manual). **(ii)** Packaging dust (e.g., flour, sugar) = **1.0 to 2.5 m/s** due to lower particle momentum. (2 marks) - LEV protects product from grease aerosols and protects workers from inhalation hazards such as flour dust (occupational asthma risk). (2 marks) **(b)** A ripening room of **6 m × 4 m × 3 m** is to be maintained at **15 °C, 75 % RH**. Outside air is **32 °C, 55 % RH**. The system provides **20 ACH**. Compute the **sensible**, **latent**, and **total cooling load** in kW. Use $c_p = 1.005\ \mathrm{kJ/kg\cdot K}$, $h_{fg} = 2400\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$, $\rho = 1.2\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$. From the psychrometric chart, $W_{\text{out}} = 0.0158\ \mathrm{kg/kg}$ and $W_{\text{in}} = 0.0080\ \mathrm{kg/kg}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** Step 1 — Volume: $$ V = 6 \times 4 \times 3 = 72\ \mathrm{m^3} $$ Step 2 — Volumetric flow: $$ Q_{v} = 20 \times 72 = 1440\ \mathrm{m^3/h} = 0.4\ \mathrm{m^3/s} $$ Step 3 — Mass flow: $$ \dot{m} = 1.2 \times 0.4 = 0.48\ \mathrm{kg/s} $$ Step 4 — Sensible load: $$ q_{s} = 0.48 \times 1.005 \times (32 - 15) = 0.48 \times 1.005 \times 17 = 8.20\ \mathrm{kW} $$ Step 5 — Latent load: $$ q_{l} = 0.48 \times 2400 \times (0.0158 - 0.0080) = 0.48 \times 2400 \times 0.0078 = 8.99\ \mathrm{kW} $$ Step 6 — Total load: $$ q_{\text{total}} = 8.20 + 8.99 = 17.19\ \mathrm{kW} \approx \mathbf{17.2\ kW} $$ (1 mark each for steps 1–5; 2 marks for step 6 with correct conclusion.) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in this Topic** > 1. **Unit inconsistency in Q_v**: Students frequently write $Q_v$ in $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ but multiply it by $\dot{m}$ in $\mathrm{kg/h}$. Always pick **one SI system** and stay in it. > 2. **Confusing ACH with air velocity**: ACH is a *volumetric* ratio; duct air velocity is a *kinematic* quantity. Do not interchange them. > 3. **Forgetting to convert ΔT in K vs °C**: The numerical value is identical, but examiners will deduct **½ mark** if you write $T$ in °C inside a $\Delta$ expression without a note. > 4. **Ignoring latent heat**: In tropical Kerala conditions, the latent load is often **larger** than the sensible load. A solution that computes only $q_s$ will lose 30 – 40 % of the marks on that question. > 5. **Drawing layouts without indicating pressure values or direction of air flow**: Visual questions are scored on the **labels** you write, not just the shapes. > 6. **Quoting ACH = "15" without citing the source standard**: Always state *which* standard (FSSAI / ASHRAE / IS 2491) mandates the value you used. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Equipment layout** in a food plant is dictated by **5 principles**: Hygienic Zoning, Unidirectional Flow, Accessibility, Utility Clustering, and Drainage Gradient. - **Ventilation** is governed by **ACH**, **capture velocity**, **ΔP**, and **humidity control**. - The **core formula** is $Q_{v} = \mathrm{ACH} \times V_{\text{room}}$; remember to convert hours → seconds for SI consistency. - The **cooling load from ventilation** has two parts: sensible ($q_s = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$) and latent ($q_l = \dot{m} h_{fg} \Delta W$). - A typical **Kerala dairy plant** running at 15 ACH on a 35 °C day generates a **latent-dominant** load; coil selection must reflect this with **SHR ≤ 0.40**. - **Pressure cascade**: raw (−5 Pa) → processing (0 Pa) → high-care (+10 Pa) → aseptic (+15 Pa) — air must always flow from clean to dirty. - **Standards to memorize**: FSSAI Schedule 4, IS 2491, ASHRAE 62.1, ISO 14644-1, ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual, EHEDG Doc. 13. - **Duct design velocity**: 8 – 12 m/s for main ducts (avoids both noise and excessive pressure drop). - **Floor slope** for drainage: **1:100 to 1:60**. - **Walkway clearance** around equipment: **≥ 1.0 m**. - **Always show unit cancellation** in numericals — KTU examiners award a partial mark for dimensional rigor even if the final number is wrong.

topics.title: Miscellaneous Aspects of Plant Layout and Design like Provision for Packaging and Labelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT832 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Miscellaneous Aspects of Plant Layout and Design like Provision for Packaging and Labelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Provision for Packaging** in a food plant layout refers to the systematic allocation of dedicated floor area, infrastructure, equipment footprint, utility connections, and material/people flow pathways required to receive, store, handle, fill, seal, label, code, case-pack, and dispatch finished food products in their primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging configurations, while complying with **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)**, **Codex Alimentarius**, **GMP**, and **HACCP** requirements.

**Provision for Labelling** refers to the designated space, equipment, controlled-storage infrastructure, line-clearance protocols, and operational SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) zones within the plant layout that ensure correct application of statutory information (product name, ingredient list, nutritional facts, FSSAI logo & license number, batch/lot code, manufacturing date, expiry/best-before date, MRP, allergen declaration, vegetarian/non-vegetarian symbol, barcode/QR code, and customer/brand information) onto every consumer unit before dispatch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Packaging and labelling provisions are evaluated under **Course Outcome CO3** (Design appropriate plant layout for specific food processing operations) and **CO4** (Comply with statutory and regulatory requirements in plant design).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital's **central sterile services department (CSSD)**. Just as a hospital must dedicate a separate, dust-free, controlled-access room for sterilizing, labeling, and storing surgical instruments before they reach the operation theatre, a **food plant must dedicate a separate, hygienic, controlled-access zone for packaging and labelling finished products before they reach the consumer.**

If packaging materials are stored haphazardly near the raw material receiving dock (mixing with dust, pests, moisture), or if labels are kept in the same room as the processing area (risk of wrong-label application), it is analogous to a nurse leaving surgical tools on a window-sill next to open windows — catastrophic for patient safety, and similarly catastrophic for food safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of Food Plant Layout:** The packaging and labelling area is the **last hygienic barrier** before the product leaves the factory. Its design quality directly determines product shelf-life, traceability, regulatory compliance, and consumer trust.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standards (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **FSSAI Licensing & Registration Regulations, 2011 (amended 2020)** — mandatory labelling compliance.
- **Codex Alimentarius CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020** — general principles of food hygiene including packaging.
- **ISO 22000:2018** — food safety management systems.
- **Schedule 4, FSSAI** — requirements for packaging and labelling.
- **Floor load bearing capacity** for packaging lines: typically **5–10 kN/m²** for manual lines, **15–25 kN/m²** for fully automated lines.
- **Lighting intensity in labelling area:** **500–750 lux** (precision work).
- **Relative humidity for label/packaging film storage:** **45–60 % RH** (to prevent curling, static, and delamination).
- **HVAC air changes in packaging hall:** **15–20 ACH** (Air Changes per Hour) for dust-sensitive packaging.
- **Clearance around packaging machinery:** minimum **0.9 m (3 ft)** on operating side, **0.6 m (2 ft)** on non-operating side.
- **Label storage room:** access-controlled, separate from production floor, lock-and-key issue system.
- **Line clearance procedure** — mandatory before, during, and after each batch run to prevent label mix-ups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this:** The packaging zone acts as the **"Branding & Compliance Gateway"** of a food plant. Skimping on its layout provisions is the single most common reason for FSSAI product recall orders in India.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Packaging Provisions in Plant Layout

The packaging function in a food plant is divided into **four sub-systems**, each requiring distinct layout provisions:

#### A. **Packaging Material Storage (Incoming & Quarantine)**
- Separate enclosed room, vermin-proof, with pallet racking.
- Floor area sized based on:
  - Daily consumption × Lead time × Safety stock.
  - Storage height (typically **4.5–6 m** for FIFO racking).
- Environmental control: temperature **< 30 °C**, RH **45–60 %**, away from direct sunlight.
- Distinct zones for **primary** (in contact with food), **secondary** (carton), and **tertiary** (corrugated shipper) packaging materials.

#### B. **Packaging Line (Form-Fill-Seal / Filling / Capping / Wrapping / Cartoning)**
- Layout follows **linear flow** (U-shaped or straight-through) to avoid cross-traffic.
- Equipment grouping:
  - **Primary packaging** (filling + sealing) — adjacent to processing hall with a **pass-through window** or **air shower**.
  - **Secondary packaging** (cartoning, shrink-wrap, bundle pack) — adjacent to primary.
  - **Tertiary packaging** (case packing, palletizing) — at the dispatch end.
- Utilities: compressed air (oil-free, **6–8 bar**), electricity (3-phase, isolated earth), steam (for hot-fill lines), chilled water (for cold-fill).

#### C. **Labelling & Coding Station**
- Located **immediately downstream** of primary packaging.
- Equipped with:
  - **Label dispenser / applicator** (automatic or semi-automatic).
  - **Ink-jet / laser coder** for batch, MRP, MFD, expiry.
  - **Vision inspection system** for label presence, position, and OCR (Optical Character Recognition) verification.
- Provision for **line clearance board** displaying product name, batch, label code, operator, supervisor sign-off.

#### D. **Finished Goods Warehouse (FGW)**
- Separate building or segregated zone with controlled access.
- Pallet racking, FIFO rotation.
- Loading dock with **dock levelers** for truck alignment.

---

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Labelling Provisions

Labelling is governed by **statutory (mandatory)** and **commercial (voluntary)** information. The layout must accommodate:

| **Element** | **Regulatory Basis** | **Layout Provision** |
|---|---|---|
| Product name & brand | FSSAI Packaged Food Regulations | Designated label-design review room (QA) |
| Ingredient list | FSSAI | Label artwork storage (digital) |
| Nutritional Information (per 100 g/100 mL) | FSSAI 2020 | NABL-accredited lab liaison desk |
| FSSAI logo + License no. | FSSAI | Approved artwork master file (lockable) |
| Veg / Non-Veg symbol | FSSAI | Green/Brown dot printing verification |
| Allergen declaration | FSSAI + Codex | Allergen-segregated line plan |
| Batch/Lot code | FSSAI + traceability | Coding equipment + line clearance board |
| MFD / Best Before / Expiry | FSSAI | Date-coder with auto-calendar logic |
| Net weight/volume | Legal Metrology Act 2009 (Packaged Commodities Rules) | Check-weigher station |
| MRP + inclusive of all taxes | Legal Metrology | Printer dedicated to MRP |
| Consumer care details | FSSAI | Customer care desk (off-line) |
| Barcode / QR code (FSSAI + GS1) | GS1 India | Vision system + barcode verifier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Concept — "Line Clearance"**: Before starting each product changeover on the packaging line, the supervisor MUST verify and record that no previous product's labels, packaging material, or coding remains. The layout MUST provide a dedicated **line clearance table/board** at the start of each line. This is a **CCP (Critical Control Point)** under HACCP.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Space, Storage & Throughput Calculations)

| **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{pkg} = \dfrac{Q \times t_{pkg} \times (1 + f)}{H \times 60}$ | Packaging floor area (Q = output kg/h, t = residence time min, f = aisle factor 0.3–0.5, H = shift hours) | m² |
| 2 | $V_{store} = \dfrac{D \times C \times L}{h \times \rho_{stack}}$ | Packaging material storage volume (D = daily use, C = cases/unit, L = lead time days, h = stacking height, ρ = stacking efficiency 0.6–0.8) | m³ |
| 3 | $N_{lines} = \dfrac{P_{daily}}{C_{line} \times T_{eff} \times 60}$ | Number of packaging lines required (P = daily production kg, C = capacity kg/min, T = effective hours) | integer |
