| 4 | $L_{line} = \sum L_i + (n-1) \times d_{op}$ | Total packaging line length (L_i = each machine length, n = machines, d_op = operator distance 1.2 m) | m |
| 5 | $Q_{room} = V \times ACH / 60$ | HVAC airflow for packaging hall (V = room volume m³, ACH = air changes/h) | m³/min |
| 6 | $L_{label} = Q_{prod} \times \dfrac{3600}{v_{line}}$ | Label consumption rate (Q_prod = units/h, v = line speed units/h, 3600 = s/h) | labels/h |
| 7 | $T_{down} = \dfrac{D_{setup} + D_{clear}}{T_{run}}$ | Line clearance downtime ratio (D_setup, D_clear in min, T_run in min) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{comp} = \dfrac{N_{m/c} \times P_{unit}}{\eta_{motor}}$ | Compressor/utility power (N = machines, P = unit kW, η = efficiency 0.85–0.92) | kW |
| 9 | $f_{label-reject} = \dfrac{N_{reject}}{N_{total}}$ | Label rejection fraction (typical acceptable < 0.005) | fraction |
| 10 | $C_{trace} = t_{recall} \times \text{coverage} / t_{legal}$ | Traceability compliance index (t in h; must be < 1 for compliance) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions for KTU Problems:**
> - Shift duration **H = 8 h** (single shift) unless otherwise stated.
> - **f (aisle/clearance factor)** = **0.3** for compact layout, **0.5** for spacious GMP layout.
> - **ρ_stack (stacking efficiency)** = **0.65** for cartons, **0.80** for rigid containers.
> - **ACH** = **15** for packaging hall, **20** for label storage room, **10** for finished goods warehouse.
> - All clearances as per **IS 2379:1990 (Packaging Code)** and **Schedule 4, FSSAI**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical parallel:** The "line clearance" concept is borrowed directly from pharma — the same FSSAI Schedule 4 rule mirrors US FDA 21 CFR Part 211.
- **Dairy plant example:** A 1,00,000 L/day dairy needs separate cold-chain packaging hall with **10 °C** ambient, washable epoxy floor (PU coving at wall-floor junction), and stainless-steel conveyor with **1.5 m** elevation above floor.
- **Snack food example:** A potato chips plant requires **nitrogen flushing + metal detection + weigh-fill + seal + cooling + cartoning + coding + case-packing** — 8 stations, total line length ~ **25–30 m**.
- **Beverage example:** A PET bottle line requires **blow-moulder → filler → capper → labeller → shrink-wrapper → palletizer** — total footprint ~ **40–60 m × 6–8 m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Judgment:** Always reserve **20 % spare area** in the packaging hall for future capacity expansion. This is a mandatory KTU-recommended practice and aligns with **Lopez Principle of Plant Design (1976)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative 1: Packaging Floor Area Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A breakfast cereal plant produces **6,000 kg/day** of packaged product. Each package takes on average **45 seconds** on the packaging line (residence time). The plant operates **1 shift of 8 hours**. Aisle/clearance factor = **0.4**. Calculate the **packaging floor area required**.

#### Step 1: Compute the hourly throughput $Q$

$$Q = \dfrac{\text{Daily output}}{\text{Shift hours}} = \dfrac{6{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}{8 \text{ h/day}} = 750 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Logic:** Daily production must be distributed across the shift hours to know the hourly material flow rate.

#### Step 2: Convert residence time $t_{pkg}$ to consistent units

$$t_{pkg} = 45 \text{ s/package} = \dfrac{45}{60} \text{ min/package} = 0.75 \text{ min/package}$$

**Logic:** Residence time is given in seconds; we convert to minutes to align with the formula's minute-based convention.

#### Step 3: Calculate instantaneous WIP (Work-In-Progress) on the line

$$WIP = Q \times t_{pkg} = 750 \text{ kg/h} \times 0.75 \text{ min/package}$$

To combine, convert $Q$ to kg/min:

$$Q = \dfrac{750}{60} = 12.5 \text{ kg/min}$$

$$WIP = 12.5 \text{ kg/min} \times 0.75 \text{ min/package} = 9.375 \text{ kg on line}$$

#### Step 4: Assume specific floor area per kg WIP based on equipment density

For a cereal packaging line, a typical specific area is **$a_{spec} = 0.8 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$** of WIP (this includes conveyor, fillers, sealers, labellers, and operator working space). Therefore:

$$A_{gross} = WIP \times a_{spec} = 9.375 \times 0.8 = 7.5 \text{ m}^2$$

#### Step 5: Apply aisle/clearance factor $f$ to get total packaging floor area

$$A_{pkg} = A_{gross} \times (1 + f) = 7.5 \times (1 + 0.4)$$

$$A_{pkg} = 7.5 \times 1.4 = 10.5 \text{ m}^2$$

#### Step 6: Add the **20 % spare area for future expansion** (KTU recommendation)

$$A_{final} = A_{pkg} \times 1.20 = 10.5 \times 1.20 = 12.6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_{final} \approx 12.6 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (rounded to 13 m}^2\text{)}}$$

**Logic Summary:** We translated throughput → WIP on line → footprint using specific area factor → gross area → net floor area with aisle factor → plus contingency for future growth. This is a **closed-form analytical method** commonly tested in KTU.

---

### 3.2 Derivative 2: Number of Packaging Lines Required

**Problem Statement:** A fruit juice bottling plant must produce **2,00,000 bottles/day** (each 200 mL). Each packaging line has rated capacity **120 bottles/min**. Effective running time per shift = **7 hours** (out of 8, allowing 1 h for changeovers and cleaning). The plant runs **2 shifts**. Calculate the **number of packaging lines** required.

#### Step 1: Daily production target $P_{daily}$

$$P_{daily} = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ bottles/day}$$

#### Step 2: Effective running time per day $T_{eff,day}$

$$T_{eff,day} = 2 \text{ shifts} \times 7 \text{ h/shift} = 14 \text{ h/day}$$

#### Step 3: Convert $T_{eff,day}$ to minutes

$$T_{eff,day} = 14 \text{ h/day} \times 60 \text{ min/h} = 840 \text{ min/day}$$

#### Step 4: Compute total capacity needed per minute

$$\text{Required rate} = \dfrac{P_{daily}}{T_{eff,day}} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{840} = 238.10 \text{ bottles/min}$$

#### Step 5: Compute the number of lines

$$N_{lines} = \dfrac{\text{Required rate}}{\text{Capacity per line}} = \dfrac{238.10}{120} = 1.984$$

#### Step 6: Round UP to next integer (you cannot have 0.984 of a line!)

$$\boxed{N_{lines} = 2 \text{ lines (mandatory)}}$$

#### Step 7: Calculate the utilization factor for completeness

$$\eta_{util} = \dfrac{238.10}{2 \times 120} = \dfrac{238.10}{240} = 0.992 \text{ or } 99.2\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A utilization of **99.2 %** is **too high** for a real plant (no margin for breakdowns, changeovers, CIP). The KTU-acceptable target is **70–85 %**. Therefore, the practical recommendation would be to install a **third line** or upgrade capacity to **150 bottles/min** per line. This is a common valuation check.

---

### 3.3 Derivative 3: Total Length of a Packaging Line

**Problem Statement:** A snack food plant has a packaging line with the following machines in sequence: **Weigh-filler (1.8 m) → Metal detector (0.9 m) → Nitrogen-flush sealer (2.4 m) → Cooling tunnel (3.6 m) → Cartoner (2.1 m) → Labeller (1.5 m) → Check-weigher (0.6 m) → Case packer (2.4 m)**. There are **8 machines** with operator access between each. Each operator gap $d_{op} = 1.2 \text{ m}$. Compute total line length.

#### Step 1: Sum of machine lengths

$$L_{machines} = 1.8 + 0.9 + 2.4 + 3.6 + 2.1 + 1.5 + 0.6 + 2.4$$

$$L_{machines} = 15.3 \text{ m}$$

#### Step 2: Number of gaps = $n - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7$ gaps

#### Step 3: Total gap length

$$L_{gaps} = 7 \times 1.2 = 8.4 \text{ m}$$

#### Step 4: Total line length

$$L_{line} = L_{machines} + L_{gaps} = 15.3 + 8.4$$

$$\boxed{L_{line} = 23.7 \text{ m}}$$

#### Step 5: Add end-clearances (1.5 m feed-end, 1.5 m discharge-end)

$$L_{final} = 23.7 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 26.7 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** End clearance on operating side ≥ 1.2 m; on non-operating side ≥ 0.9 m. Always include these in the final layout drawing.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Packaging Area Configurations

| **Parameter** | **Manual Packaging** | **Semi-Automatic** | **Fully Automatic** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific area $a_{spec}$ | **2.0 m²/kg WIP** | **1.0 m²/kg WIP** | **0.5 m²/kg WIP** |
| Manpower per line | 8–12 | 3–5 | 1–2 |
| HVAC ACH | 10 | 15 | 20 |
| Lighting (lux) | 300 | 500 | 750 |
| Floor load (kN/m²) | 5 | 10 | 20 |
| Investment (₹/line) | Low | Medium | High |
| Hygiene risk | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| KTU suitability | Small units | MSMEs | Large industries |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Flow of Packaging & Labelling Zone in Food Plant Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Raw Material Receiving Dock]) --> A1[Quarantine Storage]
    A1 --> A2[Approved Raw Material Store]
    A2 --> B1[Processing Hall - Classified Zone]
    B1 --> B2[In-Process Quality Check]
    B2 --> C0[Pass-Through Airlock Window]
    C0 --> C1[Primary Packaging - Form Fill Seal]
    C1 --> C2[Metal Detector / X-Ray Inspection]
    C2 --> C3[Check Weigher]
    C3 --> D1[Labelling Station - FSSAI Compliance]
    D1 --> D2[Vision Inspection System]
    D2 --> D3[Batch and Date Coding Station]
    D3 --> E1[Secondary Packaging - Cartoning]
    E1 --> E2[Tertiary Packaging - Case Packing]
    E2 --> E3[Palletizing]
    E3 --> F1[Finished Goods Warehouse - FIFO]
    F1 --> F2[Dispatch Dock with Dock Leveler]

    G1[Packaging Material Store - Primary] --> C1
    G2[Packaging Material Store - Secondary] --> E1
    G3[Packaging Material Store - Tertiary] --> E2
    H1[Label Store - Lockable Controlled Access] --> D1
    H2[Ink and Adhesive Store - Flammable Cabinet] --> D1
    H3[Coding Ink Store - Separate Zone] --> D3

    I1[QA Lab - On Site] -. NABL Testing .-> D1
    I2[QA Document Cell] -. Line Clearance SOP .-> D1
    I3[Maintenance Workshop] -. Spare Parts .-> C1
    I4[Wash and Change Room - Personnel] -. Hygiene .-> B1

    J1[HVAC Plant - 15 to 20 ACH] -. Filtered Air .-> C1
    J1 -. Filtered Air .-> D1
    J2[Compressed Air - Oil Free 6 to 8 Bar] -. Utility .-> C1
    J3[Steam - Hot Fill Lines] -. Utility .-> C1
    J4[Chilled Water - Cold Fill] -. Utility .-> C1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sequence of Label Application & Coding SOP (HACCP CCP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1([Batch Start Signal from Production Planning]) --> K2[Supervisor Line Clearance Check]
    K2 --> K3{Previous Labels and Material Removed?}
    K3 -- No --> K4[STOP - Initiate Clearance Procedure]
    K4 --> K2
    K3 -- Yes --> K5[Issue Approved Labels from Locked Store]
    K5 --> K6[Verify Label Specs vs Batch Sheet]
    K6 --> K7{Allergen and FSSAI Compliance OK?}
    K7 -- No --> K8[Reject - Quarantine Label Batch]
    K7 -- Yes --> K9[Load Labels on Applicator]
    K9 --> K10[Start Packaging and Labelling Run]
    K10 --> K11[Continuous Vision Inspection]
    K11 --> L1{OCR Read Success Rate above 99 percent?}
    L1 -- No --> L2[Auto Reject to Re-Work Station]
    L2 --> K10
    L1 -- Yes --> L3[Proceed to Coding Station]
    L3 --> L4[Apply Batch Lot MFD Expiry MRP]
    L4 --> L5{Date Code Verified by Vision System?}
    L5 -- No --> L6[Manual Re-Verification by QA]
    L5 -- Yes --> L7[Proceed to Secondary Packaging]
    L7 --> M1[End of Run - Reconciliation]
    M1 --> M2[Destroy Unused Labels - Documented]
    M2 --> M3[Line Clearance for Next Product]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Layout Schematic: Plan View of Packaging Hall (Not-to-Scale)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hall_Packaging[PACKAGING HALL - Classified Zone]
        direction LR
        Z1[Entry - Air Shower - Personnel] --> Z2[Packing Material Issue Counter]
        Z2 --> Z3[Primary Line 1 - FFS]
        Z2 --> Z4[Primary Line 2 - FFS]
        Z3 --> Z5[Labelling and Coding Station 1]
        Z4 --> Z6[Labelling and Coding Station 2]
        Z5 --> Z7[Check Weigher and Metal Detector]
        Z6 --> Z7
        Z7 --> Z8[Secondary Cartoning Zone]
        Z8 --> Z9[Case Packing and Palletizing]
        Z9 --> Z10[Exit to Finished Goods Warehouse]
    end

    subgraph Store_Rooms[ADJACENT SUPPORT ROOMS]
        direction TB
        S1[Primary Packaging Material Store]
        S2[Secondary Packaging Material Store]
        S3[Tertiary Packaging Material Store]
        S4[Label Storage - Lockable]
        S5[Ink and Adhesive Store - Flammable Cabinet]
        S6[QA Lab and Document Cell]
        S7[HVAC Plant Room]
        S8[Compressed Air Plant Room]
    end

    S1 -. Feeds .-> Z3
    S1 -. Feeds .-> Z4
    S2 -. Feeds .-> Z8
    S3 -. Feeds .-> Z9
    S4 -. Feeds .-> Z5
    S4 -. Feeds .-> Z6
    S5 -. Feeds .-> Z5
    S5 -. Feeds .-> Z6
    S6 -. Monitors .-> Hall_Packaging
    S7 -. Supplies Air .-> Hall_Packaging
    S8 -. Supplies Air .-> Hall_Packaging
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Layout Rule:** All support stores must be **adjacent but external** to the packaging hall. They communicate through **dedicated hatches or pass-boxes** (with UV sterilization if needed for primary packs). Direct door access between stores and processing area is **not permitted** in GMP layout — this is a frequent viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three provisions that must be made in a food plant layout for the labelling section. Briefly state the regulatory basis for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Dedicated, lockable label storage room** — to prevent mix-ups, pilferage, and unauthorized access. **Regulatory basis:** FSSAI Schedule 4, Clause 5.2 (Labelling) + Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020).

2. **Vision inspection system with OCR verification** — to verify that every label carries correct batch code, MFD, expiry, MRP, and FSSAI logo before secondary packaging. **Regulatory basis:** FSSAI Packaging & Labelling Regulations 2011 (amended 2020) + ISO 22000:2018 Clause 8.5 (Hazard control).

3. **Line clearance SOP board** — visible display at start of each line showing product, batch, label spec code, operator, supervisor sign-off, ensuring GMP traceability. **Regulatory basis:** Schedule 4 of FSSAI + WHO GMP for food.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State any three environmental conditions that must be maintained in the packaging and labelling zone of a food plant. Justify each with one sentence.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Relative humidity 45–60 % RH** — to prevent packaging film curl, label delamination, and electrostatic misreads on coding equipment.

2. **Temperature ≤ 25 °C** (or 10 °C for cold-chain products like dairy/meat) — to prevent heat distortion of plastic packaging and to maintain product integrity before sealing.

3. **Lighting intensity 500–750 lux** — to enable accurate manual label inspection, correct application, and clear reading of date codes by both operators and vision systems.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choose ONE out of TWO, 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **layout provisions required for packaging of finished products** in a food processing plant. Cover primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging zones, with reference to material flow, utilities, and statutory compliance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bakery plant produces **12,000 loaves/day**. Each loaf is packaged in a primary film wrapper (residence time on line = 30 s/loaf). The plant operates **1 shift of 8 h**, with aisle factor **f = 0.4** and specific area **a_spec = 1.0 m²/kg WIP**. Average loaf weight = **400 g**. Calculate: **(i)** Hourly throughput, **(ii)** WIP on the line in kg, **(iii)** Net packaging floor area, and **(iv)** Final floor area after adding 20 % spare. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Layout Provisions for Packaging of Finished Products:**

| **#** | **Provision** | **Description / Justification** | **Marks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary packaging zone** | Form-fill-seal equipment for direct food contact packaging. Located adjacent to processing hall, connected via pass-through airlock/window to maintain hygiene zoning. Food-grade materials only (FSSAI approved). | 1 |
| 2 | **Secondary packaging zone** | Cartoning, shrink-wrapping, bundle packing. Uses printed cartons with brand, ingredient, and nutritional info. Equipped with labellers, vision systems. | 1 |
| 3 | **Tertiary packaging zone** | Case packing with corrugated shippers, palletizing using stretch wrap. Connects to dispatch dock. Designed for forklift/pallet truck movement. | 1 |
| 4 | **Material flow design** | Linear or U-shaped flow: raw → primary → secondary → tertiary → FG warehouse. No backflow. Unidirectional to prevent cross-contamination. | 1 |
| 5 | **Utility provisions** | Compressed air (oil-free, 6–8 bar), 3-phase electricity with isolated earth, HVAC (15–20 ACH), steam for hot-fill, chilled water for cold-fill, LED lighting (500–750 lux). | 1 |
| 6 | **Storage provisions** | Separate stores for primary, secondary, tertiary materials. Climate-controlled (RH 45–60 %, T < 30 °C). Vermin-proof. FIFO racking. | 1 |
| 7 | **Statutory compliance provisions** | FSSAI Schedule 4, Legal Metrology Act 2009, Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969, ISO 22000:2018. Includes HACCP plan, line clearance SOP, traceability records. | 1 |

**Conclusion (1 sentence):** A well-designed packaging layout integrates **hygiene zoning, statutory compliance, ergonomic material flow, and contingency for future expansion**, ensuring both product safety and operational efficiency.

---

##### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $P_{daily} = 12{,}000 \text{ loaves/day}$
- $t_{pkg} = 30 \text{ s/loaf} = 0.5 \text{ min/loaf}$
- $H = 8 \text{ h/shift}$
- $f = 0.4$
- $a_{spec} = 1.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg WIP}$
- Loaf weight = $0.4 \text{ kg/loaf}$

**(i) Hourly throughput $Q$:**

$$Q = \dfrac{12{,}000 \text{ loaves}}{8 \text{ h}} = 1{,}500 \text{ loaves/h}$$

$$Q_{mass} = 1{,}500 \times 0.4 = 600 \text{ kg/h}$$

**(ii) WIP on the line in kg:**

$$Q_{per\,min} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{60} = 25 \text{ loaves/min} = 10 \text{ kg/min}$$

$$WIP = 10 \text{ kg/min} \times 0.5 \text{ min} = 5 \text{ kg on line}$$

**(iii) Net packaging floor area:**

$$A_{gross} = WIP \times a_{spec} = 5 \times 1.0 = 5 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{net} = A_{gross} \times (1 + f) = 5 \times 1.4 = 7 \text{ m}^2$$

**(iv) Final floor area with 20 % spare:**

$$A_{final} = 7 \times 1.20 = 8.4 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_{final} = 8.4 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (rounded to 9 m}^2\text{ for practical design)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Discuss the **statutory requirements for labelling of packaged food products in India** as per FSSAI. Briefly describe the layout provisions for the labelling and coding station in a food plant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fruit juice bottling plant requires **3,00,000 bottles/day** of 200 mL each. Each packaging line has capacity **150 bottles/min**, effective running time per shift = **7 h**. Plant runs **2 shifts/day**. Calculate: **(i)** Required production rate in bottles/min, **(ii)** Number of lines required, **(iii)** Utilization factor, and **(iv)** Comment on whether utilization is acceptable; if not, suggest a corrective action. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Statutory Labelling Requirements as per FSSAI (India):**

1. **Product Name** — must be specific, not generic (e.g., "Orange Juice" not "Juice").
2. **Ingredient List** — in descending order of composition, including additives with class names.
3. **Nutritional Information** — energy (kcal), protein, carbohydrate, fat, sugar, sodium per 100 g or 100 mL (mandatory since 2020 amendment).
4. **FSSAI Logo + License/Registration Number** — green/red logo with 14-digit license number.
5. **Veg / Non-Veg Symbol** — green dot (veg) or brown triangle (non-veg).
6. **Allergen Declaration** — for any of the 8 major allergens (milk, soy, gluten, etc.).
7. **Net Quantity** — by weight or volume, as per Legal Metrology Act 2009.
8. **MRP + inclusive of all taxes** — as per Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules 2011.
9. **Date Marking** — MFD, Use By / Best Before date.
10. **Batch/Lot Code** — for traceability and recall.
11. **Manufacturer/Packer Name & Address** — with FSSAI license.
12. **Customer Care Details** — phone/email/website.
13. **Barcode** — GS1 standard.
14. **Country of Origin** — "Product of India" or "Made in India" (as applicable).

**Layout Provisions for Labelling & Coding Station:**

- Located **immediately downstream of primary packaging**.
- Equipped with **automatic label applicator/dispenser** + **ink-jet or laser coder**.
- **Vision inspection system** with OCR for label presence, position, and code readability.
- **Check-weigher** for net quantity verification.
- **Line clearance board** at start of line.
- **Locked, controlled-access label store** adjacent to labelling area.
- **HVAC with 15–20 ACH** and **RH 45–60 %**.
- **Lighting 500–750 lux**.
- **Dedicated ink/adhesive store** (fire-rated cabinet for flammable inks).
- **Re-work station** for rejected/mis-labelled units.
- **QA document desk** for online records.

---

##### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $P_{daily} = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ bottles/day}$
- $C_{line} = 150 \text{ bottles/min}$
- $T_{eff} = 7 \text{ h/shift}$
- Number of shifts = 2

**(i) Required production rate:**

$$T_{eff,day} = 2 \times 7 \times 60 = 840 \text{ min/day}$$

$$\text{Rate}_{req} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{840} = 357.14 \text{ bottles/min}$$

**(ii) Number of lines required:**

$$N = \dfrac{357.14}{150} = 2.38$$

$$\boxed{N = 3 \text{ lines (rounded up)}}$$

**(iii) Utilization factor with 3 lines:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{357.14}{3 \times 150} = \dfrac{357.14}{450} = 0.794 \text{ or } 79.4\%$$

**(iv) Comment:**

A utilization of **79.4 %** falls within the **KTU-recommended acceptable range of 70–85 %**. Therefore, installing **3 lines of 150 bottles/min capacity each** is **appropriate and operationally sound**. No corrective action needed; however, preventive maintenance scheduling should be planned during the 20.6 % idle time to maintain line availability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Students forget to round UP the number of lines (you cannot have a fraction of a line!).
> 2. **Common mistake:** Confusing effective running time (7 h) with shift duration (8 h). The difference is 1 h for changeover, cleaning, and breaks.
> 3. **Common mistake:** Not adding the **20 % spare area** in area calculations.
> 4. **Common mistake:** Forgetting to convert units (s → min, h → min) consistently.
> 5. **Common mistake:** Treating utilization > 90 % as "good" — it is actually **unsafe** because no margin remains for breakdowns.
> 6. **Examiner's Tip:** Always state the **FSSAI regulation number** when discussing labelling — it earns extra marks.
> 7. **Examiner's Tip:** Always include **line clearance SOP** as a layout provision — it is the most frequently missed yet most heavily marked topic.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Bullet Points:**

- ✅ **Packaging zone** = the last hygienic barrier; design it as a classified, climate-controlled zone with **15–20 ACH** and **500–750 lux** lighting.
- ✅ **Three packaging levels**: **Primary** (food contact), **Secondary** (carton), **Tertiary** (shipper). Each needs a separate store.
- ✅ **Line clearance SOP** is mandatory before every batch — layout must include a **clearance board** at line start. This is a **HACCP CCP**.
- ✅ **Label storage room** must be **lockable, access-controlled, vermin-proof**, with RH 45–60 %.
- ✅ **FSSAI mandatory label elements**: Product name, ingredients, nutritional info (per 100 g/mL), FSSAI logo + license no., veg/non-veg symbol, allergens, net quantity, MRP, MFD, expiry, batch/lot, manufacturer details, barcode, customer care.
- ✅ **Storage environmental control**: T < 30 °C, RH 45–60 %, away from sunlight, vermin-proof.
- ✅ **Clearances around machines**: **0.9 m** operating side, **0.6 m** non-operating side, **1.2 m** between machines for operators.
- ✅ **Floor area formula**: $A_{pkg} = (Q \times t_{pkg} \times a_{spec} \times (1+f)) / 60 \times 1.20$ (with 20 % spare).
- ✅ **Number of lines**: Always **round up**. Acceptable utilization: **70–85 %**.
- ✅ **Coding equipment**: Ink-jet or laser; must apply batch, MFD, expiry, MRP. Vision verification mandatory.
- ✅ **Re-work station** must be provided within the packaging hall for label-rejected units.
- ✅ **Compressed air** for packaging must be **oil-free, food-grade, 6–8 bar**.
- ✅ **Drainage**: Epoxy floor with **PU coving** at wall-floor junction, floor slope **1:100** towards drain.
- ✅ **Always reserve 20 % spare area** for future expansion — Lopez Principle.
- ✅ **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) racking in finished goods warehouse.
- ✅ **Dock leveler** at dispatch dock for safe truck alignment.
- ✅ **Separate ink/adhesive store** for flammable material, fire-rated cabinet, away from main hall.
- ✅ **Personnel flow**: Change room → Wash room → Air shower → Packaging hall (one-way flow only).
- ✅ **Legal Metrology Act 2009** governs MRP and net weight declaration.
- ✅ **Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020)** is the international reference for packaging hygiene.
- ✅ **Traceability time** = time from raw material receipt to finished product dispatch; KTU benchmark < 4 hours for full traceability.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste Disposal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT832 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Waste Disposal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Waste Disposal in Food Plant Layout & Design** refers to the systematic engineering methodology of collecting, handling, treating, and discharging the by-products, effluents, and residuals generated during food processing operations in a manner that complies with environmental regulations, ensures public health safety, and is integrated into the plant's spatial and process flow architecture.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, waste disposal encompasses three primary waste streams:
- **Solid Waste (SW):** Peels, seeds, trimmings, spoiled raw materials, packaging rejects, sludge.
- **Liquid Waste / Effluent (LW):** Process water, cleaning-in-place (CIP) residues, condensate, blood/water from slaughterhouses.
- **Gaseous Waste (GW):** Cooking vapors, refrigerants, dust from milling, VOC emissions from frying/roasting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of OEFTT832 expects students to correlate waste stream characterization with plant zoning (i.e., the location of Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP), Solid Waste Management (SWM) yard, and biogas plant must be on the **downwind and downstream** side of the main processing block).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a food factory as the **human digestive system**:
- **Raw materials** enter like food in the mouth.
- **Processing** is like chewing and digestion in the stomach.
- **Useful products** are the nutrients absorbed into the bloodstream (the dispatch area).
- **Waste** is everything the body cannot absorb — feces, urine, and exhaled gases.
- **Waste disposal infrastructure** (sewage lines, septic tanks, exhaust fans) is the excretory system.

Just as a human body cannot function healthily if waste accumulates internally, a food plant cannot operate sustainably if its waste streams are not designed out of the building from the layout stage itself. **Retrofitting** a treatment facility into an existing plant is **5 to 8 times costlier** than including it in the initial layout.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Benchmarks

Standard metrics used across the food industry:

| Parameter | Standard Reference Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **BOD₅ (Biochemical Oxygen Demand, 5-day)** | **< 100 mg/L** for inland discharge (India: CPCB norms) | Indicates biodegradable organic load |
| **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** | **< 250 mg/L** for surface water | Measures total oxidizable matter |
| **TSS (Total Suspended Solids)** | **< 100 mg/L** | Affects turbidity and oxygen transfer |
| **pH of treated effluent** | **6.5 – 8.5** | Neutral range for safe discharge |
| **Oil & Grease** | **< 10 mg/L** | Prevents clogging and surface films |
| **Specific waste generation** | **0.2 – 0.6 kg per kg of raw material processed** (varies by product) | Used for plant sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values above are **regulatory thresholds** (Central Pollution Control Board — CPCB, India) and are frequently asked in KTU University Examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Waste Management Hierarchy (The 5 R's)

The cornerstone of food plant waste planning. KTU examiners expect students to apply this **top-down** priority:

1. **Refuse** — Reject waste-generating practices at source (best).
2. **Reduce** — Minimize generation through process modification.
3. **Reuse** — Internal recycling (e.g., using condensate as boiler feed water).
4. **Recover / Recycle** — Convert waste into co-products (e.g., animal feed, biogas, compost).
5. **Dispose** — Final, controlled treatment and discharge (last resort).

> [!NOTE]
> The plant layout must physically support the top of the hierarchy. For instance, **segregation points** must be located at the source (next to peelers, slicers, blanchers) — *not* at a central collection yard.

### 2.2 Classification of Food Industry Waste

- **Type 1 — Process Residues (Organic, High Volume):** Fruit/vegetable pomace, dairy whey, molasses, spent grain. **High BOD** (often 1,000 – 10,000 mg/L in raw form).
- **Type 2 — Packaging Waste:** Plastics, cardboard, glass, metal. **Recyclable** but storage space is a layout concern.
- **Type 3 — Wastewater / Process Effluent:** The largest volume stream. **High BOD, high nutrients (N, P)**.
- **Type 4 — Gaseous Emissions:** Particulates from dryers, cooking odors, refrigeration leaks.
- **Type 5 — Hazardous / Special Waste:** Cleaning chemicals, laboratory reagents, used oil.

### 2.3 Treatment Train (Sequential Operations)

A typical food-plant effluent treatment follows this train, and the **layout must respect this hydraulic gradient**:

> **Screening → Grit Removal → Flow Equalization → Primary Clarification (Physical) → Secondary Treatment (Biological) → Tertiary Treatment (Polishing) → Sludge Handling → Disinfection → Discharge/Sludge Reuse**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Waste Generated} = R_m \times w_f$ | $R_m$ = Raw material input, $w_f$ = waste fraction | kg/day | Daily solid waste estimation |
| 2 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}$ | $D_1, D_2$ = DO initial/final; $B_1, B_2$ = blank DO; $f$ = dilution factor; $P$ = sample vol fraction | mg/L | Standard BOD measurement |
| 3 | $\text{COD} = \dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V}$ | $A$ = blank titre, $B$ = sample titre, $N$ = normality of FAS, $V$ = sample volume | mg/L | COD via dichromate reflux |
| 4 | $\text{BOD:COD Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{\text{COD}}$ | — | dimensionless | Indicates biodegradability (>0.5 = easily treatable) |
| 5 | $Y_{biogas} = V_s \times G_y$ | $V_s$ = volatile solids fed; $G_y$ = specific gas yield | m³/day | Anaerobic digester sizing |
| 6 | $G_y \approx 0.4\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg VS}$ (for food waste) | Standard yield for fruit/veg waste | m³/kg | Quick biogas potential |
| 7 | $V_{digester} = \dfrac{Q_{waste} \times \theta}{0.85}$ | $Q_{waste}$ = feed rate; $\theta$ = HRT (15–30 days); 0.85 = working vol factor | m³ | Anaerobic digester volume |
| 8 | $\text{F/M Ratio} = \dfrac{S_0}{X \cdot \theta}$ | $S_0$ = influent BOD; $X$ = MLSS; $\theta$ = HRT | day⁻¹ | Activated sludge design |
| 9 | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{\text{Total Daily Flow (L)}}{\text{Operating Hours (h)}}$ | Average wastewater flow | L/h | Pipe & pump sizing |
| 10 | $\eta_{removal} = \dfrac{(C_{in} - C_{out})}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | $C_{in}, C_{out}$ = inlet/outlet concentration | % | Treatment efficiency |

### 2.5 Layout Engineering Logic — "Why & How"

- **Why place the ETP at the lowest elevation?** → Effluents must flow by **gravity** to minimize pumping cost (which can be 60–70% of wastewater OPEX).
- **Why segregate waste at source?** → Mixing organic with packaging waste **contaminates** recyclables and increases treatment cost.
- **How is the ETP zone buffered?** → A **green belt of 10–50 m width** (depending on plant size) is mandated by CPCB to absorb odor and noise.
- **Why vent steam and odor away from production?** → Contamination risk and worker comfort. **Prevailing wind direction** dictates ETP placement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Real-World Utility:** Waste disposal is not a back-end afterthought. Modern food plant layouts are designed **concurrently** with utilities and effluent systems to enable **zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD)** or **circular economy** compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Daily Solid Waste Generation

**Given:**
- A fruit processing plant processes $R_m = 50{,}000\ \text{kg/day}$ of mangoes.
- The waste fraction (peel + stone + trimmings) is empirically $w_f = 0.45$.

**Step 1 — Apply the basic waste generation equation:**

$$W_{daily} = R_m \times w_f$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$W_{daily} = 50{,}000\ \text{kg/day} \times 0.45$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$W_{daily} = 22{,}500\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to tonnes for layout sizing of collection bins:**

$$W_{daily} = 22.5\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** With a bulk density of mango waste $\rho_b \approx 450\ \text{kg/m}^3$, the required bin volume per day is:

$$V_{bin} = \dfrac{22{,}500}{450} = 50\ \text{m}^3$$

> For a 2-day storage buffer (typical for transport scheduling), the layout must allocate a **100 m³ enclosed waste yard**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Biogas Yield from Anaerobic Digestion

**Given:**
- Volatile solids fed to digester: $V_s = 5{,}000\ \text{kg VS/day}$.
- Specific gas yield for fruit waste: $G_y = 0.4\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg VS}$.

**Step 1 — Apply biogas yield equation:**

$$Y_{biogas} = V_s \times G_y$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$Y_{biogas} = 5{,}000 \times 0.4$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$Y_{biogas} = 2{,}000\ \text{m}^3\text{/day}$$

**Step 4 — Methane content** (typical for food waste): 60% CH₄.

$$Q_{CH_4} = 2{,}000 \times 0.60 = 1{,}200\ \text{m}^3\text{/day}$$

**Step 5 — Energy equivalent** (calorific value of methane $\approx 35.8\ \text{MJ/m}^3$):

$$E = 1{,}200 \times 35.8 = 42{,}960\ \text{MJ/day}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kWh:**

$$E = \dfrac{42{,}960}{3.6} = 11{,}933\ \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 7 — Layout implication:** A continuous-feed digester of capacity $11.9\ \text{MWh/day}$ is required — this directly drives the **footprint** of the biogas plant in the layout.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — BOD Load and Aeration Tank Sizing

**Given:**
- Wastewater flow: $Q = 1{,}000\ \text{m}^3\text{/day}$.
- Influent BOD₅: $S_0 = 1{,}500\ \text{mg/L}$.
- Target effluent BOD₅: $S = 30\ \text{mg/L}$ (CPCB norm).
- Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS): $X = 3{,}000\ \text{mg/L}$.
- Hydraulic Retention Time: $\theta = 6\ \text{h} = 0.25\ \text{day}$.

**Step 1 — BOD loading rate:**

$$\text{BOD Load} = Q \times S_0 = 1{,}000 \times 1{,}500 = 1.5 \times 10^6\ \text{g/day} = 1{,}500\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — F/M ratio check (must be 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹ for conventional activated sludge):**

$$\text{F/M} = \dfrac{S_0}{X \cdot \theta} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{3{,}000 \times 0.25} = 2.0\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

> This is **too high** → design must be revised. Lower HRT or increase MLSS.

**Step 3 — Revised design:** Choose $\theta = 1.0\ \text{day}$:

$$\text{F/M}_{new} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{3{,}000 \times 1.0} = 0.5\ \text{day}^{-1} \quad (\text{Acceptable})$$

**Step 4 — Aeration tank volume:**

$$V_{tank} = Q \times \theta_{new} = 1{,}000 \times 1.0 = 1{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Surface area (assuming depth $h = 4.5\ \text{m}$):**

$$A_{tank} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{h} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{4.5} \approx 222.2\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Tank dimensions (square):**

$$L = W = \sqrt{222.2} \approx 14.9\ \text{m} \approx 15\ \text{m}$$

**Engineering conclusion:** The aeration tank occupies a **15 m × 15 m × 4.5 m** plot in the ETP zone, which must be shown in the plant layout.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Treatment Efficiency

**Given:** $C_{in} = 1{,}500\ \text{mg/L BOD}$, $C_{out} = 30\ \text{mg/L BOD}$.

**Step 1 — Apply efficiency formula:**

$$\eta_{removal} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\eta_{removal} = \dfrac{1{,}500 - 30}{1{,}500} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\eta_{removal} = \dfrac{1{,}470}{1{,}500} \times 100 = 98\%$$

> A properly designed secondary + tertiary train is required to achieve **>95% BOD removal**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario | Key Parameter | Equation Used | Output for Layout |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dairy plant | Whey volume | $V_{whey} = M_{milk} \times 0.09$ | Sizes whey concentration area |
| Slaughterhouse | Blood + wash water | $Q = \text{no. of animals} \times \text{per-animal effluent}$ | Sizes ETP inlet works |
| Brewery | Spent grain | $W = \text{batch size} \times 0.20$ | Sizes solid waste yard |
| Fruit juice | Pomace | $W = \text{fruit input} \times 0.40$ | Sizes dryer / biogas feed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Waste Management Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Plant Operation] --> B{Waste Generated?}
    B -- No --> Z[No Action]
    B -- Yes --> C[Segregate at Source]
    C --> D{Waste Type?}
    D -- Solid Organic --> E[Collection Bins at Source]
    D -- Liquid Effluent --> F[Floor Drains to ETP]
    D -- Packaging --> G[Recyclable Storage]
    D -- Gaseous --> H[Stack Scrubber]
    E --> I{Recoverable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Biogas / Composting / Animal Feed]
    I -- No --> K[Secure Landfill]
    F --> L[ETP: Primary Treatment]
    L --> M[ETP: Secondary Biological]
    M --> N[ETP: Tertiary Polishing]
    N --> O[Discharge to CPCB Norms]
    G --> P[Sold to Recycler]
    H --> Q[Atmospheric Release Monitored]
    J --> R[Reuse Loop to Plant]
    O --> R
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of an ETP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INLET[Inlet Works]
        A1[Bar Screen] --> A2[Grit Chamber] --> A3[Flow Equalization Tank]
    end
    subgraph PRIMARY[Primary Treatment - Physical]
        B1[Primary Clarifier] --> B2[Sludge to Digester]
    end
    subgraph SECONDARY[Secondary Treatment - Biological]
        C1[Aeration Tank] --> C2[Secondary Clarifier] --> C3[Return Activated Sludge]
        C3 --> C1
    end
    subgraph TERTIARY[Tertiary Treatment - Polishing]
        D1[Sand Filter] --> D2[Activated Carbon] --> D3[Disinfection Chlorination]
    end
    subgraph SLUDGE[Sludge Handling]
        E1[Thickener] --> E2[Digester] --> E3[Dried Sludge Cake]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C2 --> D1
    B2 --> E1
    D3 --> OUT((Treated Effluent Outlet))
    E3 --> OUT2((Biosolids for Land Application))
```

### 4.3 Spatial Zoning Logic in Plant Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLANT[Food Plant Site]
        direction TB
        NW[North-West Zone: Main Production Block]
        NE[North-East Zone: Raw Material Storage]
        SW[South-West Zone: Finished Goods Dispatch]
        SE[South-East Zone: ETP / Biogas / Solid Waste Yard]
        CE[Central Zone: Utilities Boiler, Chiller, Compressor]
    end
    WIND[Prevailing Wind Direction] -.->|Pushes odor away from production| PLANT
    GRADE[Natural Slope of Land] -.->|Gravity flow to ETP| PLANT
    BUF[Green Belt Buffer 10 to 50 m] -.->|Odor Noise Barrier| SE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout Rule of Thumb (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **ETP / Waste Yard → Downwind + Downstream + Lowest Elevation.**
> - **Raw material receipt → Upswind + Upstream + Higher Elevation.**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Effluent" in the context of a food processing plant. List any four characteristics used to characterize food industry effluents.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Effluent** is the liquid waste stream discharged from a food processing plant, typically comprising process water, cleaning residues, and soluble organic matter.

**Four characteristics:**
1. **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** — measures biodegradable organic load.
2. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** — measures total oxidizable organics.
3. **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** — measures particulate load.
4. **pH** — indicates acidity/alkalinity (food wastes are often acidic, pH 3–6).

> **Award marks:** Definition (1 mark) + 4 characteristics (0.5 each = 2 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the waste management hierarchy. Why is "Refuse" given the highest priority during plant layout design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **5 R Hierarchy** (top → bottom priority):
1. **Refuse**
2. **Reduce**
3. **Reuse**
4. **Recover / Recycle**
5. **Dispose**

**Why Refuse is highest priority:**
Refusing waste generation at the design stage eliminates the need for downstream treatment infrastructure, saving **capital cost, operational cost, and floor space** in the layout. A layout that inherently minimizes waste reduces the ETP footprint, lowers pumping costs, and improves the plant's environmental compliance rating.

> **Award marks:** Hierarchy (2 marks) + justification (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

> A fruit juice concentrate plant processes **80,000 kg/day** of mangoes. The waste fraction (peel + stone) is **45%** of raw material. The wastewater flow is **1,200 m³/day** with influent BOD₅ of **2,000 mg/L**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the daily solid waste generated and design the size of the collection yard for a **3-day** storage buffer. Assume bulk density of waste = **500 kg/m³**.
>
> **(b)** If the waste is sent to an anaerobic digester with a specific gas yield of **0.4 m³/kg VS** and **85%** of the solid waste is volatile solids, estimate the daily biogas production. If the digester HRT is **20 days**, determine the digester volume. Comment on the placement of the ETP in the plant layout.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Solid Waste Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Daily solid waste:**

$$W_{daily} = R_m \times w_f = 80{,}000 \times 0.45 = 36{,}000\ \text{kg/day} = 36\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

**Step 2 — Volume per day using bulk density:**

$$V_{day} = \dfrac{W_{daily}}{\rho_b} = \dfrac{36{,}000}{500} = 72\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — 3-day storage buffer volume:**

$$V_{buffer} = 72 \times 3 = 216\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Practical design (with 20% free-board for vehicle movement and spillage):**

$$V_{design} = 216 \times 1.20 = 259.2\ \text{m}^3 \approx 260\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Footprint (assuming 3 m stacking height):**

$$A_{yard} = \dfrac{260}{3} \approx 86.7\ \text{m}^2 \approx 90\ \text{m}^2$$

> The collection yard must be **approximately 10 m × 9 m**, paved, with a drain and wash-down facility, located **adjacent to the peeling/juicing area**.

---

**Part (b) — Biogas & Digester Sizing (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Volatile solids fed to digester:**

$$V_s = 36{,}000 \times 0.85 = 30{,}600\ \text{kg VS/day}$$

**Step 2 — Biogas yield:**

$$Y_{biogas} = V_s \times G_y = 30{,}600 \times 0.4 = 12{,}240\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Methane fraction (60%):**

$$Q_{CH_4} = 12{,}240 \times 0.60 = 7{,}344\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Energy value (35.8 MJ/m³):**

$$E = 7{,}344 \times 35.8 = 2{,}62{,}915\ \text{MJ/day} \approx 73\ \text{MWh/day}$$

**Step 5 — Digester volume (HRT = 20 days, working vol factor 0.85):**

$$V_{digester} = \dfrac{Q_{waste,vol} \times \theta}{0.85}$$

Solid waste feed volume: $30{,}600 / 1{,}000\ \text{(density of slurry approx)} = 30.6\ \text{m}^3\text{/day}$ (assuming slurry ~ 8% TS → feed slurry ~ 382.5 m³/day)

$$V_{digester} = \dfrac{382.5 \times 20}{0.85} \approx 9{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6 — ETP Placement Comment:**

The ETP and biogas plant must be located:
- **Downwind** of the production block (prevailing wind direction must be considered).
- **At the lowest elevation** to enable gravity flow of effluent (saves pumping cost).
- **At least 50 m away** from the production block, separated by a **green belt buffer**.
- **Near the boundary wall** for easy access to municipal sewer connection or sludge transport.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]**

> A dairy plant processes **2,00,000 litres/day** of milk. Wastewater generation is **2.5 litres per litre of milk processed**. The influent BOD₅ is **1{,}800 mg/L** and the target effluent BOD₅ is **< 30 mg/L** (CPCB discharge norm).
>
> **(a)** Calculate the daily wastewater flow, the BOD load, and the required treatment efficiency. Design the volume of an activated sludge aeration tank for F/M = **0.4 day⁻¹** with MLSS = **3{,}500 mg/L**.
>
> **(b)** Draw a labelled process flow diagram of the complete ETP (Primary → Secondary → Tertiary) and explain the function of each unit. State the **KTU 2024 layout placement rules** for the ETP in a food plant.

#### Model Solution (Concise)

**Part (a) — Hydraulic & Aeration Design (7 marks)**

$$Q = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2.5 = 5{,}00{,}000\ \text{L/day} = 500\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$\text{BOD Load} = 500 \times 1{,}800 = 9 \times 10^5\ \text{g/day} = 900\ \text{kg/day}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{1{,}800 - 30}{1{,}800} \times 100 = 98.33\%$$

For F/M = 0.4: $\theta = \dfrac{S_0}{X \cdot (\text{F/M})} = \dfrac{1{,}800}{3{,}500 \times 0.4} = 1.286\ \text{days}$

$$V_{tank} = Q \times \theta = 500 \times 1.286 = 643\ \text{m}^3$$

For depth 4.5 m: $A = 643 / 4.5 = 142.9\ \text{m}^2 \rightarrow L = W \approx 12\ \text{m}$.

**Part (b) — Flow Diagram & Layout (7 marks)**

Flow: Bar Screen → Grit Chamber → Equalization Tank → Primary Clarifier → Aeration Tank → Secondary Clarifier → Sand Filter → Activated Carbon → Chlorination → Discharge.

**Layout Rules:**
1. ETP at the **lowest elevation** of the site.
2. **Downwind** of production block (use meteorological data).
3. **50 m green belt** buffer for odor and noise control.
4. **Separate vehicle access** for sludge disposal.
5. Located **near boundary wall** for effluent disposal route.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up BOD and COD** — BOD measures *biodegradable* organics only; COD includes non-biodegradable. COD is always **greater than or equal to** BOD.
> 2. **Ignoring bulk density** when sizing storage — students often quote mass (kg) instead of volume (m³), losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting free-board** in tank/yard design — a 15–20% allowance is mandatory for operational access.
> 4. **Stating ETP placement without justification** — KTU examiners award marks only when *downwind + downstream + lowest elevation* is justified with logic.
> 5. **Confusing HRT with SRT** — Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) governs tank volume; Solids Retention Time (SRT) governs sludge age in biological treatment.
> 6. **Missing units in the final answer** — Always write **units** (mg/L, m³/day, kg/day) to avoid 0.5 mark deductions.
> 7. **Skipping mass balance** in biogas problems — examiners expect you to show: waste → VS → gas → energy, step by step.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly)**

- ✅ **3 Waste Streams:** Solid, Liquid, Gaseous — each requires a dedicated handling system.
- ✅ **5 R Hierarchy:** Refuse > Reduce > Reuse > Recycle > Dispose (layout must support the top).
- ✅ **Layout Golden Rule:** ETP = **Downwind + Downstream + Lowest Elevation + Green-belt buffered**.
- ✅ **CPCB Discharge Norms (Memorize):** BOD₅ < 100 mg/L; COD < 250 mg/L; TSS < 100 mg/L; pH 6.5–8.5; O&G < 10 mg/L.
- ✅ **BOD:COD Ratio:** > 0.5 ⇒ easily biodegradable; < 0.3 ⇒ needs advanced treatment.
- ✅ **Biogas Yield (Food Waste):** ~ **0.4 m³/kg VS**; Methane ~ 60%; Energy ~ 35.8 MJ/m³ CH₄.
- ✅ **Standard HRT:** Anaerobic digester = 15–30 days; Aeration tank = 4–24 h (design F/M = 0.2–0.5 day⁻¹).
- ✅ **Sludge Handling:** Primary sludge → Thickener → Digester → Drying beds → Land application.
- ✅ **Storage Buffer:** Always design for **2–3 days** of waste accumulation, with **15–20% free-board**.
- ✅ **Mass balance is mandatory** for full marks in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Always state units** in final answers.
- ✅ **Segregation at source** is a layout decision, not an operational one.
- ✅ **Prevailing wind direction** must be obtained from meteorological data during site selection.
- ✅ **ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge)** is the emerging trend — mention in long-answer questions for bonus credit.
- ✅ **Circular economy integration** (e.g., peel → pectin; whey → lactose; spent grain → cattle feed) earns appreciation marks.

---

**End of Module 3 — Waste Disposal Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — OEFTT832 Food Plant Layout and Design.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of General Food Plant Layout and Design
Plant Layout and Design of Baking Oven and Frying Plant
Chocolate and Ice-Cream Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT832 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chocolate and Ice-Cream Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Chocolate Plant — Definition

A **Chocolate Plant** is a process-engineered food manufacturing facility designed to receive cocoa beans and ancillary ingredients (sugar, milk solids, cocoa butter, emulsifier, flavour) and convert them into finished chocolate products (bars, blocks, coated centres, pralines) through the unit operations of **roasting, winnowing, grinding, refining, conching, tempering, moulding, cooling, and packaging** under tightly controlled temperature (15–18 °C) and relative humidity (50–60 %) conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition:** *Chocolate* (as per Codex Alimentarius / FSSAI) is a homogeneous product obtained by an adequate process of manufacture from cocoa nibs, cocoa mass, cocoa powder, sugar (sucrose), with or without cocoa butter, milk solids, emulsifier (e.g., lecithin ≤ 0.3–0.5 %) and permitted flavouring substances.

### 1.2 Ice-Cream Plant — Definition

An **Ice-Cream Plant** is a dairy-based frozen dessert facility where the ingredients (milk fat, MSNF, sugar, stabiliser, emulsifier, water, and optional fruits/nuts/variegates) are blended, pasteurised, homogenised, aged, **dynamically frozen** with simultaneous air incorporation (overrun), hardened, packed, and stored at –25 °C to –30 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key FSSAI Definition:** *Ice cream* is a frozen dairy dessert obtained from milk, cream, sugar, stabiliser and/or emulsifier, frozen while being agitated to incorporate air, and contains **not less than 10 % milk fat, 3.5 % MSNF, and 36 % total solids**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Chocolate — A Bakery Oven Analogy:** Think of making chocolate like preparing a high-quality *cake batter* — but with cocoa nibs replacing the eggs/flour, refined in a *5-roller mill* (like rolling dough), and matured in a *conche* (like slow-cooking a curry to deepen flavour). The final "tempering" step is similar to **laminating puff pastry** — controlled folding of fat (cocoa butter crystals) to obtain layers of stability and sheen.

**Ice-Cream — A Whipped Cream Analogy:** Ice cream is essentially *frozen whipped cream* with stabilisers acting as the *scaffolding* and emulsifiers as the *welding* between fat and water. Without overrun (air incorporation), the product is dense like a frozen pudding; with controlled overrun (50–100 %), it becomes the *light, scoopable* dessert we know.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Layout Drivers:**
> - **Chocolate plant** → dust zone (cocoa dust is explosive at 50 g/m³), temperature-controlled wrapping halls, low humidity.
> - **Ice-cream plant** → cold zones (–30 °C), CIP-friendly sanitary design, segregation of raw/finished zones.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chocolate Plant — Unit Operation Breakdown

**A. Bean Reception & Storage**
- Beans arrive in jute/sisal bags or bulk containers. Storage in well-ventilated warehouses at **20–30 °C**, RH < 70 %, away from direct sunlight.
- Moisture content must be brought down to 6–8 % to prevent mould growth.

**B. Cleaning & Sorting**
- Vibratory screens remove stones, sticks, broken beans, and flat beans.
- Magnetic separators (rare-earth NdFeB) remove ferrous contamination.
- Destoners use air aspiration (differential density).

**C. Roasting (100–150 °C / 15–45 min)**
- Develops *Maillard reaction* products and pyrazines (chocolate aroma).
- Reduces moisture (8 % → 2 %).
- **Why:** Critical for flavour precursor development; over-roasting yields bitter, acrid notes.

**D. Cracking & Winnowing**
- Mechanical cracking → husk (shell) and nibs separation by air stream.
- **Husk utilisation:** Fuel for boiler, mulch, or furfural extraction.

**E. Grinding (Melangeur / Bead Mill / Ball Mill)**
- Nibs (cell diameter 25–30 μm) ground → heat from friction melts **cocoa butter** → **chocolate liquor** (or *cocoa mass*) with 50–58 % fat.
- Particle size reduced to < 30 μm (perceptible grittiness threshold = 25 μm).

**F. Mixing (Kneading)**
- Recipe blend:
  - Dark chocolate: cocoa liquor + sugar + cocoa butter + lecithin.
  - Milk chocolate: cocoa liquor + sugar + milk powder + cocoa butter + lecithin.
- Standard mix is loaded into a *kneader* at 40–50 °C.

**G. Refining (5-Roll Refiner)**
- Sequential 5-roll refiner with rolls rotating at increasing speeds.
- Reduces sugar / milk particle size to **18–22 μm** (sensory threshold ≈ 25 μm).
- Output moisture ≈ 0.8–1.2 %.

**H. Conching (50–80 °C / 12–72 h)**
- Three effects:
  1. **Moisture reduction** to < 1 %.
  2. **Volatile acids** (acetic, butyric) and undesirable volatiles removed.
  3. **Coating of particles** with cocoa butter film (smoothness).
- Modern *Lödige* and *Buhler* conches use high shear.

**I. Tempering (Pre-crystallisation)**
- Cocoa butter is **polymorphic** with six crystal forms (I–VI). Only **Form V (β)** is stable and gives gloss, snap, and bloom-resistance.
- **Temper cycle:**
  - Melt completely at 45–50 °C (destroy all crystal nuclei).
  - Cool to 27–28 °C (Form III and IV form).
  - Re-warm to 30–32 °C (melt Forms III/IV, retain only Form V seeds).
  - Pour into mould.

**J. Moulding, Cooling Tunnel, Demoulding, Wrapping, Storage**
- Moulds cooled in tunnel at 7–12 °C for 20–40 min.
- Stored at 15–18 °C, 50–60 % RH.

### 2.2 Ice-Cream Plant — Unit Operation Breakdown

**A. Ingredient Reception & Storage**
- Liquid milk, cream in insulated SS tanks (4 °C).
- Sugar, milk powder, stabiliser, emulsifier in silos / drums.

**B. Mixing / Blending**
- Standard Ice-cream Mix Composition:

| Component | % by Weight |
|---|---|
| Milk Fat | 10 – 16 |
| MSNF | 9 – 12 |
| Sugar (Sucrose + Glucose) | 12 – 16 |
| Stabiliser + Emulsifier | 0.2 – 0.5 |
| Water (cumulative) | Balance (≈ 55 – 62) |

- All dry ingredients reconstituted in water at 40–50 °C.

**C. Pasteurisation (HTST: 80 °C / 25 s)**
- Kills pathogens (*Salmonella*, *Listeria*); inactivates enzymes.
- Optional *vat pasteurisation* (68 °C / 30 min) for premium artisan lines.

**D. Homogenisation (140 – 200 bar at 65–70 °C)**
- Two-stage: 1st stage 175 bar, 2nd stage 35 bar.
- Reduces fat globule to < 2 μm → prevents creaming and improves whipping.

**E. Ageing / Maturation (4–5 °C / 4–24 h)**
- **Functions:**
  - Hydration of stabilisers (viscosity build-up).
  - Partial crystallisation of milk fat (improved whipping).
  - Protein–water binding.

**F. Dynamic Freezing (with Air Incorporation)**
- **Batch freezer:** small capacity, harder, denser ice cream (overrun 50–80 %).
- **Continuous freezer (e.g., Hoyer, Tetra Pak):** –5 to –6 °C outlet, overrun 60–120 %.
- Ice crystals < 50 μm → smooth texture; large crystals → coarse, icy mouthfeel.

**G. Inclusion Addition & Variegating**
- Fruits, nuts, chocolate chips, sauces added via *inclusion feeder* / *variegate pump* before extrusion.

**H. Packaging & Hardening**
- Cup filling, cone making, stick-bar moulding, family-pack extrusion.
- **Hardening tunnel** at –35 to –40 °C, air velocity 3–5 m/s, until core reaches –18 °C (typically 2–4 h).

**I. Cold Storage & Distribution**
- Hardening room at –25 to –30 °C.
- Distribution cold chain maintained at **–18 °C** or below.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all formulas below to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Overrun (\%)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{mix}} - W_{\text{frozen}}}{W_{\text{frozen}}} \times 100$ | Calculates % air incorporated into ice cream. Critical for yield, scoopability, and cost. |
| 2 | $\text{Total Solids (\%)} = F + MSNF + S + E$ where F = fat, MSNF = milk-solids-not-fat, S = sugar, E = stabiliser/emulsifier | Mass balance of ice-cream mix. Used in product development. |
| 3 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \; \; [\text{kJ}]$ | Heat load calculation in chocolate tempering & ice-cream hardening. |
| 4 | $Q = m \cdot L \; \; [\text{kJ}]$ | Latent heat removal during ice cream freezing & hardening. |
| 5 | $R_{\text{ms}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{actual}}}{R_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Refrigeration mass balance; checks plant refrigeration capacity. |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}}{T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{cold}}}$ | Thermodynamic efficiency in CIP & refrigeration systems. |
| 7 | $E_{\text{specific}} = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m_{\text{refined}}} \; \; [\text{kWh/tonne}]$ | Specific energy of chocolate refining; design criterion for 5-roll refiner motor. |
| 8 | $v = \dfrac{V_{\text{ice}}}{V_{\text{mix}}} = 1 + \dfrac{\text{Overrun}}{100}$ | Volume increase in ice cream due to air. Used in cup-fill calculation. |
| 9 | $\rho_{\text{ice-cream}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{mix}}}{1 + \text{OV}}$ | Apparent density of aerated ice cream for hopper sizing. |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{CIP}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}})$ | CIP heating/cooling duty; mandatory for plant utility design. |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Plant Design
- **Overrun** dictates *cup-fill volume*, *pack-size economics*, and *refrigeration load* of hardening room.
- **Tempering cycle** dictates the *cooling-tunnel capacity* and the *chilled-water/cooling-coil* design of the chocolate moulding hall.
- **Specific refining energy** dictates the *transformer and cabling* sizing of the chocolate mill.
- **CIP heat balance** dictates the *steam-boiler capacity* and *return-pump sizing* of both plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Overrun Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A chocolate ice-cream manufacturer prepares a mix of mass $W_{\text{mix}}$. After dynamic freezing and air incorporation, the mass of the frozen aerated product is $W_{\text{frozen}}$. Derive the percentage overrun expression.

**Step 1 — Define overrun.**
Overrun is the percentage increase in volume of the ice cream relative to the original mix, expressed in terms of mass because the mass of air incorporated is negligible (air density 1.2 kg/m³ vs. mix density 1100 kg/m³).

$$\text{Overrun} = \frac{V_{\text{frozen}} - V_{\text{mix}}}{V_{\text{mix}}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume to mass using density.**

$$\text{Overrun} = \frac{\dfrac{W_{\text{frozen}}}{\rho_{\text{frozen}}} - \dfrac{W_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{mix}}}}{\dfrac{W_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{mix}}}}$$

**Step 3 — Since air is negligible in mass balance, $W_{\text{mix}} = W_{\text{frozen}}$ (approximately). Let common mass be $W$.**

$$\text{Overrun} = \frac{\dfrac{W}{\rho_{\text{frozen}}} - \dfrac{W}{\rho_{\text{mix}}}}{\dfrac{W}{\rho_{\text{mix}}}} = \left( \frac{\rho_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{frozen}}} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Replace density ratio by mass ratio (mass conserved).**

$$\frac{\rho_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{frozen}}} = \frac{W_{\text{mix}}}{W_{\text{frozen}}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and convert to percentage.**

$$\text{Overrun (\%)} = \left( \frac{W_{\text{mix}}}{W_{\text{frozen}}} - 1 \right) \times 100 = \frac{W_{\text{mix}} - W_{\text{frozen}}}{W_{\text{frozen}}} \times 100$$

> **Logic Summary:** We have expressed overrun in terms of measurable *masses* instead of densities, because air mass is negligible and a balance is easier on the shop floor. The expression derived is the standard industrial form used in ice-cream plant mass-balance calculations.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Overrun Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Mix mass $W_{\text{mix}} = 1000 \; \text{kg}$. After continuous freezing, the aerated ice cream mass is $W_{\text{frozen}} = 625 \; \text{kg}$. (Note: mass of air is assumed zero; the apparent loss in mass is due to higher volume at lower density.)

**Step 1 — Apply derived formula.**

$$\text{Overrun (\%)} = \frac{W_{\text{mix}} - W_{\text{frozen}}}{W_{\text{frozen}}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\text{Overrun (\%)} = \frac{1000 - 625}{625} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator.**

$$1000 - 625 = 375$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate division and percentage.**

$$\frac{375}{625} \times 100 = 0.6 \times 100 = 60 \;\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The mix has been aerated to an overrun of **60 %**, which is typical for a standard *premium* ice-cream line. Higher overrun (e.g., 80–100 %) is used in *economy* products, while lower overrun (30–50 %) is preferred in *gelato-style* dense ice cream.

### 3.3 Derivation of Refining Specific Energy

**Step 1 — Define total electrical energy consumed by refiner.**

$$E_{\text{total}} = P \cdot t \; \; [\text{kWh}]$$

where $P$ = power draw in kW, $t$ = time in hours.

**Step 2 — Define mass throughput of refiner.**

$$m_{\text{refined}} \; \; [\text{tonnes}]$$

**Step 3 — Specific energy is energy per unit mass of product.**

$$E_{\text{specific}} = \frac{P \cdot t}{m_{\text{refined}}} \; \; [\text{kWh/tonne}]$$

**Step 4 — Industrial benchmark values.**

- 5-roll refiner: 25–45 kWh/tonne for chocolate.
- Bead mill (alternative): 15–25 kWh/tonne (energy-efficient).

**Step 5 — Motor sizing.**

$$P_{\text{motor}} = E_{\text{specific}} \times \dot{m}_{\text{plant}}$$

If plant capacity = 2 tonnes/h and $E_{\text{specific}}$ = 35 kWh/tonne, then:

$$P_{\text{motor}} = 35 \times 2 = 70 \; \text{kW}$$

Adding 20 % safety factor:

$$P_{\text{motor, design}} = 70 \times 1.2 = 84 \; \text{kW}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Process Criticalities

| Parameter | Chocolate Plant | Ice-Cream Plant |
|---|---|---|
| Critical temperature | 15 – 18 °C (moulding hall) | – 30 °C (hardening) |
| Critical humidity | 50 – 60 % RH | < 80 % RH (cold dry air) |
| Energy-intensive operation | Conching (12 – 72 h, 50–80 °C) | Hardening & cold storage |
| Hygienic zone class | High (FSMA / GFSI) | Very high (dairy + cold) |
| Fire / explosion risk | Cocoa dust (50 g/m³ MEC) | Refrigerant (ammonia) |
| CIP frequency | Daily (milk-chocolate line) | Every 4–6 h (dairy) |
| Material flow driver | Conche capacity | Freezer capacity |
| Bottleneck unit op. | Conching | Continuous freezer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Chocolate Plant — Block-Level Process Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bean Reception and Weighing] --> B[Cleaning and Sorting]
    B --> C[Roasting 100 to 150 C]
    C --> D[Cracking]
    D --> E[Winnowing]
    E --> F[Nib Storage and Blending]
    F --> G[Grinding to Chocolate Liquor]
    G --> H[Mixing with Sugar and Milk Powder]
    H --> I[5 Roll Refining]
    I --> J[Conching 12 to 72 h]
    J --> K[Standardisation and Lecithin Addition]
    K --> L[Tempering 30 to 32 C]
    L --> M[Moulding]
    M --> N[Cooling Tunnel 7 to 12 C]
    N --> O[Demoulding]
    O --> P[Wrapping 15 to 18 C 50 pct RH]
    P --> Q[Finished Goods Storage]
    Q --> R[Dispatch Cold Store 15 to 18 C]
```

> **Engineering Reading:** Each block is a unit operation that requires its own **HVAC zoning**, **CIP loop**, and **electrical panel**. Conching and refining are the **bottleneck operations**; the entire plant throughput (tonnes/day) is governed by the cumulative capacity of conches.

### 4.2 Ice-Cream Plant — Block-Level Process Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Milk and Cream Reception] --> B1[Storage Silos 4 C]
    B1 --> C1[Mixing and Blending 40 to 50 C]
    C1 --> D1[HTST Pasteurisation 80 C 25 s]
    D1 --> E1[Homogenisation 175 bar]
    E1 --> F1[Cooling to 4 C]
    F1 --> G1[Ageing Tank 4 to 5 C 4 to 24 h]
    G1 --> H1[Dynamic Freezing minus 5 to minus 6 C]
    H1 --> I1[Inclusions and Variegate Addition]
    I1 --> J1[Packaging Cup Cone Stick]
    J1 --> K1[Hardening Tunnel minus 35 to minus 40 C]
    K1 --> L1[Finished Cold Store minus 25 C]
    L1 --> M1[Dispatch minus 18 C Cold Chain]
```

> **Engineering Reading:** Notice the *serial temperature gradient* (40 °C → 80 °C → 4 °C → –6 °C → –40 °C → –25 °C). The plant layout must ensure **unidirectional material flow** with no backflow, and the **refrigeration load** peaks at the hardening tunnel.

### 4.3 Plant Layout Zoning — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Zone 1 Raw Material Godown] --> Z2[Zone 2 Process Hall]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3 Moulding and Cooling]
    Z3 --> Z4[Zone 4 Wrapping 15 to 18 C]
    Z4 --> Z5[Zone 5 Finished Store]
    Z6[Zone 6 Cold Store minus 25 C] --> Z5
    Z7[Zone 7 CIP and Utilities] --> Z2
    Z8[Zone 8 Effluent Treatment] --> Z2
```

> **Layout Principle:** *Personnel flow* and *material flow* must **never cross** to prevent cross-contamination. The *CIP / utility zone* and the *ETP* should be located **downstream** of the prevailing wind and the gradient of waste-water flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define *overrun* in ice cream. List any two factors that affect overrun.**

*Model Answer:*
Overrun is the percentage increase in volume of the ice cream obtained from a given volume of mix, resulting from the incorporation of air during the freezing process.

$$\text{Overrun (\%)} = \frac{W_{\text{mix}} - W_{\text{frozen}}}{W_{\text{frozen}}} \times 100$$

**Factors affecting overrun:**
1. Type of freezer (batch vs. continuous) and dasher speed.
2. Fat content of the mix and presence of emulsifier (MSNF helps stabilise air cells).
3. *Other factors:* ageing time, mix viscosity, draw temperature, and incorporation of stabilisers.

*(3 marks — full credit expected for definition + 2 factors.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is *conching*? Why is it considered a critical step in chocolate manufacture?**

*Model Answer:*
Conching is a long-duration kneading and agitating process carried out in a *conche* machine at 50–80 °C for 12 to 72 hours, after refining and before tempering.

**Importance:**
1. **Moisture removal** to < 1 %, preventing microbial spoilage and improving shelf life.
2. **Removal of volatile acids** (acetic, butyric) and off-flavours → smoother, more rounded chocolate flavour.
3. **Coating of solid particles** with a thin film of cocoa butter, giving the final *smooth mouthfeel* and uniform gloss.

*(3 marks — definition + any two significances.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU ESE 2024 Scheme):** Answer **one** of the following. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the manufacturing process of milk chocolate with a neat process flow diagram. Highlight the role of each unit operation.**

*Model Answer:*

**1. Bean Reception & Cleaning (1 mark):** Cocoa beans received in jute bags; cleaned of foreign matter, stones, and broken beans using vibrating screens and magnetic separators. Moisture brought to 6–8 %.

**2. Roasting (1 mark):** 100–150 °C for 15–45 min. Maillard reaction develops *chocolate aroma precursors* (pyrazines, aldehydes); moisture reduced to ≈ 2 %.

**3. Cracking & Winnowing (1 mark):** Beans cracked between rolls; air stream separates lighter husks from heavier nibs.

**4. Grinding (1 mark):** Nibs ground in melangeur/ball mill. Frictional heat melts cocoa butter → *chocolate liquor* (≈ 50–58 % fat). Particle size < 30 μm.

**5. Mixing / Kneading (1 mark):** Recipe mixing — chocolate liquor + sugar + full-cream milk powder + cocoa butter. Typical milk-chocolate recipe: 11 % liquor, 25 % cocoa butter + milk fat, 45 % sugar, 19 % whole-milk powder, 0.4 % lecithin.

**6. Refining (1 mark):** 5-roll refiner reduces sugar + milk particle size to 18–22 μm (sensory threshold ≈ 25 μm).

**7. Conching (1 mark):** 12–72 h at 50–80 °C. Moisture < 1 %, volatile acids removed, particles coated with cocoa butter.

*Total for (a): 7 marks. Diagram (flowchart with 9–10 blocks) carries additional clarity marks.*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of *tempering* in chocolate manufacture. List the polymorphic forms of cocoa butter and explain why Form V is preferred.**

*Model Answer:*

**Definition (2 marks):** Tempering is a controlled pre-crystallisation step in which molten chocolate is cooled, agitated, and re-warmed to preferentially form the **stable Form V (β-crystals)** of cocoa butter, while eliminating unstable Forms I, II, III, IV, and VI.

**Process (2 marks):**
- Heat chocolate to 45–50 °C (destroy all crystal nuclei).
- Cool to 27–28 °C (Forms III and IV form).
- Re-warm to 30–32 °C (Forms III, IV melt; only Form V seeds remain).
- Pour into mould.

**Polymorphic Forms (2 marks):**
| Form | Crystal Type | Stability | Melting Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | γ (gamma) | Unstable | 17 °C |
| II | α (alpha) | Unstable | 22 °C |
| III | β' (beta-prime) | Unstable | 26 °C |
| IV | β' (beta-prime) | Unstable | 28 °C |
| V | β (beta) | **Stable** | 34 °C |
| VI | β (beta) | Stable but forms over months | 36 °C |

**Why Form V is preferred (1 mark):** Gives **glossy surface, sharp snap, fast release from moulds, and resistance to fat bloom** during storage.

*Total: 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the manufacturing process of ice cream with a process flow diagram. State the standard composition of an ice-cream mix.**

*Model Answer:*

**Standard Mix Composition (2 marks):**

| Component | % by Weight |
|---|---|
| Milk Fat | 10 – 16 |
| MSNF (Milk Solids Not Fat) | 9 – 12 |
| Sugar (Sucrose + Glucose Syrup) | 12 – 16 |
| Stabiliser + Emulsifier | 0.2 – 0.5 |
| Water (total) | Balance (≈ 55 – 62) |

**Unit Operations (5 marks):**

1. **Reception & Storage** — milk/cream in insulated SS tanks at 4 °C.
2. **Mixing / Blending** — 40–50 °C, dry ingredients reconstituted.
3. **Pasteurisation** — HTST 80 °C / 25 s.
4. **Homogenisation** — 175 bar, two-stage.
5. **Ageing** — 4–5 °C / 4–24 h (stabiliser hydration, fat crystallisation).
6. **Dynamic Freezing** — –5 to –6 °C outlet, overrun 50–100 %.
7. **Inclusion Addition** — fruits, nuts, variegate, colour, flavour.
8. **Packaging** — cups, cones, sticks, family packs.
9. **Hardening** — –35 to –40 °C tunnel; core to –18 °C.
10. **Storage & Dispatch** — –25 °C cold store → –18 °C cold chain.

*Total: 7 marks.*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Define *overrun* in ice cream. A mix of 1200 kg produces 800 kg of frozen ice cream. Calculate the overrun and discuss factors affecting it.**

*Model Answer:*

**Definition (1 mark):** Overrun is the percentage increase in volume of the ice cream compared to the mix, due to air incorporation during freezing.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\text{Overrun (\%)} = \frac{W_{\text{mix}} - W_{\text{frozen}}}{W_{\text{frozen}}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Overrun (\%)} = \frac{1200 - 800}{800} \times 100 = \frac{400}{800} \times 100 = 50 \;\%$$

> The overrun of 50 % is typical of a *premium* category.

**Factors Affecting Overrun (3 marks):**

1. **Type of freezer:** Continuous freezers give more uniform, higher overrun (60–100 %) compared to batch freezers (50–80 %).
2. **Mix composition:** Higher fat, higher MSNF, presence of emulsifier (lecithin/mono-/diglyceride) → higher overrun.
3. **Stabilisers:** Alginate, guar gum, carrageenan, LBG — improve air-cell stability and reduce collapse.
4. **Ageing time:** Longer ageing (12–24 h) → better fat crystallisation → higher overrun.
5. **Dasher speed and barrel pressure** of the freezer.
6. **Draw temperature** of the freezer outlet.

*Total: 7 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing *conching* with *refining*.** Refining is *size reduction* of sugar/milk; conching is *flavour and texture* development. Marks deducted if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the *mass balance* in overrun problems.** Always write the formula before substituting values; many students incorrectly invert the numerator/denominator.
> 3. **Listing only 2 polymorphic forms of cocoa butter.** All **six** forms (I–VI) must be stated, and **Form V** must be justified with at least 2 reasons (gloss + bloom resistance).
> 4. **Omitting *HTST parameters* (80 °C / 25 s).** Generic statements like "pasteurise the mix" fetch 0 marks; quantitative data is mandatory.
> 5. **Ignoring the *cold chain* —18 °C in ice cream.** Always mention hardening → cold storage → distribution as a connected chain.
> 6. **Missing the *HVAC* specification** in chocolate plant layout questions. Always state **15–18 °C and 50–60 % RH** for the moulding/wrapping zone.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4**

**Chocolate Plant — Key Points:**
- Cocoa bean → **Roasting (100–150 °C, 15–45 min)** → **Winnowing** → **Nibs → Grinding (chocolate liquor)** → **Mixing** → **5-Roll Refining (18–22 μm)** → **Conching (12–72 h, 50–80 °C)** → **Tempering (Form V crystals)** → **Moulding → Cooling (7–12 °C)** → **Wrapping (15–18 °C, 50–60 % RH)**.
- **Polymorphic forms of cocoa butter:** I, II, III, IV, V, VI → **Form V (β)** is the only desirable form (gloss, snap, bloom-resistance).
- **Moulding hall condition:** 15–18 °C, 50–60 % RH (to prevent *fat bloom* and *sugar bloom*).
- **Cocoa dust MEC:** 50 g/m³ → explosion-proof electrical fittings mandatory in cleaning/winnowing zone.
- **Refining specific energy:** 25–45 kWh/tonne (design benchmark).

**Ice-Cream Plant — Key Points:**
- **Mix composition:** 10–16 % Fat, 9–12 % MSNF, 12–16 % Sugar, 0.2–0.5 % Stab/Emul, balance Water.
- **HTST Pasteurisation:** 80 °C / 25 s.
- **Homogenisation:** Two-stage, 175 bar first stage, 35 bar second stage.
- **Ageing:** 4–5 °C / 4–24 h.
- **Freezing:** –5 to –6 °C outlet; ice crystal < 50 μm.
- **Overrun:** 50 % (premium), 80–100 % (economy), 30–50 % (gelato).
- **Hardening:** –35 to –40 °C, until core reaches –18 °C.
- **Storage & Distribution:** –25 °C plant store → –18 °C cold chain.
- **Stabilisers:** LBG, guar, carrageenan, alginate, gelatin; **Emulsifiers:** mono-/diglycerides, lecithin, polysorbate 80.

**Layout / Engineering — Key Points:**
- Unidirectional material flow (raw → process → packing → store).
- CIP loops separate for raw and pasteurised sections.
- Cold storage sizing based on **Q = m·L** latent heat + **m·c_p·ΔT** sensible heat.
- ETP sized at 3–4 m³/kL of mix processed (typical ice-cream).
- Personnel flow **never** crosses material flow → separate corridors.

**KTU High-Yield Numerical Hotspots:**
1. Overrun calculation (most frequent — 3 to 7 marks).
2. Mix mass-balance and total-solids verification.
3. Refining specific-energy → motor sizing.
4. Refrigeration load for hardening tunnel (latent + sensible heat).
5. Cup-fill volume = $\dfrac{m_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{ice-cream}}}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fruits and Vegetables Processing Industries including Beverages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT832 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fruits and Vegetables Processing Industries including Beverages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fruits and Vegetables Processing Industries

### Formal Definition
The **fruits and vegetables processing industry** encompasses a sector of food engineering that transforms perishable horticultural raw materials into stable, shelf-life-extended, value-added products through unit operations such as cleaning, sorting, peeling, slicing, blanching, pasteurization, sterilization, freezing, dehydration, fermentation, and aseptic packaging. As per the **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** and **Codex Alimentarius** guidelines, these industries are classified under **RTE (Ready-To-Eat)**, **RTH (Ready-To-Heat)**, and **RTC (Ready-To-Cook)** categories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The module integrates **process flow analysis (PFD)**, **sanitary plant design**, and **HACCP-compliant layout planning** for high-volume, seasonal-raw-material-dependent industries.

### Sub-Sectors Covered
- **Fruit-based**: Juices, concentrates, jams, jellies, marmalades, canned fruits, dried fruits, fruit pulp, IQF (Individual Quick Frozen) fruits, fruit wines, cider.
- **Vegetable-based**: Canned vegetables, frozen vegetables, pickles, sauces, ketchup, purees, dehydrated vegetables, vegetable powders, frozen ready-meals.
- **Beverages**: Soft drinks (carbonated & non-carbonated), bottled water, fruit beverages, ready-to-serve (RTS) drinks, nectars, squashes, cordials, tea processing, coffee processing, brewing & distilling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology**
> - **Pulp**: Mechanically extracted, sieved, fruit flesh without juice separation.
> - **Nectar**: Pulp + sugar + water + acid (min 20% pulp by FSSAI).
> - **RTS (Ready-to-Serve)**: Pre-diluted beverage, ready for direct consumption (min 10% fruit juice).
> - **Concentrate**: Juice reduced to ~65–72 °Brix (degrees Brix = % sucrose by weight).

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen-to-Factory" Intuition

Think of a fruit processing plant as a **giant industrial-scale modular kitchen**.
